topics.title: Engineering application of optimization: Statement of an optimization problem, classification, review of basic calculus concepts, stationary points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT413 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engineering application of optimization: Statement of an optimization problem, classification, review of basic calculus concepts, stationary points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Engineering Optimization — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Optimization** is the branch of applied mathematics and engineering science that deals with finding the **best (most favorable)** value of an objective (performance criterion) from a set of available alternatives, subject to constraints imposed by physical, economic, or technological limitations.

In the formal mathematical sense, optimization is the process of selecting the values of **decision variables** from an admissible domain such that an **objective function** attains its **maximum** or **minimum** value, while all **equality and inequality constraints** are simultaneously satisfied.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are traveling from **Thiruvananthapuram** to **Kozhikode** in Kerala. You have multiple route options:
- The shortest route (minimize **distance**)
- The fastest route (minimize **time**)
- The cheapest route (minimize **fuel cost**)
- The most scenic route (maximize **satisfaction**)

Each of these represents an **optimization problem**. You pick the *best* route based on what you want to optimize (the **objective**) while obeying practical limits (e.g., the road must exist, your fuel tank capacity, toll constraints — these are **constraints**). Engineering optimization does the same thing, but with mathematical rigor and often hundreds or thousands of variables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples:**
> - Designing a **structural beam** with minimum weight that can carry a given load
> - Finding the **optimal shape** of an aircraft wing for minimum drag
> - Determining the **minimum cost** production schedule in a manufacturing plant
> - Tuning a **PID controller** for fastest settling time

## 1.3 The Standard Statement of an Optimization Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Mathematical Statement:**
>
> **Find** $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ that **minimizes** (or maximizes) $f(X)$
> **Subject to:**
> $g_j(X) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ (inequality constraints)
> $h_k(X) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$ (equality constraints)
> $X \in S$ where $S$ is the **feasible region**.

Where:
- $X$ is the **vector of design variables** ($n$-dimensional)
- $f(X)$ is the **objective function** (scalar)
- $g_j(X)$ and $h_k(X)$ are the **constraint functions**
- $S$ is the **search / feasible space**
- **Bold constants and standard metrics:** The symbol $n$ (number of variables), $m$ (number of inequality constraints), and $p$ (number of equality constraints) are all **non-negative integers**.

## 1.4 Why Optimization is Central to Engineering

Every engineering design decision involves trade-offs. Optimization provides the **systematic, mathematical framework** to resolve these trade-offs in an optimal manner rather than relying on intuition or trial-and-error. It is the **backbone** of modern CAD/CAE, operations research, and data-driven engineering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an Optimization Problem (The Three Pillars)

An optimization problem has **three essential ingredients**:

### 2.1.1 Objective Function $f(X)$
- A scalar-valued function that quantifies the *goodness* of a design.
- We either **minimize** it (e.g., cost, weight, error) or **maximize** it (e.g., profit, efficiency, reliability).
- **Maximization can be converted to minimization** using:
$$f_{\min}(X) = -f_{\max}(X)$$

### 2.1.2 Design Variables $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$
- The **independent parameters** that the designer can control.
- Each variable can be:
  - **Continuous** (real numbers, e.g., length, diameter)
  - **Discrete** (integers, e.g., number of bolts)
  - **Mixed** (a combination of both)

### 2.1.3 Constraints
- **Inequality constraints** $g_j(X) \leq 0$ define a region of admissible designs.
- **Equality constraints** $h_k(X) = 0$ typically arise from physical laws (e.g., equilibrium equations).
- A problem with **no constraints** is called an **unconstrained optimization problem**.
- A problem with **at least one constraint** is called a **constrained optimization problem**.

## 2.2 Classification of Optimization Problems

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask 3-mark questions on classification. Memorize the tree below.

### 2.2.1 Based on the Nature of the Objective Function
| **Type** | **Objective Function Form** | **Solution Method** |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | $f(X)$ and all constraints are **linear** | Simplex Method, Graphical |
| **Non-Linear Programming (NLP)** | At least one function is **non-linear** | KKT conditions, Gradient methods |
| **Quadratic Programming (QP)** | $f(X)$ is **quadratic**, constraints linear | Modified Simplex, Active Set |
| **Geometric Programming (GP)** | $f(X)$ is a **posynomial** | Dual transformation |
| **Integer Programming (IP)** | Variables restricted to **integers** | Branch and Bound, Cutting Planes |

### 2.2.2 Based on the Nature of Design Variables
- **Static Optimization** — variables are time-invariant (steady-state design)
- **Dynamic Optimization** — variables vary with time (optimal control)

### 2.2.3 Based on the Number of Objectives
- **Single-Objective Optimization** — one objective function
- **Multi-Objective Optimization** — multiple conflicting objectives (leads to **Pareto optimality**)

### 2.2.4 Based on the Nature of the Feasible Region
- **Unconstrained** — no restrictions on $X$
- **Constrained** — feasible region $S$ is a proper subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$

### 2.2.5 Based on Determinism
- **Deterministic Optimization** — all data known with certainty
- **Stochastic Optimization** — parameters are random variables

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|
| General Problem | $\min f(X)$ s.t. $g_j(X) \leq 0, h_k(X)=0$ | Standard form |
| Max-to-Min Conversion | $\max f(X) \equiv \min [-f(X)]$ | Multiply by $-1$ |
| Gradient (row vector) | $\nabla f(X) = \left[\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right]$ | First derivative |
| Hessian Matrix | $H(X) = \left[\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}\right]$ | $n \times n$ symmetric |
| Stationary Point | $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ | First-order necessary condition |
| Unconstrained Min (S.O.C.) | $\nabla^2 f(X^*) > 0$ (positive definite) | $n=1$: $f'' > 0$ |
| Unconstrained Max (S.O.C.) | $\nabla^2 f(X^*) < 0$ (negative definite) | $n=1$: $f'' < 0$ |
| Constrained Optimum (KKT) | $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j + \sum \mu_k \nabla h_k = 0$ | KKT First Order |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All symbols in the table are **dimensionless abstract** unless tied to a physical problem. For example, in beam design, $X$ may have units of **meters (m)**, $f$ may have units of **Newtons (N)**, and weight minimization gives an answer in **kg**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification guides the **choice of solver**. A linear program in 10,000 variables can be solved in milliseconds; a non-convex NLP may need evolutionary algorithms. Choosing the right classification is the **first critical step** in any optimization study — it determines feasibility, computational cost, and the type of software (MATLAB, Python `scipy`, GAMS, LINGO) required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Review of Basic Calculus Concepts for Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> This section is foundational. KTU frequently tests these derivations in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 3.1.1 The Gradient Vector (First Derivative)

For a scalar function $f(X)$ where $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the **gradient** is the vector of all first partial derivatives:

$$
\nabla f(X) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\[2pt] \vdots \\[2pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Why is the gradient important?**
- $\nabla f(X)$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** of $f$.
- $-\nabla f(X)$ points in the direction of **steepest descent** (used in gradient-based minimizers).
- At a **stationary point**, $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ — the function is locally flat.

### 3.1.2 The Hessian Matrix (Second Derivative)

The Hessian $H(X)$ (also written $\nabla^2 f(X)$) is the square matrix of second partial derivatives:

$$
H(X) = \nabla^2 f(X) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\[4pt] \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Properties of the Hessian:**
- $H(X)$ is **symmetric** whenever the mixed partials are continuous (Clairaut's Theorem): $\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_j \partial x_i}$
- The **definiteness** of $H$ at a stationary point determines whether the point is a minimum, maximum, or saddle point.

### 3.1.3 Taylor Series Expansion (Foundation for Stationarity)

For a twice continuously differentiable function, we can write the Taylor series around a point $X^*$:

$$
f(X) = f(X^*) + \nabla f(X^*)^T (X - X^*) + \dfrac{1}{2} (X - X^*)^T H(X^*) (X - X^*) + \text{higher order terms}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
- The **constant term** $f(X^*)$ is the value at the reference point.
- The **linear term** $\nabla f(X^*)^T (X - X^*)$ represents the *tangent* behavior.
- The **quadratic term** $\dfrac{1}{2} (X - X^*)^T H(X^*) (X - X^*)$ captures the *curvature*.

If $X^*$ is a local minimum, then for $X$ close to $X^*$, we must have $f(X) \geq f(X^*)$. The linear term must therefore vanish, forcing $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$, and the quadratic term must be non-negative, forcing $H(X^*)$ to be **positive semi-definite**. This is the rigorous mathematical origin of the stationary point conditions.

## 3.2 Stationary Points: First and Second Order Conditions

### 3.2.1 Definition of a Stationary Point

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A point $X^*$ is called a **stationary point** of $f(X)$ if $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ (i.e., all first partial derivatives vanish simultaneously).

### 3.2.2 First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

If $f(X)$ is differentiable and $X^*$ is an **unconstrained local extremum** (minimum or maximum), then:

$$
\nabla f(X^*) = 0
$$

**Derivation (single-variable, $n=1$):**
Consider $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$. A local minimum at $x^*$ means $f(x) \geq f(x^*)$ for all $x$ near $x^*$. Expanding by Taylor's theorem:

$$
f(x) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)(x - x^*) + \dfrac{1}{2} f''(\xi)(x - x^*)^2
$$

For $x$ on the **right** of $x^*$, $(x - x^*) > 0$. For $f(x) \geq f(x^*)$, we need the first-order term to vanish as $x \to x^*$. The only way this holds for both sides is:

$$
f'(x^*) = 0
$$

For $n$ variables, the condition generalizes to the **gradient being the zero vector**:

$$
\nabla f(X^*) = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\bigg|_{X^*} = 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

### 3.2.3 Second-Order Sufficient Conditions (SOSC)

For a function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$:

| **Condition at $x^*$** | **Conclusion** |
|---|---|
| $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$ | **Strict local minimum** |
| $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$ | **Strict local maximum** |
| $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0$ | **Inconclusive** (need higher derivatives) |

For a function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$:

| **Condition at $X^*$** | **Conclusion** |
|---|---|
| $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ and $H(X^*)$ **positive definite** | **Strict local minimum** |
| $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ and $H(X^*)$ **negative definite** | **Strict local maximum** |
| $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ and $H(X^*)$ **indefinite** | **Saddle point** |
| $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ and $H(X^*)$ **semi-definite** | **Inconclusive** |

### 3.2.4 Worked Example: Univariate Stationary Point Analysis

**Problem:** Find the stationary points of $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2$ and classify them.

**Step 1: Compute the first derivative.**
$$
f'(x) = \dfrac{df}{dx} = 3x^2 - 12x + 9
$$

**Step 2: Set the first derivative to zero and solve.**
$$
3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0
$$

Factor:
$$
3(x^2 - 4x + 3) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3(x-1)(x-3) = 0
$$

Therefore, the stationary points are $x = 1$ and $x = 3$.

**Step 3: Compute the second derivative.**
$$
f''(x) = \dfrac{d^2f}{dx^2} = 6x - 12
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the second derivative at each stationary point.**

At $x = 1$:
$$
f''(1) = 6(1) - 12 = -6 < 0
$$

Since $f''(1) < 0$, $x = 1$ is a **local maximum**.

At $x = 3$:
$$
f''(3) = 6(3) - 12 = 6 > 0
$$

Since $f''(3) > 0$, $x = 3$ is a **local minimum**.

**Step 5: Compute the function values for reporting.**
$$
f(1) = 1^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 2 = 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 = 6
$$
$$
f(3) = 3^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 2 = 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 = 2
$$

**Conclusion:**
- **Local maximum** at $x = 1$ with $f(1) = 6$
- **Local minimum** at $x = 3$ with $f(3) = 2$

### 3.2.5 Worked Example: Bivariate Stationary Point Analysis

**Problem:** Find the stationary points of $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y + 13$ and classify them.

**Step 1: Compute the partial derivatives.**
$$
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x - 4
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2y - 6
$$

**Step 2: Set the gradient to zero.**
$$
\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 2x - 4 \\ 2y - 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving the system:
$$
2x - 4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 2
$$
$$
2y - 6 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = 3
$$

So $X^* = (2, 3)^T$ is the only stationary point.

**Step 3: Compute the Hessian matrix.**
$$
H(X) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Check definiteness using principal minors (Sylvester's Criterion).**

For a 2×2 symmetric matrix, positive definiteness requires:
- **Leading principal minor:** $H_{11} = 2 > 0$ ✓
- **Determinant:** $\det(H) = (2)(2) - (0)(0) = 4 > 0$ ✓

Both conditions satisfied ⇒ $H$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 5: Conclude.**
- $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ and $H(X^*)$ is positive definite ⇒ $X^* = (2, 3)$ is a **strict local (in fact, global) minimum**.
- $f(2, 3) = 4 + 9 - 8 - 18 + 13 = 0$

## 3.3 Quick Reference: Sign of $f''$ in 1-D

$$
f''(x^*) > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Concave up (Min)}
$$
$$
f''(x^*) < 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Concave down (Max)}
$$
$$
f''(x^*) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Inconclusive (Higher-order test or Inflection)}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anatomy of an Optimization Problem (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Problem] --> B[Design Variables X]
    A --> C[Objective Function fX]
    A --> D[Constraints gX and hX]
    B --> B1[Continuous]
    B --> B2[Discrete]
    B --> B3[Mixed]
    C --> C1[Linear]
    C --> C2[Nonlinear]
    C --> C3[Quadratic]
    D --> D1[Inequality less than zero]
    D --> D2[Equality equals zero]
    A --> E[Solution X Star]
    E --> F{Feasible?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Optimal Design]
    F -- No --> H[Infeasible]
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Optimization Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Optimization Problem] --> Q1{Constraints?}
    Q1 -- None --> R1[Unconstrained]
    Q1 -- Present --> R2[Constrained]
    P --> Q2{Objective Type?}
    Q2 -- Linear --> S1[LP]
    Q2 -- Quadratic --> S2[QP]
    Q2 -- General Nonlinear --> S3[NLP]
    P --> Q3{Variable Type?}
    Q3 -- Real --> T1[Continuous]
    Q3 -- Integer --> T2[Integer Programming]
    Q3 -- Mixed --> T3[Mixed Integer NLP]
    P --> Q4{Objectives?}
    Q4 -- One --> U1[Single Objective]
    Q4 -- Many --> U2[Multi Objective Pareto]
    P --> Q5{Data Nature?}
    Q5 -- Known --> V1[Deterministic]
    Q5 -- Random --> V2[Stochastic]
```

## 4.3 Stationary Point Decision Flow (Second-Order Test)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute gradient of f at X Star] --> B{Is gradient zero vector?}
    B -- No --> C[Not a stationary point]
    B -- Yes --> D[Compute Hessian Matrix H]
    D --> E{Eigenvalues of H}
    E -- All Positive --> F[Strict Local Minimum]
    E -- All Negative --> G[Strict Local Maximum]
    E -- Mixed Signs --> H[Saddle Point]
    E -- Has Zeros --> I[Inconclusive Higher Order Test Needed]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Optimization Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[Input Problem Data] --> M1[Pre Processing]
    M1 --> M2[Gradient Computation]
    M2 --> M3[Stationarity Check]
    M3 --> M4[Definiteness Test on Hessian]
    M4 --> M5[Classification of X Star]
    M5 --> O[Output Optimal Solution and Type]
    M5 -- Infeasible --> E[Error Exit]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the general form of an optimization problem and define the feasible region.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The general form of an optimization problem is:

$$
\text{Find } X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \text{ that minimizes } f(X)
$$
$$
\text{Subject to: } g_j(X) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$
$$
h_k(X) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p
$$

Where $X$ is the vector of design variables, $f(X)$ is the objective function, $g_j(X)$ are inequality constraints, and $h_k(X)$ are equality constraints.

**Feasible Region:** The set of all points $X$ that satisfy **every** constraint is called the **feasible region** $S$, defined as:

$$
S = \{ X \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(X) \leq 0, \; j=1,\ldots,m \text{ and } h_k(X)=0, \; k=1,\ldots,p \}
$$

A point $X \in S$ is called a **feasible point**; the optimum must lie in $S$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Define stationary point. State the necessary and sufficient conditions for a function $f(x)$ to have a local minimum at $x = x^*$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Stationary Point:** A point $x^*$ (or $X^*$ in higher dimensions) is called a stationary point of $f$ if its first derivative (or gradient, in higher dimensions) is zero. In 1-D: $f'(x^*) = 0$. In $n$-D: $\nabla f(X^*) = \mathbf{0}$.

**Conditions for Local Minimum at $x^*$:**

- **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC):** $f'(x^*) = 0$
- **Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC):** $f''(x^*) > 0$

For an $n$-variable function, the SOSC generalizes to: the Hessian matrix $H(X^*)$ must be **positive definite**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always** state both FONC and SOSC explicitly. Marks are lost if you write only the gradient condition without classifying with the Hessian.
> - **Never** confuse *necessary* with *sufficient* conditions. $\nabla f = 0$ is necessary but not sufficient; you must check $H$ for a conclusion.
> - For **constrained** problems (KKT), include the complementary slackness and dual feasibility conditions — forgetting these is a common 2-mark loss.
> - Show **all intermediate steps** in differentiation. Examiners award step marks generously for partial work.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Classify optimization problems in detail with suitable examples.** (7 marks)

**(b) Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 16x - 8$.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

Optimization problems can be classified along several dimensions:

**1. Based on the existence of constraints:**
- **Unconstrained:** No restrictions on variables, e.g., minimizing $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5$.
- **Constrained:** Variables restricted by $g_j(X) \leq 0$ and/or $h_k(X) = 0$, e.g., minimizing the weight of a pressure vessel subject to a minimum volume.

**2. Based on the nature of the objective and constraints:**
- **Linear Programming (LP):** Both $f$ and constraints are linear. *Example:* Profit maximization in a factory.
- **Non-Linear Programming (NLP):** At least one function is non-linear. *Example:* Minimize drag of an airfoil.
- **Quadratic Programming (QP):** $f$ is quadratic, constraints are linear. *Example:* Portfolio optimization.
- **Geometric Programming (GP):** $f$ is a posynomial. *Example:* Engineering design with power-law models.
- **Integer Programming (IP):** Variables are integers. *Example:* Number of machines to purchase.

**3. Based on the nature of variables:**
- **Static:** Time-invariant (steady-state design).
- **Dynamic:** Time-varying (trajectory optimization of a satellite).

**4. Based on the number of objectives:**
- **Single-Objective:** One criterion to optimize.
- **Multi-Objective:** Multiple criteria, solved via Pareto front.

**5. Based on certainty of data:**
- **Deterministic:** All parameters known.
- **Stochastic:** Parameters are random variables.

*(1.5 marks per major category, with examples.)*

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 16x - 8$

**Step 1:** Compute the first derivative.

$$
f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2 + 16
$$

**Step 2:** Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve.

$$
4x^3 - 12x^2 + 16 = 0
$$

Divide by 4:

$$
x^3 - 3x^2 + 4 = 0
$$

We look for rational roots. Try $x = -1$:

$$
(-1)^3 - 3(-1)^2 + 4 = -1 - 3 + 4 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

So $(x + 1)$ is a factor. Perform polynomial division:

$$
x^3 - 3x^2 + 4 = (x + 1)(x^2 - 4x + 4) = (x + 1)(x - 2)^2
$$

Set to zero:

$$
(x + 1)(x - 2)^2 = 0
$$

The stationary points are $x = -1$ and $x = 2$ (double root).

**Step 3:** Compute the second derivative.

$$
f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x
$$

**Step 4:** Classify each stationary point.

At $x = -1$:
$$
f''(-1) = 12(1) - 24(-1) = 12 + 24 = 36 > 0
$$

Since $f''(-1) > 0$, the point $x = -1$ is a **local minimum**.
$$
f(-1) = 1 + 4 - 16 - 8 = -19
$$

At $x = 2$:
$$
f''(2) = 12(4) - 24(2) = 48 - 48 = 0
$$

Since $f''(2) = 0$, the second-order test is **inconclusive**. We must examine the first derivative's sign on either side of $x = 2$:

For $x = 1.9$:
$$
f'(1.9) = 4(6.859) - 12(3.61) + 16 = 27.436 - 43.32 + 16 = 0.116 > 0
$$

For $x = 2.1$:
$$
f'(2.1) = 4(9.261) - 12(4.41) + 16 = 37.044 - 52.92 + 16 = 0.124 > 0
$$

Since $f'(x) > 0$ on **both** sides of $x = 2$, the function is **increasing through $x = 2$**. Hence $x = 2$ is an **inflection point** (not a local extremum).

**Final Answer:**
- $x = -1$ is a **local minimum** with $f(-1) = -19$
- $x = 2$ is an **inflection point**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) Explain the engineering significance of optimization with at least three real-world examples.** (7 marks)

**(b) Find the stationary points of $f(x, y) = 2x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 4y + 10$ and classify them using the Hessian test.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

Engineering optimization is the **systematic process** of selecting the best design from a set of alternatives, balancing performance, cost, and constraint satisfaction. Its significance lies in:

**1. Cost Reduction:** Optimization identifies the minimum-material or minimum-energy design without violating performance. *Example:* Minimizing the weight of a steel truss bridge while ensuring it can support a 50-tonne load, leading to material cost savings of 15-30%.

**2. Improved Performance:** In aerospace engineering, the **shape of an aircraft wing** is optimized to minimize drag and maximize lift-to-drag ratio. Tools like CFD coupled with gradient-based optimizers (e.g., adjoint methods) routinely achieve 5-10% drag reduction.

**3. Process Efficiency:** In chemical engineering, **reactor design** is optimized to maximize yield of a desired product subject to temperature, pressure, and equilibrium constraints. This is formulated as an NLP problem.

**4. Resource Allocation:** In operations research, **linear programming** is used to schedule production across multiple plants to minimize total cost while meeting demand — a classic application in manufacturing industries.

**5. Reliability and Safety:** In structural engineering, optimization can maximize the **safety factor** (or minimize the probability of failure) under random loading conditions — a stochastic optimization problem.

*(Approximately 1.4 marks per point with clear engineering examples.)*

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $f(x, y) = 2x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 4y + 10$

**Step 1:** Compute the first partial derivatives.

$$
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 4x - 4
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2y - 4
$$

**Step 2:** Set the gradient to zero.

$$
\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 4x - 4 \\ 2y - 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving the system:
$$
4x - 4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 1
$$
$$
2y - 4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = 2
$$

Stationary point: $X^* = (1, 2)^T$

**Step 3:** Compute the Hessian matrix.

$$
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} = 4
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} = 2
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Test definiteness using Sylvester's criterion.

- **Leading principal minor:** $H_{11} = 4 > 0$ ✓
- **Determinant:** $\det(H) = (4)(2) - (0)(0) = 8 > 0$ ✓

Since both principal minors are positive, $H$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 5:** Conclude.

Since $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ and $H$ is positive definite, $X^* = (1, 2)$ is a **strict local minimum** (in fact, the global minimum since $f$ is convex).

**Step 6:** Compute the minimum value.

$$
f(1, 2) = 2(1)^2 + (2)^2 - 4(1) - 4(2) + 10 = 2 + 4 - 4 - 8 + 10 = 4
$$

**Final Answer:** The function has a unique stationary point at $X^* = (1, 2)$, which is a **strict local (global) minimum** with $f(1, 2) = 4$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] An optimization problem is fully specified by **three components**: the **design variables** $X$, the **objective function** $f(X)$, and the **constraints** $g_j(X), h_k(X)$.
- [x] **Maximization is converted to minimization** by negating the objective: $\max f = \min(-f)$.
- [x] The **feasible region** $S$ is the set of all $X$ satisfying every constraint; the optimal solution must lie in $S$.
- [x] Classification is **multi-dimensional**: based on constraints (unconstrained/constrained), objective (LP/NLP/QP/GP/IP), variables (static/dynamic, continuous/discrete), objectives (single/multi), and data (deterministic/stochastic).
- [x] The **gradient** $\nabla f(X)$ points in the direction of steepest ascent; $-\nabla f$ is the descent direction.
- [x] A **stationary point** $X^*$ satisfies $\nabla f(X^*) = 0$ (first-order necessary condition).
- [x] **Second-order sufficient condition:** $H(X^*)$ positive definite $\Rightarrow$ strict local **minimum**; negative definite $\Rightarrow$ strict local **maximum**; indefinite $\Rightarrow$ **saddle point**; semi-definite $\Rightarrow$ inconclusive.
- [x] In 1-D: $f'(x^*)=0$ and $f''(x^*)>0$ ⇒ minimum; $f''(x^*)<0$ ⇒ maximum; $f''(x^*)=0$ ⇒ inconclusive (use higher derivatives or sign chart).
- [x] The **Hessian** is a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix of second partial derivatives; its definiteness is checked using **Sylvester's criterion** (all leading principal minors positive ⇒ positive definite).
- [x] For **constrained** problems, the **KKT conditions** generalize the unconstrained gradient condition: $\nabla f(X^*) + \sum \lambda_j \nabla g_j(X^*) + \sum \mu_k \nabla h_k(X^*) = 0$, along with complementary slackness and dual feasibility.
- [x] Always **verify second-order conditions** before declaring a stationary point as a minimum or maximum in the KTU exam.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** solving $\nabla f = 0$ but forgetting to classify with the Hessian — partial marks only.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of single and two variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT413 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions of single and two variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Function of a Single Variable

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **function of a single variable** is a rule that assigns to each element `x` in a domain `D` a **single, unique** element `y = f(x)` in the codomain. Formally,

$$f : D \subseteq \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, \quad x \mapsto f(x)$$

The **graph** is the set of ordered pairs `{ (x, f(x)) : x ∈ D }` which, in the two-dimensional Cartesian plane, traces a curve. KTU 2024 emphasises the interplay between algebraic representation and geometric intuition, so a function is not merely an equation — it is a mapping that produces a unique output for every admissible input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Valued Rule:** For a given `x`, there must be **exactly one** `f(x)`. The vertical line test is the geometric check for this property. This is the cornerstone concept the Board Examiner tests repeatedly.

**Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a function of one variable as a **vending machine that accepts only one coin type**. You put in one number (say, ₹10), and the machine gives out exactly one product (say, a juice box). If the same input could give two different outputs, the machine would be "broken" — that is, the rule would not be a function. The temperature of a city during a day `T(t)`, the speed of a car `v(t)`, or the profit from selling `x` units `P(x)` are all single-variable functions.

### 1.2 Function of Two Variables

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **function of two variables** is a mapping that takes an **ordered pair** `(x, y) ∈ D ⊆ ℝ²` and returns a single real number `z = f(x, y)`.

$$f : D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, \quad (x, y) \mapsto f(x, y)$$

The **graph** of `f` is a **surface** in 3D space given by the set

$$\{ (x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^{3} : z = f(x, y), \; (x, y) \in D \}$$

**Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a **hiking map with elevation contours**. At any point on the map (latitude `x`, longitude `y`), there is exactly one height above sea level. The function `h(x, y)` gives that elevation. You cannot stand at one point and have two different elevations simultaneously. Other real-world examples:

| Application | Function | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| Heat distribution on a metal plate | `T(x, y)` | Position (x, y) on plate |
| Manufacturing cost | `C(K, L)` | Capital (K) and Labour (L) inputs |
| Profit from two products | `P(x, y)` | Quantities (x, y) of products A and B |
| Pressure in a tank | `P(x, y, z)` | Spatial coordinates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Domain Rule:** For a function `f(x, y)`, the domain is the set of all `(x, y)` for which the expression is mathematically defined. You must exclude points that make the denominator zero, produce a negative argument under an even root, or take the logarithm of a non-positive number.

### 1.3 Domain and Range — Operational Definitions

For `f : ℝ² → ℝ`:
- **Domain** `D(f) = { (x, y) : f(x, y) is defined }`
- **Range** `R(f) = { z ∈ ℝ : ∃ (x, y) ∈ D(f), z = f(x, y) }`

For example, consider

$$f(x, y) = \sqrt{1 - x^{2} - y^{2}}$$

The domain requires `1 - x² - y² ≥ 0`, i.e., `x² + y² ≤ 1` — a **closed unit disk**. The range is `[0, 1]`. This single example tests the KTU Examiner's most common 3-mark problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Functions (KTU 2024 Critical Distinctions)

**A. Polynomial Functions of Two Variables**
General form: `f(x, y) = Σ aᵢⱼ xⁱ yʲ`. Example:

$$f(x, y) = 2x^{2} + 3xy - 5y^{2} + 4x - 7y + 9$$

Such **quadratic functions** are the bedrock of optimization because they yield closed-form solutions.

**B. Implicit vs Explicit Functions**
- **Explicit:** `z = f(x, y)` — solved for `z`.
- **Implicit:** `F(x, y, z) = 0` — cannot be solved for `z` in closed form. Example: `x² + y² + z² = 1`.

**C. Convex and Concave Functions** (Crucial for Optimization!)
- A function `f` is **convex** on a convex set `D` if for all `x₁, x₂ ∈ D` and `λ ∈ [0, 1]`:

$$f(\lambda x_{1} + (1-\lambda) x_{2}) \leq \lambda f(x_{1}) + (1-\lambda) f(x_{2})$$

- A function is **concave** if the inequality reverses.
- **Geometric Intuition:** A convex function lies *below* the chord connecting any two points on its graph. Visualize the bowl of a wine glass: that is a convex function. An inverted bowl is concave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Convexity Matters in Optimization:** The KTU 2024 scheme stresses that for a **convex function on a convex set, every local minimum is a global minimum**. This is the single most powerful result in mathematical optimization and the reason engineers prefer to reformulate problems as convex ones.

**D. Continuity of Multivariable Functions**
`f(x, y)` is **continuous at** `(a, b)` if:

$$\lim_{(x, y) \to (a, b)} f(x, y) = f(a, b)$$

The limit must be **path-independent** — i.e., the same value must be obtained whether `(x, y)` approaches `(a, b)` along the x-axis, y-axis, the line `y = mx`, or any curved path. If two different paths yield different limits, the limit does not exist, and the function is **discontinuous** at that point.

**E. Differentiability of Two-Variable Functions**
`f(x, y)` is **differentiable at** `(a, b)` if there exist constants `A, B` such that:

$$f(a + h, b + k) - f(a, b) = Ah + Bk + o(\sqrt{h^{2} + k^{2}})$$

Equivalently, the increment can be approximated linearly. Differentiable ⇒ Continuous, but Continuous ⇏ Differentiable.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Single variable function | `y = f(x)` | One input → one output |
| Two variable function | `z = f(x, y)` | Ordered pair → one output |
| Partial derivative w.r.t. x | `fₓ = ∂f/∂x` | Hold `y` constant, differentiate w.r.t. `x` |
| Partial derivative w.r.t. y | `f_y = ∂f/∂y` | Hold `x` constant, differentiate w.r.t. `y` |
| Mixed partials (Clairaut's) | `∂²f/∂x∂y = ∂²f/∂y∂x` | Equal if continuous |
| Gradient vector | `∇f = (fₓ, f_y)` | Points in direction of steepest ascent |
| Directional derivative | `D_u f = ∇f · u` | Where `u` is a unit vector |
| Hessian matrix | `H = [[fₓₓ, fₓ_y], [f_y_x, f_y_y]]` | 2×2 matrix of second partials |
| Critical point | `∇f = (0, 0)` | Necessary condition for extremum |
| Second derivative test | `D = fₓₓ · f_y_y - (fₓ_y)²` | `D > 0, fₓₓ > 0` → min; `D > 0, fₓₓ < 0` → max; `D < 0` → saddle |
| Convexity (single var.) | `f''(x) ≥ 0 ∀ x` | Sufficient condition |
| Convexity (two var.) | `H` positive semidefinite | All eigenvalues `≥ 0` |
| Level curve | `f(x, y) = c` | Trace of constant function value |
| Taylor expansion (single) | `f(x+h) ≈ f(x) + f'(x)h + ½f''(x)h²` | Up to second order |
| Taylor expansion (two) | `f(X+H) ≈ f(X) + ∇f·H + ½ Hᵀ Hf H` | Vector form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **machine learning**, the gradient `∇f` is the direction in which the loss function increases fastest; gradient descent takes steps in `-∇f`. In **structural engineering**, level curves of stress functions `σ(x, y) = c` show lines of constant stress. In **economics**, indifference curves are level sets of utility functions.

### 2.3 Level Curves (Contour Plots) — Geometric Mastery

For a function `f(x, y)`, a **level curve** at height `c` is the set of points

$$L_{c} = \{ (x, y) \in D : f(x, y) = c \}$$

These are the "elevations" of the surface. The level curves of `f(x, y) = x² + y²` are concentric circles `x² + y² = c` for `c ≥ 0`. The closer the level curves, the steeper the surface at that location. KTU examiners often ask: *"Sketch the level curves of `f(x, y) = x² - y` for c = 0, 1, 2."* The answer is the family of parabolas `y = x² - c`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Taylor Series Expansion for a Function of Two Variables

Starting from the single-variable Taylor expansion and extending to two variables, we derive the multivariable form rigorously.

**Step 1: Single-Variable Foundation**

For a function `g(t) = f(x + th, y + tk)` of a single variable `t`, apply the single-variable Taylor expansion around `t = 0`:

$$g(t) = g(0) + t \, g'(0) + \frac{t^{2}}{2} g''(0) + O(t^{3})$$

**Step 2: Chain Rule Application**

By the multivariable chain rule:

$$g'(t) = f_{x}(x+th, y+tk) \cdot h + f_{y}(x+th, y+tk) \cdot k$$

Setting `t = 0`:

$$g'(0) = f_{x}(x, y) \, h + f_{y}(x, y) \, k = \nabla f(x,y) \cdot \begin{bmatrix} h \\ k \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Second Derivative**

Differentiating `g'(t)` once more:

$$g''(t) = h^{2} f_{xx} + 2hk \, f_{xy} + k^{2} f_{yy}$$

where all partials are evaluated at `(x + th, y + tk)`. At `t = 0`:

$$g''(0) = h^{2} f_{xx}(x,y) + 2hk \, f_{xy}(x,y) + k^{2} f_{yy}(x,y)$$

**Step 4: Assemble the Expansion**

Substituting into the Taylor formula and setting `t = 1` (i.e., evaluating at `(x+h, y+k)`):

$$f(x+h, y+k) = f(x, y) + \left[ f_{x} \, h + f_{y} \, k \right] + \frac{1}{2} \left[ f_{xx} \, h^{2} + 2 f_{xy} \, hk + f_{yy} \, k^{2} \right] + O(\Vert H \Vert^{3})$$

**Step 5: Matrix-Vector Form (Most Elegant)**

In compact notation, where `X = (x, y)ᵀ` and `H = (h, k)ᵀ`:

$$\boxed{f(X + H) \approx f(X) + \nabla f(X)^{T} H + \frac{1}{2} H^{T} H_{f}(X) H}$$

where `H_f(X)` is the **Hessian matrix** at `X`. This formula is the foundation of **Newton's method** for multivariable optimization.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Critical Point Classification

> **[KTU Pattern Problem]**
> Find and classify the critical points of
> $$f(x, y) = x^{3} - 3x + 3xy^{2}$$

**Step 1: Compute First-Order Partial Derivatives**

$$f_{x} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^{2} - 3 + 3y^{2}$$

$$f_{y} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 6xy$$

**Step 2: Solve the Critical Point Equations `∇f = 0`**

Setting `f_y = 0`:

$$6xy = 0 \implies x = 0 \text{ or } y = 0$$

**Case 1:** `x = 0`. Substitute into `f_x = 0`:
$$3(0) - 3 + 3y^{2} = 0 \implies y^{2} = 1 \implies y = \pm 1$$
Critical point: `(0, 1)` and `(0, -1)`.

**Case 2:** `y = 0`. Substitute into `f_x = 0`:
$$3x^{2} - 3 = 0 \implies x^{2} = 1 \implies x = \pm 1$$
Critical point: `(1, 0)` and `(-1, 0)`.

**Step 3: Compute Second-Order Partial Derivatives**

$$f_{xx} = 6x, \quad f_{yy} = 6x, \quad f_{xy} = 6y$$

**Step 4: Apply the Second Derivative Test**

The discriminant is:

$$D(x, y) = f_{xx} \cdot f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2} = (6x)(6x) - (6y)^{2} = 36(x^{2} - y^{2})$$

**Step 5: Classify Each Critical Point**

| Point | `fₓₓ = 6x` | `D = 36(x² - y²)` | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| `(0, 1)` | `0` | `-36` | **Saddle Point** (D < 0) |
| `(0, -1)` | `0` | `-36` | **Saddle Point** (D < 0) |
| `(1, 0)` | `6` | `36` | **Local Minimum** (D > 0, fₓₓ > 0) |
| `(-1, 0)` | `-6` | `36` | **Local Maximum** (D > 0, fₓₓ < 0) |

> [!NOTE]
> At `(0, ±1)`, the test gives `D < 0` directly, so they are saddle points even though `fₓₓ = 0`. The test is decisive whenever `D ≠ 0`. When `D = 0`, the test is **inconclusive** and higher-order analysis is required.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functions of Single vs. Two Variables

| Property | Single Variable `f(x)` | Two Variables `f(x, y)` |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Subset of ℝ | Subset of ℝ² |
| Graph | 2D curve | 3D surface |
| Differentiation | One derivative `f'(x)` | Two partials `fₓ, f_y` |
| Critical point | `f'(x) = 0` | `fₓ = 0` and `f_y = 0` |
| First derivative test | Sign change of `f'` | Gradient direction reversal |
| Second derivative test | `f'' > 0` min, `f'' < 0` max | Hessian discriminant `D` |
| Convexity condition | `f'' ≥ 0` | Hessian positive semidefinite |
| Curvature measure | `f''` (scalar) | Hessian matrix (2×2) |
| Taylor expansion | `f + f'h + ½f''h²` | `f + ∇f·H + ½HᵀHf H` |
| Level set | Point (x) | Level curve (1D curve in 2D) |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Directional Derivative

> **Problem:** Find the directional derivative of `f(x, y) = x²y + 3y²` at point `(1, 2)` in the direction of vector `v = (3, 4)`.

**Step 1: Compute the Gradient**

$$f_{x}(x, y) = 2xy, \quad f_{y}(x, y) = x^{2} + 6y$$

$$\nabla f(x, y) = (2xy, \; x^{2} + 6y)$$

**Step 2: Evaluate Gradient at `(1, 2)`**

$$\nabla f(1, 2) = (2 \cdot 1 \cdot 2, \; 1 + 6 \cdot 2) = (4, 13)$$

**Step 3: Normalize the Direction Vector**

$$\Vert v \Vert = \sqrt{3^{2} + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$\hat{u} = \left( \frac{3}{5}, \frac{4}{5} \right)$$

**Step 4: Compute the Directional Derivative**

$$D_{\hat{u}} f(1, 2) = \nabla f(1, 2) \cdot \hat{u} = (4)\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) + (13)\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) = \frac{12 + 52}{5} = \frac{64}{5} = 12.8$$

**Interpretation:** The function `f` increases at a rate of `12.8` units per unit distance in the direction `(3, 4)` from the point `(1, 2)`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart for Critical Points of f(x, y)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Function f(x,y)"] --> B["Compute Partial Derivatives"]
    B --> C["f_x and f_y"]
    C --> D["Solve f_x = 0 AND f_y = 0"]
    D --> E["Critical Point Set: P1, P2, ..."]
    E --> F["Compute Second Partials"]
    F --> G["Build Hessian H = f_xx, f_xy, f_yy"]
    G --> H["Compute Discriminant D = f_xx f_yy minus f_xy squared"]
    H --> I{"D greater than 0?"}
    I -- "No" --> J{"D less than 0?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> K{"f_xx greater than 0?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> L["SADDLE POINT"]
    J -- "No" --> M["Inconclusive: Use Higher Order Test"]
    K -- "Yes" --> N["LOCAL MINIMUM"]
    K -- "No" --> O["LOCAL MAXIMUM"]
    L --> P["End: Classification Complete"]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Function Hierarchy: From Single to Multivariable

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Single Variable f of x"] --> B["Derivative f prime of x"]
    B --> C["Second Derivative f double prime"]
    C --> D["Optimization: f prime equals 0"]
    A2["Two Variables f of x,y"] --> B2["Partial f_x and f_y"]
    B2 --> C2["Gradient Vector nabla f"]
    C2 --> D2["Hessian Matrix H"]
    D2 --> E2["Critical Point: nabla f equals 0"]
    D --> F["Concise Analysis"]
    E2 --> F["Concise Analysis"]
    F --> G["Application to Optimization Problems"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Function Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Vector X = x, y"] --> B["Domain Validator"]
    B --> C{"X in Domain D?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Return Undefined"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Expression Evaluator"]
    E --> F["Compute f of x,y numerically"]
    F --> G["Output Scalar z = f of x,y"]
    G --> H["Store in Cache for Gradient Computation"]
    H --> I["Finite Difference or Auto Differentiation"]
    I --> J["Output Gradient nabla f"]
    J --> K["Feed to Optimizer Newton or Gradient Descent"]
    K --> L["Convergence Check"]
    L --> M{"Gradient Norm below epsilon?"}
    M -- "Yes" --> N["Output Optimal X star and f of X star"]
    M -- "No" --> O["Update X using Step Direction"]
    O --> A
```

### 4.4 Level Curve Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Surface z = f of x,y"] --> B["Intersect with plane z = c"]
    B --> C["Level Curve L_c: f of x,y = c"]
    C --> D["Different c gives different curves"]
    D --> E["Closely spaced curves = Steep surface"]
    D --> F["Widely spaced curves = Gentle slope"]
    E --> G["Used in Topographic Maps and Contour Plots"]
    F --> G
```

### 4.5 Convex vs. Concave Function: Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Function f of x,y"] --> B{"Check Hessian H Positive Semidefinite?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["CONVEX FUNCTION"]
    C --> C1["Every local min is global min"]
    C --> C2["Set of minima is convex"]
    C --> C3["Easy to optimize"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Check Hessian H Negative Semidefinite?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["CONCAVE FUNCTION"]
    E --> E1["Every local max is global max"]
    E --> E2["Set of maxima is convex"]
    D -- "No" --> F["NEITHER CONVEX NOR CONCAVE"]
    F --> F1["May have saddle points"]
    F --> F2["Multiple local optima possible"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Define a function of two variables. Find the domain and range of `f(x, y) = √(9 - x² - y²)`.**

**Model Answer:**

A function of two variables is a mapping `f : D ⊆ ℝ² → ℝ` that assigns a unique real number `f(x, y)` to each ordered pair `(x, y)` in the domain `D`.

For the given function `f(x, y) = √(9 - x² - y²)`, the expression under the square root must be non-negative:

$$9 - x^{2} - y^{2} \geq 0 \implies x^{2} + y^{2} \leq 9$$

**Domain:** `D = { (x, y) ∈ ℝ² : x² + y² ≤ 9 }` — a closed disk of radius 3 centered at the origin.

**Range:** Since `x² + y²` ranges from `0` to `9`, the expression `9 - x² - y²` ranges from `0` to `9`, and taking the square root:

$$R = [0, 3]$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

**State and explain Clairaut's theorem on equality of mixed partial derivatives.**

**Model Answer:**

**Clairaut's Theorem (Schwarz's Theorem):** If `f(x, y)` and its partial derivatives `fₓ, f_y, fₓ_y, f_y_x` are all continuous in a neighbourhood of the point `(a, b)`, then the mixed partial derivatives are equal at that point:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x \, \partial y}(a, b) = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y \, \partial x}(a, b)$$

**Significance:** This theorem justifies the symmetry of the Hessian matrix `H = Hᵀ` in optimization. As long as the second partials are continuous (which they are for all "well-behaved" KTU problems), the order of differentiation does not matter, simplifying the calculation of mixed derivatives significantly. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** For the function `f(x, y) = x³ + y³ - 3xy`, find all critical points and classify them using the second derivative test. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Find the directional derivative of `f(x, y) = x² - y²` at the point `(2, 1)` in the direction of the vector `v = (1, 2)`. Also find the maximum directional derivative and the direction in which it occurs. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Compute the first partial derivatives.

$$f_{x} = 3x^{2} - 3y, \quad f_{y} = 3y^{2} - 3x$$

**Step 2:** Set the gradient to zero.

$$3x^{2} - 3y = 0 \implies y = x^{2}$$

$$3y^{2} - 3x = 0 \implies x = y^{2}$$

Substituting `y = x²` into `x = y²`:

$$x = (x^{2})^{2} = x^{4} \implies x^{4} - x = 0 \implies x(x^{3} - 1) = 0$$

So `x = 0` or `x³ = 1` implying `x = 1`. The critical points are `(0, 0)` and `(1, 1)`.

**Step 3:** Compute the second partial derivatives.

$$f_{xx} = 6x, \quad f_{yy} = 6y, \quad f_{xy} = -3$$

**Step 4:** Compute the discriminant `D = fₓₓ · f_y_y - (fₓ_y)² = 36xy - 9`.

| Point | `fₓₓ` | `f_y_y` | `D` | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `(0, 0)` | `0` | `0` | `-9` | **Saddle Point** (D < 0) |
| `(1, 1)` | `6` | `6` | `27` | **Local Minimum** (D > 0, fₓₓ > 0) |

At `(1, 1)`, the function value is `f(1, 1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1`. Hence the local minimum is `-1`. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute the gradient of `f(x, y) = x² - y²`.

$$f_{x} = 2x, \quad f_{y} = -2y$$

$$\nabla f(x, y) = (2x, -2y)$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate at `(2, 1)`.

$$\nabla f(2, 1) = (4, -2)$$

**Step 3:** Normalize the direction vector `v = (1, 2)`.

$$\Vert v \Vert = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5}$$

$$\hat{u} = \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the directional derivative.

$$D_{\hat{u}} f(2, 1) = (4)\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right) + (-2)\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right) = \frac{4 - 4}{\sqrt{5}} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Maximum directional derivative.

The maximum directional derivative at any point is the magnitude of the gradient:

$$\max D_{\hat{u}} f(2,1) = \Vert \nabla f(2,1) \Vert = \sqrt{4^{2} + (-2)^{2}} = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5}$$

It occurs in the direction of the gradient:

$$\hat{u}_{max} = \frac{\nabla f(2,1)}{\Vert \nabla f(2,1) \Vert} = \left( \frac{4}{2\sqrt{5}}, \frac{-2}{2\sqrt{5}} \right) = \left( \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}} \right)$$

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — December 2023]*

**(a)** Use the Taylor series expansion of `f(x, y) = eˣ sin(y)` about the point `(0, 0)` to compute `f(0.1, 0.1)` approximately up to second-order terms. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A rectangular box is to be constructed with volume `8 m³`. The cost per square metre of the base is ₹200, the top is ₹100, and the sides are ₹50. Use optimization of a function of two variables to find the dimensions that minimize the cost. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivatives at `(0, 0)`.

$$f(x, y) = e^{x} \sin y$$

First partials:

$$f_{x} = e^{x} \sin y, \quad f_{y} = e^{x} \cos y$$

At `(0, 0)`: `f = 0`, `fₓ = 0`, `f_y = 1`.

Second partials:

$$f_{xx} = e^{x} \sin y, \quad f_{yy} = -e^{x} \sin y, \quad f_{xy} = e^{x} \cos y$$

At `(0, 0)`: `fₓₓ = 0`, `f_y_y = 0`, `fₓ_y = 1`.

**Step 2:** Apply the second-order Taylor formula.

$$f(h, k) \approx f(0,0) + f_{x} h + f_{y} k + \frac{1}{2}\left[ f_{xx} h^{2} + 2 f_{xy} hk + f_{yy} k^{2} \right]$$

$$f(h, k) \approx 0 + 0 \cdot h + 1 \cdot k + \frac{1}{2}\left[ 0 \cdot h^{2} + 2(1) hk + 0 \cdot k^{2} \right]$$

$$f(h, k) \approx k + hk$$

**Step 3:** Substitute `h = k = 0.1`.

$$f(0.1, 0.1) \approx 0.1 + (0.1)(0.1) = 0.1 + 0.01 = 0.11$$

**Verification:** Exact value `e^0.1 · sin(0.1) ≈ 1.1052 · 0.0998 ≈ 0.1103`. The approximation `0.11` is accurate to 0.27%, confirming the validity of the second-order Taylor expansion. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Set up the problem. Let the box have base dimensions `x` and `y` (metres) and height `z` (metres). Volume constraint:

$$xyz = 8 \implies z = \frac{8}{xy}$$

**Step 2:** Formulate the cost function. Areas and their costs:

- Base area = `xy` @ ₹200/m² → cost = `200xy`
- Top area = `xy` @ ₹100/m² → cost = `100xy`
- Four sides, each of area `xz` or `yz`, total = `2xz + 2yz` @ ₹50/m² → cost = `50(2xz + 2yz) = 100xz + 100yz`

Total cost:

$$C(x, y) = 200xy + 100xy + 100xz + 100yz = 300xy + 100xz + 100yz$$

Substituting `z = 8/(xy)`:

$$C(x, y) = 300xy + 100x \cdot \frac{8}{xy} + 100y \cdot \frac{8}{xy} = 300xy + \frac{800}{y} + \frac{800}{x}$$

**Step 3:** Compute partial derivatives.

$$C_{x} = 300y - \frac{800}{x^{2}}, \quad C_{y} = 300x - \frac{800}{y^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Set to zero.

$$300y = \frac{800}{x^{2}} \implies x^{2} y = \frac{8}{3}$$

$$300x = \frac{800}{y^{2}} \implies xy^{2} = \frac{8}{3}$$

Dividing these two equations:

$$\frac{x^{2}y}{xy^{2}} = 1 \implies \frac{x}{y} = 1 \implies x = y$$

Substituting back: `x³ = 8/3` ⇒ `x = y = (8/3)^(1/3) = 2 / (3)^(1/3)`. Computing:

$$x = y = \frac{2}{3^{1/3}} \approx \frac{2}{1.4422} \approx 1.387 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Find `z`.

$$z = \frac{8}{xy} = \frac{8}{(1.387)^{2}} \approx \frac{8}{1.924} \approx 4.158 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Verify it's a minimum using the second derivative test.

$$C_{xx} = \frac{1600}{x^{3}} > 0, \quad C_{yy} = \frac{1600}{y^{3}} > 0, \quad C_{xy} = 300$$

$$D = C_{xx} C_{yy} - C_{xy}^{2} = \frac{1600}{x^{3}} \cdot \frac{1600}{y^{3}} - 90000$$

At `x = y ≈ 1.387`: `D ≈ (1600/1.387³)² - 90000 ≈ 600² - 90000 = 360000 - 90000 = 270000 > 0`. Since `D > 0` and `Cₓₓ > 0`, this is a **local minimum**.

**Optimal Dimensions:** `x = y ≈ 1.387 m`, `z ≈ 4.158 m`. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Marks at Risk:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Discriminant:** Students frequently compute `D = fₓₓ · f_y_y + (fₓ_y)²` instead of `D = fₓₓ · f_y_y - (fₓ_y)²`. The MINUS sign is mandatory and the most common source of `−2 marks`.
> 2. **Unnormalized Direction Vector:** In directional derivative problems, the direction vector `v` MUST be normalized to a unit vector `û` before dotting with the gradient. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Convexity Confusion:** Many students incorrectly classify `fₓₓ > 0` alone as a sufficient condition for a minimum in two variables. The discriminant `D > 0` is **necessary**; `fₓₓ` only then disambiguates min vs. max.
> 4. **Domain Restrictions:** When asked for the domain, students forget to exclude points causing division by zero or negative radicands, losing the "domain" portion of marks.
> 5. **Taylor Series Sign:** In the mixed partial term, it is `2 · fₓ_y · h · k`, NOT `fₓ_y · h · k`. Missing the factor of 2 results in `−1 mark`.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Functions of Single and Two Variables:**

- ✅ **Single variable function:** `f : ℝ → ℝ`; graph is a **2D curve**; derivative is a **single number** `f'(x)`.
- ✅ **Two variable function:** `f : ℝ² → ℝ`; graph is a **3D surface**; derivative is the **gradient vector** `∇f = (fₓ, f_y)`.
- ✅ **Domain** for `f(x, y) = √g(x,y)` or `ln g(x,y)`: require `g(x, y) > 0` (or `≥ 0` for `√`).
- ✅ **Partial derivative** `fₓ`: differentiate treating `y` as a constant.
- ✅ **Clairaut's theorem:** `fₓ_y = f_y_x` whenever both are continuous.
- ✅ **Critical point condition:** `∇f = (0, 0)` — solve simultaneously.
- ✅ **Second derivative test discriminant:** `D = fₓₓ · f_y_y − (fₓ_y)²`.
  - `D > 0, fₓₓ > 0` → **local minimum**
  - `D > 0, fₓₓ < 0` → **local maximum**
  - `D < 0` → **saddle point**
  - `D = 0` → **inconclusive** (use higher-order test)
- ✅ **Gradient direction:** `∇f` points in the direction of **steepest ascent**; `−∇f` in steepest descent.
- ✅ **Directional derivative formula:** `D_û f = ∇f · û` where `û` is a unit vector.
- ✅ **Maximum directional derivative magnitude:** `‖∇f‖`; direction: `û = ∇f / ‖∇f‖`.
- ✅ **Convexity (two variables):** Hessian is positive semidefinite ⟺ all eigenvalues `≥ 0` ⟺ all principal minors `≥ 0`.
- ✅ **Level curves:** `f(x, y) = c`; closer spacing indicates steeper gradient.
- ✅ **Taylor expansion (two variables, second order):** `f(X + H) ≈ f(X) + ∇f · H + ½ Hᵀ H_f H`.
- ✅ **Engineering link:** Gradient descent and Newton's method in ML use these exact formulas.

**Master Formula Card:**

$$\boxed{\nabla f = \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \right), \quad H_{f} = \begin{bmatrix} f_{xx} & f_{xy} \\ f_{yx} & f_{yy} \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = f_{xx} f_{yy} - (f_{xy})^{2}}$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convexity and concavity of functions: Definition of global and local optima, optimality criteria, linear programming methods for optimum design, standard form of linear programming (LP) problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT413 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Convexity and concavity of functions: Definition of global and local optima, optimality criteria, linear programming methods for optimum design, standard form of linear programming (LP) problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Convexity and Concavity of Functions

### Formal Academic Definition

A function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is said to be **convex** on a convex set $S$ if for any two points $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2 \in S$ and for every scalar $\lambda \in [0, 1]$, the following inequality holds:

$$f(\lambda \mathbf{x}_1 + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{x}_2) \leq \lambda f(\mathbf{x}_1) + (1-\lambda) f(\mathbf{x}_2)$$

Similarly, $f$ is said to be **concave** on $S$ if for all $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2 \in S$ and $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$f(\lambda \mathbf{x}_1 + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{x}_2) \geq \lambda f(\mathbf{x}_1) + (1-\lambda) f(\mathbf{x}_2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A function is **convex** if its epigraph (set of points above the graph) is a convex set. A function is **concave** if its hypograph is convex, equivalently, $-f$ is convex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear functions are simultaneously convex and concave.** This is a special structural property fundamental to Linear Programming (LP).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **stretched rubber sheet** over a bowl:
- The **bowl shape** is a **convex function** — any chord (straight line connecting two points on the curve) lies *above* the curve. Picture the function $f(x) = x^2$.
- Now invert the bowl into a **dome** — this represents a **concave function** (e.g., $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$). Any chord lies *below* the curve.

In engineering design, convexity guarantees that **local optima are also global optima** — a critical property that makes optimization tractable.

---

## 1.2 Global and Local Optima

### Local Optimum (Local Minimum)

A point $\mathbf{x}^* \in S$ is a **local minimum** of $f(\mathbf{x})$ over $S$ if there exists an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \forall \, \mathbf{x} \in S \cap N_{\varepsilon}(\mathbf{x}^*)$$

where $N_{\varepsilon}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is the **ε-neighborhood** defined as:

$$N_{\varepsilon}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \{\mathbf{x} : \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert < \varepsilon\}$$

A point is a **strict local minimum** if $f(\mathbf{x}^*) < f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{x}^*$ in the neighborhood.

### Global Optimum (Global Minimum)

A point $\mathbf{x}^* \in S$ is a **global minimum** of $f(\mathbf{x})$ over $S$ if:

$$f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \forall \, \mathbf{x} \in S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Local minimum** = Best point in a small neighborhood
> - **Global minimum** = Best point over the *entire feasible region*
> - Every global minimum is a local minimum, but the converse is **NOT** true in general non-convex problems.

### Intuitive Analogy

Consider mountain ranges:
- A **local minimum** is the bottom of a small valley.
- A **global minimum** is the bottom of the **deepest valley** in the entire mountain range.
- A **convex** mountain range has only **one valley**, so the local min equals the global min.

---

## 1.3 Optimality Criteria — First and Second Order

### First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

If $f(\mathbf{x})$ is continuously differentiable ($\mathcal{C}^1$) and $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a local minimum in the interior of the feasible region, then:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

The gradient vector components:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \left[ \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right]^T_{\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}^*} = \mathbf{0}$$

### Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)

If $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and the **Hessian matrix** $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **positive definite**, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local minimum**:

$$\mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} > 0 \quad \forall \, \mathbf{d} \neq \mathbf{0}$$

For local maximum, the Hessian must be **negative definite**.

The Hessian matrix is defined as:

$$\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance of Optimality Criteria:**
> In **structural design**, optimality criteria methods (OCM) exploit these conditions to find minimum-weight trusses and frames — directly applying $K \cdot \mathbf{u} = \lambda \cdot \mathbf{C}_0 \cdot \mathbf{u}$ in displacement-based design.

---

## 1.4 Linear Programming Methods for Optimum Design

### What is Linear Programming (LP)?

A **Linear Programming** problem is an optimization problem where:
- The **objective function** is linear.
- All **constraints** are linear equalities or inequalities.
- Decision variables are typically non-negative.

### Standard Form of an LP Problem

The **standard form** of a maximization LP problem is:

$$\text{Maximize } Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$$

Subject to:
$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}, \quad \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of decision variables
- $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the cost/profit coefficient vector
- $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the constraint coefficient matrix
- $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the resource requirement vector
- $\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$ denotes non-negativity constraints

### Alternative Canonical Forms

| Form Type | Objective | Constraints |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Standard Form | Maximize $Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}, \; \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$ |
| Canonical Form (Max) | Maximize $Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}, \; \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$ |
| Canonical Form (Min) | Minimize $Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{b}, \; \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Rules (KTU Board Exam Favorites):**
> - $\leq$ → add **slack variable** ($s_i \geq 0$)
> - $\geq$ → subtract **surplus variable** ($e_i \geq 0$)
> - $=$ → add **artificial variable** ($a_i \geq 0$)
> - Unrestricted $x_j$ → replace by $x_j' - x_j''$ with $x_j', x_j'' \geq 0$

### Engineering Application Example

A **diet problem** in nutrition: Minimize food cost while meeting nutritional requirements — a classic LP model.
A **production mix problem**: Maximize profit given machine-hour and raw material constraints.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Convexity — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Properties of Convex Functions

For a convex function $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined on a convex set $S$:

1. **Line Segment Property:** The line segment joining any two points on the graph of $f$ lies above or on the graph.
2. **Subgradient Property:** At any point $\mathbf{x}$, there exists a subgradient $\mathbf{g}$ such that:
$$f(\mathbf{y}) \geq f(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{g}^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}) \quad \forall \, \mathbf{y} \in S$$
3. **First-Order Test:** $f$ is convex iff for all $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in S$:
$$f(\mathbf{y}) \geq f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x})$$
4. **Second-Order Test:** If $f$ is twice differentiable, $f$ is convex on $S$ iff the Hessian is **positive semidefinite** for all $\mathbf{x} \in S$:
$$\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \succeq 0 \quad \forall \, \mathbf{x} \in S$$

### 2.1.2 Why Convexity Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Theorem of Convex Optimization:**
> For a **convex function** on a **convex feasible region**, **every local minimum is a global minimum**. This is why algorithms (gradient descent, simplex, interior-point) can guarantee optimality in LP.

### 2.1.3 Hessian Definiteness Test (Sylvester's Criterion)

For a symmetric matrix $\mathbf{H}$, positive definiteness requires **all leading principal minors** to be positive:

| Order | Leading Principal Minor | Condition |
|-------|------------------------|-----------|
| 1st | $H_{11}$ | $> 0$ |
| 2nd | $\det \begin{bmatrix} H_{11} & H_{12} \\ H_{21} & H_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ | $> 0$ |
| $k$-th | $D_k$ | $> 0$ |

---

## 2.2 Global vs. Local Optima — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Conditions Hierarchy

- **FONC (First-Order Necessary):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ → **stationary point** (min, max, or saddle).
- **SONC (Second-Order Necessary):** $\mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} \geq 0$ for all feasible $\mathbf{d}$ → candidate minimum.
- **SOSC (Second-Order Sufficient):** $\mathbf{d}^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{d} \neq \mathbf{0}$ → confirmed strict minimum.

### 2.2.2 Relationship Between Convexity and Optima

| Function Type | Local Min = Global Min? | Hessian Property |
|---------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Strictly Convex | **YES** | $\mathbf{H} \succ 0$ |
| Convex | **YES** | $\mathbf{H} \succeq 0$ |
| Concave | Local Max = Global Max | $\mathbf{H} \preceq 0$ |
| Neither (Non-convex) | **NO** | Indefinite $\mathbf{H}$ |

---

## 2.3 LP Standard Form — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Assumptions of LP (KTU Frequently Tested)

1. **Proportionality:** Contribution of $x_j$ to objective and constraints is proportional to $x_j$.
2. **Additivity:** Total contribution = sum of individual variable contributions.
3. **Certainty:** All parameters $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}$ are known with certainty.
4. **Divisibility (Continuity):** Decision variables can take any real value (integer programming violates this).

### 2.3.2 Solution Characteristics

For an LP in standard form, the feasible region is a **convex polyhedron**, and the optimal solution (if it exists) occurs at a **vertex (extreme point)** of this polyhedron. This is the foundation of the **Simplex Method**.

The basic feasible solution (BFS) corresponds to a vertex. With $m$ equations and $n$ variables, the number of BFS candidates is:

$$\binom{n}{m} = \frac{n!}{m!(n-m)!}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Note:**
> An LP always has an **optimal basic feasible solution** if one exists (Fundamental Theorem of LP).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Use / Condition |
|---|---------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Convex Function | $f(\lambda \mathbf{x}_1 + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{x}_2) \leq \lambda f(\mathbf{x}_1) + (1-\lambda) f(\mathbf{x}_2)$ | $\forall \lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | Concave Function | $f(\lambda \mathbf{x}_1 + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{x}_2) \geq \lambda f(\mathbf{x}_1) + (1-\lambda) f(\mathbf{x}_2)$ | $\forall \lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 3 | FONC (Unconstrained) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary for local optimum |
| 4 | SOSC (Min) | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$ | Sufficient for strict local min |
| 5 | SOSC (Max) | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \prec 0$ | Sufficient for strict local max |
| 6 | Convexity Test (2nd order) | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \succeq 0 \; \forall \mathbf{x} \in S$ | Function is convex |
| 7 | Local Min | $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}) \; \forall \mathbf{x} \in N_{\varepsilon}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ | Within $\varepsilon$-neighborhood |
| 8 | Global Min | $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x}) \; \forall \mathbf{x} \in S$ | Over entire feasible set |
| 9 | Standard LP Form | Max $Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$, s.t. $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}, \; \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}$ | $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, rank$(\mathbf{A}) = m$ |
| 10 | Slack Variable | $\leq \to + s_i$ | $s_i \geq 0$ |
| 11 | Surplus Variable | $\geq \to - e_i$ | $e_i \geq 0$ |
| 12 | Number of BFS | $\binom{n}{m}$ | $n$ vars, $m$ constraints |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Convexity** is exploited in **structural optimization** (minimum weight trusses), **machine learning** (SVM, logistic regression loss is convex), and **optimal control**.
- **Optimality criteria methods (OCM)** use Kuhn-Tucker conditions iteratively to design trusses with displacement/volume constraints.
- **LP** solves real-world problems in **production planning**, **transportation**, **assignment**, **blending**, and **network flow** — every engineering manager uses LP daily.
- **KKT conditions** unify convex optimization and constrained LP theory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Convexity Test for a Function

**Problem:** Test whether $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5$ is convex or concave on $\mathbb{R}$. Also find the global minimum.

### Step 1: Compute the First Derivative

$$f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(x^2 - 4x + 5) = 2x - 4$$

### Step 2: Compute the Second Derivative (Hessian in 1D)

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(2x - 4) = 2$$

### Step 3: Apply the Second-Order Convexity Test

For a univariate function:
- $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x$ → **strictly convex**
- $f''(x) < 0$ for all $x$ → **strictly concave**

Here:
$$f''(x) = 2 > 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$

Therefore, $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5$ is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}$.

### Step 4: Find the Global Minimum Using FONC

Set $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$:
$$2x^* - 4 = 0$$
$$x^* = 2$$

### Step 5: Compute the Minimum Value

$$f(2) = (2)^2 - 4(2) + 5 = 4 - 8 + 5 = 1$$

### Step 6: Verification Using SOSC

Hessian $H = f''(x^*) = 2 > 0$ (positive definite in 1D) → $\mathbf{x}^* = 2$ is a **strict local minimum**, and by strict convexity, it is the **unique global minimum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Global minimum of $f$ is at $\mathbf{x}^* = 2$ with $f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 1$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Convexity Test for a Multivariable Function

**Problem:** Test $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 + 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1 - 8x_2$ for convexity. Find the global minimum.

### Step 1: Compute the Gradient

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 + 2x_2 - 4$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 4x_2 + 2x_1 - 8$$

The gradient vector:
$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 + 2x_2 - 4 \\ 2x_1 + 4x_2 - 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Hessian Matrix

$$H_{11} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} = 2$$
$$H_{12} = H_{21} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} = 2$$
$$H_{22} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} = 4$$

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Apply Sylvester's Criterion for Positive Definiteness

**First leading principal minor:**
$$D_1 = H_{11} = 2 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Second leading principal minor:**
$$D_2 = \det \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = (2)(4) - (2)(2) = 8 - 4 = 4 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Since $D_1 > 0$ and $D_2 > 0$, the Hessian $\mathbf{H}$ is **positive definite**. Therefore, $f(\mathbf{x})$ is **strictly convex**.

### Step 4: Find the Stationary Point

Set $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$2x_1^* + 2x_2^* - 4 = 0 \quad \quad (i)$$
$$2x_1^* + 4x_2^* - 8 = 0 \quad \quad (ii)$$

Subtract (i) from (ii):
$$(2x_1^* + 4x_2^* - 8) - (2x_1^* + 2x_2^* - 4) = 0$$
$$2x_2^* - 4 = 0 \implies x_2^* = 2$$

Substitute into (i):
$$2x_1^* + 2(2) - 4 = 0$$
$$2x_1^* + 4 - 4 = 0$$
$$2x_1^* = 0 \implies x_1^* = 0$$

### Step 5: Compute the Minimum Function Value

$$f(0, 2) = (0)^2 + 2(2)^2 + 2(0)(2) - 4(0) - 8(2)$$
$$= 0 + 8 + 0 - 0 - 16$$
$$= -8$$

### Step 6: Final Verification

By strict convexity, the stationary point $\mathbf{x}^* = (0, 2)$ is the **unique global minimum** with $f(\mathbf{x}^*) = -8$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This problem is the standard form of a quadratic objective, common in **quadratic programming (QP)** and **structural optimization** of trusses with stiffness constraints.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Conversion of LP to Standard Form

**Problem:** Convert the following LP to standard form:

$$\text{Minimize } Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$$
Subject to:
$$x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 10$$
$$3x_1 + x_2 \geq 6$$
$$x_1 + x_2 = 8$$
$$x_1 \geq 0, \; x_2 \text{ unrestricted in sign}$$

### Step 1: Convert Minimization to Maximization

Multiply objective by $-1$:
$$\text{Maximize } Z' = -3x_1 - 5x_2$$

### Step 2: Convert $\leq$ Constraint to $=$ Using Slack Variable

$$x_1 + 2x_2 + s_1 = 10, \quad s_1 \geq 0$$

### Step 3: Convert $\geq$ Constraint to $=$ Using Surplus Variable

$$3x_1 + x_2 - e_1 = 6, \quad e_1 \geq 0$$

### Step 4: Equality Constraint — Add Artificial Variable (for Big-M or Two-Phase)

$$x_1 + x_2 + a_1 = 8, \quad a_1 \geq 0$$

### Step 5: Handle Unrestricted Variable $x_2$

Substitute $x_2 = x_2' - x_2''$, with $x_2' \geq 0, \; x_2'' \geq 0$.

### Step 6: Standard Form Final Statement

$$\text{Maximize } Z' = -3x_1 - 5(x_2' - x_2'')$$
Subject to:
$$x_1 + 2(x_2' - x_2'') + s_1 = 10$$
$$3x_1 + (x_2' - x_2'') - e_1 = 6$$
$$x_1 + (x_2' - x_2'') + a_1 = 8$$
$$x_1, x_2', x_2'', s_1, e_1, a_1 \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always show all conversion steps explicitly. Examiners award 1 mark per correct transformation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — LP Formulation of a Production Problem

**Problem:** A factory produces two products A and B. Product A requires 2 hours of machine time and 1 kg of raw material; yields a profit of ₹30. Product B requires 1 hour of machine time and 3 kg of raw material; yields a profit of ₹20. Available: 100 hours of machine time and 60 kg of raw material. Formulate the LP.

### Step 1: Define Decision Variables

Let $x_1$ = units of Product A, $x_2$ = units of Product B.

### Step 2: Write the Objective Function

$$\text{Maximize } Z = 30x_1 + 20x_2$$

### Step 3: Write the Constraints

Machine time: $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 100$

Raw material: $x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 60$

Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

### Step 4: Standard Form Conversion

Add slack variables $s_1, s_2 \geq 0$:

$$\text{Maximize } Z = 30x_1 + 20x_2 + 0s_1 + 0s_2$$

Subject to:
$$2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 100$$
$$x_1 + 3x_2 + s_2 = 60$$
$$x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2 \geq 0$$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{c} = \begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 20 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Table

| Problem Type | Convexity Test | Optimum Found By | Engineering Analogy |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$, $a>0$ | Strictly Convex | $\nabla f = 0$ | Parabolic potential energy |
| $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{x} + r$ | Convex if $\mathbf{Q} \succeq 0$ | $\nabla f = -2\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{p} = 0$ | Stiffness minimization |
| Linear $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | Both convex & concave | Simplex / Vertex | Profit maximization |
| $f(\mathbf{x}) = -\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x}$ | Strictly Concave | $\nabla f = 0$ | Stability maxima |

## 4.1 Convex vs. Concave Function — Geometric Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A3["Local Min = Global Min"]
        A4["Example: f(x) = x², f(x) = eˣ"]
    end

    subgraph B["CONCAVE FUNCTION f(x)"]
        B1["Definition: f(λx1 + (1-λ)x2) ≥ λf(x1) + (1-λ)f(x2)"]
        B2["Hessian H(x) ⪯ 0 for all x in S"]
        B3["Local Max = Global Max"]
        B4["Example: f(x) = √x, f(x) = ln(x)"]
    end

    subgraph C["LINEAR FUNCTION f(x) = c^T x"]

        C4["Solved by Simplex Method"]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4

    A --- B

```
