| 3 | Directional derivative | $D_d f(x) = \nabla f(x)^T d$ | Scalar |
| 4 | Steepest descent unit dir. | $\hat{d} = -\nabla f / \| \nabla f \|$ | Unit vector |
| 5 | Optimal step (quadratic) | $\alpha_k^{*} = \dfrac{\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k}{\nabla f_k^T Q \nabla f_k}$ | Scalar, $\alpha > 0$ |
| 6 | Stopping criterion | $\| \nabla f(x_k) \| \leq \varepsilon$ | Tolerance |
| 7 | Convergence rate (quadratic) | $r = \dfrac{\kappa(Q) - 1}{\kappa(Q) + 1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Condition number | $\kappa(Q) = \dfrac{\lambda_{\max}}{\lambda_{\min}}$ | $\kappa \geq 1$ |
| 9 | Orthogonality of gradients | $\nabla f(x_{k+1})^T \nabla f(x_k) = 0$ | Under exact line search |
| 10 | Armijo condition | $f(x_k + \alpha_k d_k) \leq f(x_k) + c_1 \alpha_k \nabla f_k^T d_k$ | $0 < c_1 < 1$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gradient-based methods form the computational backbone of:
- **Machine Learning & Deep Learning**: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD), Adam, RMSProp are all descendants of the steepest descent method.
- **Structural Optimization**: Minimum-weight design of trusses and frames.
- **Aerospace Trajectory Optimization**: Fuel-minimizing spacecraft trajectories.
- **Control Systems**: Model Predictive Control (MPC) uses gradient methods at each sampling instant.
- **Signal Processing**: Adaptive filters (LMS algorithm) are essentially steepest descent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $-\nabla f(x)$ is the Steepest Descent Direction

**Setup**: Let $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ be a point and $f$ be differentiable. We seek a unit vector $\hat{d}$ (with $\| \hat{d} \| = 1$) along which $f$ decreases fastest.

**Step 1 — Taylor series expansion** for small $\alpha > 0$:

$$
f(x + \alpha \hat{d}) = f(x) + \alpha \, \nabla f(x)^T \hat{d} + \mathcal{O}(\alpha^2)
$$

For infinitesimal $\alpha$, the linear term dominates, so the function decreases by an amount:

$$
\Delta f = f(x + \alpha \hat{d}) - f(x) \approx \alpha \, \nabla f(x)^T \hat{d}
$$

**Step 2 — To minimize $\Delta f$** (most negative), we must minimize the dot product $\nabla f(x)^T \hat{d}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(x)^T \hat{d} &= \| \nabla f(x) \| \cdot \| \hat{d} \| \cdot \cos\theta \\
&\geq -\| \nabla f(x) \| \cdot \| \hat{d} \| \\
&= -\| \nabla f(x) \|
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between $\nabla f(x)$ and $\hat{d}$. Equality holds **iff** $\cos\theta = -1$, i.e., $\theta = \pi$ — the directions are **anti-parallel**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion**: The minimizer is

$$
\hat{d}^{*} = -\frac{\nabla f(x)}{\| \nabla f(x) \|}
$$

This proves that the unit direction of steepest descent is the negative normalized gradient. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Form Optimal Step Length for Quadratic $f$

**Setup**: Let $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^T Q x - b^T x + c$ with $Q$ symmetric positive-definite. The gradient is:

$$
\nabla f(x) = Qx - b
$$

At point $x_k$ with descent direction $d_k = -\nabla f(x_k)$, the line-search function is:

$$
\phi(\alpha) = f(x_k - \alpha \nabla f(x_k))
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\phi(\alpha)$ with respect to $\alpha$** using the chain rule:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = \nabla f(x_k - \alpha \nabla f_k)^T \cdot (-\nabla f_k) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Express the gradient at the new point** for a quadratic $f$:

$$
\nabla f(x_k - \alpha \nabla f_k) = Q(x_k - \alpha \nabla f_k) - b = (Qx_k - b) - \alpha Q \nabla f_k = \nabla f_k - \alpha Q \nabla f_k
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\big( \nabla f_k - \alpha Q \nabla f_k \big)^T (-\nabla f_k) &= 0 \\
-\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k + \alpha \, \nabla f_k^T Q \nabla f_k &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\alpha$**:

$$
\alpha_k^{*} = \frac{\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k}{\nabla f_k^T Q \nabla f_k}
$$

This is the **Cauchy step length**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Orthogonality of Successive Gradients

**Setup**: Under exact line search, $\phi'(\alpha_k) = 0$, i.e., $\nabla f(x_{k+1})^T d_k = 0$. Since $d_k = -\nabla f(x_k)$:

$$
\nabla f(x_{k+1})^T (-\nabla f(x_k)) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \nabla f(x_{k+1})^T \nabla f(x_k) = 0
$$

Hence successive gradients are **orthogonal** — this geometrically forces the iterates to "zig-zag" toward the minimum, especially for ill-conditioned problems.

---

## 3.4 KTU Standard Worked Example — Full Solution

> **Problem** (Typical KTU Module 2 ESE problem): Use the steepest descent method with exact line search to minimize
> $$
> f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^{2} + 2x_1 x_2 + 5x_2^{2} - 8x_1 - 16x_2
> $$
> starting from $x_0 = (0, 0)^T$. Perform **three iterations** and verify convergence.

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient**:

$$
\nabla f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4x_1 + 2x_2 - 8 \\[4pt] 2x_1 + 10x_2 - 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Hence the Hessian is:

$$
Q = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 10 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 1 (at $x_0 = (0,0)$)**:

$$
\nabla f_0 = \begin{bmatrix} -8 \\ -16 \end{bmatrix}, \quad d_0 = -\nabla f_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute step length using the Cauchy formula:

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{\nabla f_0^T \nabla f_0}{\nabla f_0^T Q \nabla f_0}
$$

Numerator:

$$
\nabla f_0^T \nabla f_0 = (-8)^2 + (-16)^2 = 64 + 256 = 320
$$

Denominator:

$$
Q \nabla f_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -8 \\ -16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -32 - 32 \\ -16 - 160 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -64 \\ -176 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\nabla f_0^T Q \nabla f_0 = (-8)(-64) + (-16)(-176) = 512 + 2816 = 3328
$$

Therefore:

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{320}{3328} = \frac{5}{52} \approx 0.0962
$$

Update:

$$
x_1 = x_0 + \alpha_0 d_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{5}{52} \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 40/52 \\ 80/52 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/13 \\ 20/13 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.7692 \\ 1.5385 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 2 (at $x_1 = (10/13, 20/13)$)**:

$$
\nabla f_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4(10/13) + 2(20/13) - 8 \\ 2(10/13) + 10(20/13) - 16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (40 + 40 - 104)/13 \\ (20 + 200 - 208)/13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -24/13 \\ 12/13 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
d_1 = -\nabla f_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 24/13 \\ -12/13 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute step length:

$$
\nabla f_1^T \nabla f_1 = (24/13)^2 + (12/13)^2 = \frac{576 + 144}{169} = \frac{720}{169}
$$

$$
Q \nabla f_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -24/13 \\ 12/13 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{13} \begin{bmatrix} -96 + 24 \\ -48 + 120 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -72/13 \\ 72/13 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\nabla f_1^T Q \nabla f_1 = \frac{(-24/13)(-72/13) + (12/13)(72/13)}{1} = \frac{1728 + 864}{169} = \frac{2592}{169}
$$

$$
\alpha_1 = \frac{720/169}{2592/169} = \frac{720}{2592} = \frac{5}{18} \approx 0.2778
$$

Update:

$$
x_2 = x_1 + \alpha_1 d_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 10/13 \\ 20/13 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{5}{18} \begin{bmatrix} 24/13 \\ -12/13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/13 + 120/234 \\ 20/13 - 60/234 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 50/39 \\ 50/39 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.2821 \\ 1.2821 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Iteration 3 (at $x_2 = (50/39, 50/39)$)**:

$$
\nabla f_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 4(50/39) + 2(50/39) - 8 \\ 2(50/39) + 10(50/39) - 16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (200 + 100 - 312)/39 \\ (100 + 500 - 624)/39 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -12/39 \\ -24/39 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4/13 \\ -8/13 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\| \nabla f_2 \| = \sqrt{(4/13)^2 + (8/13)^2} = \frac{\sqrt{80}}{13} \approx 0.686
$$

Since $\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$ is the typical tolerance, one more iteration is needed; the iterates are visibly converging toward the true optimum $(4/3, 4/3)$.

**Step 5 — Verify the true optimum** by setting $\nabla f = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4x_1 + 2x_2 &= 8 \\
2x_1 + 10x_2 &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving: $x_1 = x_2 = \dfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.3333$, and $f(4/3, 4/3) = -\dfrac{40}{3} \approx -13.333$ is the minimum value.

The sequence $x_0 \to x_1 \to x_2$ is approaching $(4/3, 4/3)$ — convergence is verified.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Steepest Descent Algorithm

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the entire iterative logic of the gradient-based method, including the inner line-search sub-routine and the gradient orthogonality check.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Initialize k = 0, choose x0 and tolerance epsilon]
    B --> C[Compute gradient gk = grad f xk]
    C --> D{Is norm gk less than or equal to epsilon?}
    D -- YES --> E([OUTPUT x star = xk, STOP])
    D -- NO --> F[Set search direction dk = minus gk]
    F --> G[Line Search Subroutine: minimize phi alpha equals f xk plus alpha dk over alpha greater than 0]
    G --> H[Optimal step length alpha star satisfies grad f xk plus alpha star dk transpose dk = 0]
    H --> I[Update xk plus 1 = xk plus alpha star dk]
    I --> J[Increment k = k plus 1]
    J --> C
    E --> K([END])
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Vector Geometry of One Steepest Descent Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Current Point xk] --> P2[Compute gradient gk at xk]
    P2 --> P3[Direction dk = minus gk]
    P3 --> P4[Move along dk by step alpha k]
    P4 --> P5[New Point xk plus 1]
    P5 --> P6[Verify orthogonality: gk plus 1 dot gk equals 0]
    P6 --> P7[Converged when norm gk plus 1 less than epsilon]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Convergence Rate vs. Condition Number

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Quadratic f of x with Hessian Q] --> S2[Compute eigenvalues lambda max and lambda min of Q]
    S2 --> S3[Condition number kappa = lambda max over lambda min]
    S3 --> S4{Is kappa close to 1?}
    S4 -- YES --> S5[FAST CONVERGENCE: nearly spherical level sets]
    S4 -- NO --> S6[SLOW ZIG ZAG CONVERGENCE: elongated elliptical level sets]
    S5 --> S7[Rate r = kappa minus 1 over kappa plus 1 is small]
    S6 --> S8[Rate r is close to 1, many iterations needed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The reason steepest descent zig-zags on ill-conditioned problems is geometric — the level sets of $f$ are elongated ellipses. Each gradient step is perpendicular to the current level set, so successive steps bounce off the walls of the "valley" instead of going straight to the bottom. A large condition number $\kappa$ amplifies this bouncing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the steepest descent method for unconstrained optimization. State the direction vector used in this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The steepest descent (or gradient descent) method is an iterative first-order algorithm for finding the minimum of a differentiable unconstrained function $f(x)$. Starting from an initial guess $x_0$, the iterates are updated as:

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \alpha_k \, d_k
$$

The direction vector is the **negative gradient**:

$$
d_k = -\nabla f(x_k)
$$

The scalar step length $\alpha_k > 0$ is determined by exact or inexact line search to ensure descent. The algorithm terminates when $\| \nabla f(x_k) \| \leq \varepsilon$. (1 mark — definition, 1 mark — iteration, 1 mark — direction and stopping rule)

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Cauchy step length formula for a quadratic function. Why does the steepest descent method show slow convergence for ill-conditioned problems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For the quadratic $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^T Q x - b^T x + c$ with symmetric positive-definite $Q$:

$$
\alpha_k^{*} = \frac{\nabla f(x_k)^T \, \nabla f(x_k)}{\nabla f(x_k)^T \, Q \, \nabla f(x_k)}
$$

(1 mark) The method converges slowly when the Hessian $Q$ is **ill-conditioned** (large condition number $\kappa$). (1 mark) The asymptotic linear convergence rate is $r = (\kappa - 1)/(\kappa + 1)$, which approaches 1 as $\kappa \to \infty$, meaning the iterates make negligible progress per step — producing the characteristic **zig-zag path** along elongated elliptical level sets. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Convention**: Part B of Module 2 carries 14 marks. Students answer either **Question A** or **Question B** in full.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Prove that the negative gradient direction $-\nabla f(x)$ is the direction of steepest descent of a differentiable function $f$ at a point $x$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — Let $\hat{d}$ be any unit vector ($\| \hat{d} \| = 1$). The directional derivative in direction $\hat{d}$ is, by the definition of the gradient:

$$
D_{\hat{d}} f(x) = \nabla f(x)^T \hat{d}
$$

This represents the instantaneous rate of change of $f$ along $\hat{d}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — To make $f$ decrease as rapidly as possible, we must choose $\hat{d}$ that minimizes the dot product $\nabla f(x)^T \hat{d}$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, for any two vectors $u, v$:

$$
|u^T v| \leq \| u \| \, \| v \| \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; u^T v \geq -\| u \| \, \| v \|
$$

Apply with $u = \nabla f(x)$ and $v = \hat{d}$:

$$
\nabla f(x)^T \hat{d} \geq -\|\nabla f(x)\| \cdot \|\hat{d}\| = -\|\nabla f(x)\|
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Equality holds in Cauchy–Schwarz **iff** $\hat{d}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla f(x)$, i.e.:

$$
\hat{d} = -\frac{\nabla f(x)}{\| \nabla f(x) \|}
$$

The minimum value of the directional derivative is $-\| \nabla f(x) \|$, attained only at this unit vector. Hence this is the unique direction of *steepest descent*.

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — In the steepest descent method, the normalizing factor is absorbed into the step length $\alpha_k$, so the practical search direction is simply $d_k = -\nabla f(x_k)$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^{2} + 2x_1 x_2 + 5x_2^{2} - 8x_1 - 16x_2$ using the steepest descent method with exact line search. Start from $x_0 = (0, 0)^T$ and carry out two complete iterations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1 (3.5 marks)** — Gradient and direction:

$$
\nabla f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 4x_1 + 2x_2 - 8 \\ 2x_1 + 10x_2 - 16 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

At $x_0 = (0, 0)^T$:

$$
\nabla f_0 = \begin{bmatrix} -8 \\ -16 \end{bmatrix}, \quad d_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step length:

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{(-8)^2 + (-16)^2}{(-8, -16) \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -8 \\ -16 \end{bmatrix}} = \frac{320}{3328} = \frac{5}{52} \approx 0.0962
$$

Update:

$$
x_1 = \frac{5}{52} \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10/13 \\ 20/13 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.7692 \\ 1.5385 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration 2 (3.5 marks)** — New gradient:

$$
\nabla f_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4(10/13) + 2(20/13) - 8 \\ 2(10/13) + 10(20/13) - 16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -24/13 \\ 12/13 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
d_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 24/13 \\ -12/13 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step length:

$$
\alpha_1 = \frac{(24/13)^2 + (12/13)^2}{(24/13, -12/13) \, Q \, (24/13, -12/13)^T} = \frac{720/169}{2592/169} = \frac{5}{18} \approx 0.2778
$$

Update:

$$
x_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 10/13 \\ 20/13 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{5}{18} \begin{bmatrix} 24/13 \\ -12/13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 50/39 \\ 50/39 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.2821 \\ 1.2821 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The true optimum is $(4/3, 4/3) \approx (1.3333, 1.3333)$ — the iterates are clearly converging. $\blacksquare$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Cauchy optimal step length formula for the steepest descent method applied to the quadratic function $f(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^T Q x - b^T x + c$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — Gradient of the quadratic:

$$
\nabla f(x) = Qx - b
$$

The gradient at a general point is $Q$ times $x$ minus $b$. For the steepest descent iteration:

$$
d_k = -\nabla f(x_k) = -(Qx_k - b)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Form the one-dimensional line-search function $\phi(\alpha) = f(x_k + \alpha d_k)$ and differentiate:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = \nabla f(x_k + \alpha d_k)^T d_k
$$

Setting $\phi'(\alpha) = 0$:

$$
\nabla f(x_k + \alpha d_k)^T d_k = 0
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Using the gradient formula for the new point:

$$
\nabla f(x_k + \alpha d_k) = Q(x_k + \alpha d_k) - b = (Qx_k - b) + \alpha Q d_k = \nabla f_k + \alpha Q d_k
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — Substituting and using $d_k = -\nabla f_k$:

$$
(\nabla f_k - \alpha Q \nabla f_k)^T (-\nabla f_k) = 0
$$

$$
-\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k + \alpha \, \nabla f_k^T Q \nabla f_k = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha_k^{*} = \frac{\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k}{\nabla f_k^T Q \nabla f_k}\;}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Use the steepest descent method to minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^{2} + 5 x_2^{2}$ starting from $x_0 = (10, 2)^T$. Compute $\kappa(Q)$, the convergence rate, and discuss the convergence behaviour.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Identify $Q$:

$$
Q = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark)** — Eigenvalues and condition number:

$$
\lambda_{\min} = 2, \quad \lambda_{\max} = 10, \quad \kappa(Q) = \frac{10}{2} = 5
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)** — Convergence rate:

$$
r = \frac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks)** — Iteration 1 at $x_0 = (10, 2)^T$:

$$
\nabla f_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix}, \quad d_0 = \begin{bmatrix} -20 \\ -20 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{400 + 400}{(20, 20) Q (20, 20)^T} = \frac{800}{20^2 \cdot 2 + 20^2 \cdot 10} = \frac{800}{4800} = \frac{1}{6}
$$

$$
x_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{6} \begin{bmatrix} -20 \\ -20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20/3 \\ -4/3 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 6.667 \\ -1.333 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks)** — Iteration 2 at $x_1 = (20/3, -4/3)^T$:

$$
\nabla f_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 40/3 \\ -40/3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad d_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -40/3 \\ 40/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\alpha_1 = \frac{(40/3)^2 + (40/3)^2}{(40/3, -40/3) \, Q \, (40/3, -40/3)^T} = \frac{3200/9}{64000/9} = \frac{1}{20}
$$

$$
x_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 20/3 \\ -4/3 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{20} \begin{bmatrix} -40/3 \\ 40/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20/3 - 2/3 \\ -4/3 + 2/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18/3 \\ -2/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ -2/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Discussion (built into the steps):** The iterates oscillate across the $x_1$-axis — the zig-zag pattern characteristic of an ill-conditioned problem with $\kappa = 5$ and elongated elliptical level sets. Although the function value decreases monotonically, the iterates take many steps to approach the optimum at the origin. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute the denominator $\nabla f_k^T Q \nabla f_k$ correctly** in the Cauchy step. Many students skip the $Q$ multiplication — this is the most common 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing up $\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k$ with $\| \nabla f_k \|^2$ notation mid-calculation** — both are correct, but consistency matters. KTU evaluators look for clean notation.
> 3. **Not showing the orthogonality check** $\nabla f_{k+1}^T \nabla f_k = 0$ after the first iteration. This 1-mark step is frequently asked as a verification.
> 4. **Skipping the gradient derivation** at the start of a worked problem. Even in 7-mark sub-parts, the gradient should be written explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting the Hessian identification** for quadratic problems. Always extract $Q$ and $b$ explicitly.
> 6. **Reporting step length as negative**: $\alpha_k$ must always be strictly positive for a descent direction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Gradient-Based Method (Module 2)**

- **Algorithm skeleton**: $x_{k+1} = x_k + \alpha_k d_k$ with $d_k = -\nabla f(x_k)$.
- **Cauchy step (quadratic)**: $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\nabla f_k^T \nabla f_k}{\nabla f_k^T Q \nabla f_k}$.
- **Stop when**: $\| \nabla f(x_k) \| \leq \varepsilon$ (typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$).
- **First-order method**: uses only $\nabla f$ — no Hessian needed. Cheaper per iteration than Newton's method.
- **Convergence rate** on strongly convex quadratic: linear, with ratio $r = \dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}$.
- **Orthogonality property**: $\nabla f(x_{k+1})^T \nabla f(x_k) = 0$ under exact line search.
- **Zig-zag path** appears when $\kappa(Q) \gg 1$ — the level sets are elongated ellipses.
- **Spherical case** ($Q = cI$): converges in **one step** from any starting point.
- **Comparison with Newton's method**: Newton's method has quadratic convergence but requires Hessian inversion; steepest descent is more robust globally but slower near the minimum.
- **Armijo backtracking** is the standard inexact line search used in practice.
- **Common pitfalls**: forgetting $Q$ in the denominator, omitting the orthogonality check, negative step length, not showing the gradient computation explicitly.
- **Engineering applications**: machine learning (SGD), adaptive filters (LMS), MPC, structural optimization, trajectory planning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton's method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT413 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Newton's method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Newton's Method** (also called the **Newton-Raphson Method** in the context of root-finding, and the **Newtonian Optimization Algorithm** in the context of unconstrained optimization) is a second-order, iterative, descent-type numerical optimization technique used to locate the local minimum (or maximum) of a scalar-valued, differentiable objective function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$.

The method leverages the **second-order Taylor series expansion** of the objective function about the current iterate $\mathbf{x}_k$ and replaces the function with its **quadratic approximation**. The minimum of this quadratic surrogate is then accepted as the next iterate, which yields the canonical update rule:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - [\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}_k)]^{-1} \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is the **gradient vector** (vector of first-order partial derivatives) and $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is the **Hessian matrix** (matrix of second-order partial derivatives), both evaluated at the current point $\mathbf{x}_k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PERAT413, Module 2):**
> Newton's method is classified under the family of **Cauchy's methods of descent** as a *modified second-order gradient method* in which the direction of search is not the steepest descent direction ($-\nabla f$), but the direction governed by the inverse Hessian. It belongs to the broad class of **variable metric (quasi-Newton) precursors** and provides the theoretical foundation for more advanced algorithms such as the Davidon-Fletcher-Powell (DFP) and BFGS methods covered in later modules.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **blindfolded on a hilly, undulating terrain** and asked to find the lowest valley. You are allowed to feel the ground beneath your feet to estimate:
1. The **slope** (gradient) — which tells you which way is downhill.
2. The **curvature** of the terrain (Hessian) — which tells you how steep and how rapidly the slope is changing.

A simple *steepest descent* walker would only feel the slope and take a small step in that direction — slow, zigzag, and cautious. A *Newton's method* walker, in contrast, is mathematically "clairvoyant": by feeling both the slope **and** the curvature, the walker can **predict** the shape of the entire hill in the immediate vicinity and jump **directly** to the predicted valley floor in a single, optimally-sized step.

> **Engineering Intuition:** In structural design, if you are computing the optimal cross-section of a beam to minimize weight subject to a deflection constraint, the gradient alone (Cauchy's method) zigzags. Newton's method, by accounting for the second derivative (curvature), converges in very few iterations — often in a single step for purely quadratic problems.

## 1.3 When Is Newton's Method Preferred?

- When the objective function is **twice continuously differentiable** ($C^2$).
- When the Hessian is **cheap to compute and easy to invert** (low-dimensional problems, $n \le 100$ typically).
- When **high accuracy** in few iterations is required (Newton's method exhibits **quadratic convergence** near the optimum).
- When the function is **nearly quadratic** in the region of interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (KTU 2024 — CO Mapping):**
> - **CO2:** *Apply numerical optimization techniques to solve engineering problems.* ✓ Newton's method is a direct application.
> - **CO3:** *Analyze convergence behaviour and computational efficiency of iterative methods.* ✓ Quadratic vs. linear convergence is a key analytical discussion.
> - **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4) → Evaluate (L5).

## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Significance

Newton's method was first described by **Isaac Newton in 1669** in *De Analysi per Aequationes Infinitas* and later refined by **Joseph Raphson in 1690** for polynomial root-finding. In modern engineering, it is the workhorse behind:
- **Nonlinear Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** solvers (e.g., Abaqus, ANSYS Newton-Raphson solvers).
- **Process optimization** in chemical engineering (reactor design, distillation columns).
- **Optimal control** and trajectory optimization in aerospace (minimum-fuel orbital transfers).
- **Machine learning** second-order optimizers (e.g., classical Newton used as a baseline for natural gradient methods).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The algorithm operates on a rigorous, repeatable 7-step logical framework:

1. **Initialization:** Choose an initial guess $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^n$. Set iteration counter $k = 0$. Choose a stopping tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$ (typically $10^{-6}$).

2. **Gradient Evaluation:** Compute the gradient vector $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

3. **Hessian Evaluation:** Compute the Hessian matrix $\mathbf{H}_k = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.

4. **Search Direction Computation:** Solve the **Newton linear system** for the search direction $\mathbf{d}_k$:
   $$\mathbf{H}_k \, \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$$

5. **Step Size Determination:** Newton's method inherently uses a **unit step length** ($\alpha_k = 1$), since the quadratic model is minimized exactly at $\mathbf{d}_k$. This is in contrast to steepest descent where a line search is mandatory.

6. **Update:** Generate the next iterate:
   $$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$$

7. **Termination Check:** If $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1})\| \le \varepsilon$ or $\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k\| \le \varepsilon$, **stop** and report $\mathbf{x}^* \approx \mathbf{x}_{k+1}$. Otherwise, set $k \leftarrow k+1$ and return to Step 2.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Mathematical Justification

The method emerges naturally from a **second-order Taylor series expansion** of $f$ about $\mathbf{x}_k$:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{p}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{p} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p} \equiv \hat{f}_k(\mathbf{p})$$

This approximation $\hat{f}_k(\mathbf{p})$ is a **quadratic function** of the perturbation $\mathbf{p}$. The unique minimizer of an unconstrained quadratic is found by setting its gradient to zero:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{p}} \hat{f}_k(\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{g}_k + \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving for $\mathbf{p}^*$ yields $\mathbf{p}^* = -\mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$, which is exactly the Newton step. Hence, the method is sometimes called the **"Iterated Quadratic Approximation"** method.

## 2.3 Convergence Properties

| Property | Value | Engineering Implication |
|----------|-------|-------------------------|
| **Order of Convergence** | **Quadratic** ($p = 2$) | Number of correct digits approximately **doubles** per iteration near $\mathbf{x}^*$ |
| **Asymptotic Error Constant** | $\mu = \frac{\|f'''(\mathbf{x}^*)\|}{2 \vert f''(\mathbf{x}^*) \vert}$ (scalar case) | Governs the radius of the *basin of quadratic convergence* |
| **Local Convergence Condition** | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ must be **positive definite** | Guarantees $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a strict local minimum |
| **Global Convergence** | **Not guaranteed** without modifications | Plain Newton can diverge for poor $\mathbf{x}_0$ |
| **Rate Function** | $\lim_{k\to\infty} \frac{\|e_{k+1}\|}{\|e_k\|^2} = \mu$ | The hallmark signature of quadratic convergence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scalar Form Convergence:** For $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, the iteration simplifies to:
> $$x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$
> which is the form most often tested in KTU Part A questions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name / Significance | Units (if applicable) |
|---|---------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$ | Vector Newton update | Dimensionless / problem-dependent |
| 2 | $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$ | Scalar Newton update | Problem-dependent |
| 3 | $\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ | Newton linear system (often solved via Cholesky factorization) | Linear system |
| 4 | $\hat{f}_k(\mathbf{p}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{p} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p}$ | Quadratic model at $\mathbf{x}_k$ | Objective value |
| 5 | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$ | Newton direction (also called the *Newton step*) | Search direction |
| 6 | $\alpha_k = 1$ | Canonical step length | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\lim_{k \to \infty} \dfrac{\Vert e_{k+1} \Vert}{\Vert e_k \Vert^2} = \mu$ | Quadratic convergence definition | Convergence metric |
| 8 | $\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert \le \varepsilon$ | Termination criterion (gradient norm) | Tolerance |
| 9 | $\Vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert \le \varepsilon$ | Termination criterion (step norm) | Tolerance |
| 10 | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0},\; \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \succ 0$ | Second-order **sufficiency conditions** for local minimum | Algebraic condition |

## 2.5 Comparison with Steepest Descent (Cauchy's Method)

| Feature | Steepest Descent (Cauchy) | Newton's Method |
|---------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| Order of convergence | **Linear** | **Quadratic** |
| Information used | Gradient only ($\nabla f$) | Gradient + Hessian ($\nabla f, \nabla^2 f$) |
| Step length | Requires **line search** | **Unit step** ($\alpha = 1$) |
| Direction | $-\mathbf{g}_k$ (locally steepest) | $-\mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$ (scaled steepest) |
| Computational cost / iter | $O(n)$ | $O(n^3)$ (Hessian inversion) |
| Robustness | High | Moderate (needs good $\mathbf{x}_0$, PD Hessian) |
| Best for | Ill-conditioned, large-scale problems | Low-to-moderate $n$, near-quadratic regions |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **process engineering**, the optimization of a chemical reactor's operating temperature and pressure to maximize yield is often a non-quadratic problem. While the first few iterations of Newton's method may behave erratically (as it approaches from a poor starting guess), the moment it enters the *region of quadratic convergence* (within a small neighbourhood of $\mathbf{x}^*$), it converges in **3–5 iterations** to engineering tolerance ($10^{-6}$). This makes it the method of choice in **real-time optimization** of industrial process control loops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Newton Update Rule from First Principles

We begin with the **second-order Taylor series expansion** of a scalar function $f(x)$ about the current iterate $x_k$. This is a local polynomial approximation of $f$ in the neighbourhood of $x_k$:

$$f(x) = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x - x_k) + \frac{1}{2}f''(x_k)(x - x_k)^2 + \frac{1}{6}f'''(\xi)(x - x_k)^3$$

where $\xi$ lies between $x$ and $x_k$. For points sufficiently close to $x_k$, the cubic and higher-order remainder terms become negligible. The resulting **quadratic surrogate** (truncated Taylor model) is:

$$\hat{f}(x) = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)(x - x_k) + \frac{1}{2}f''(x_k)(x - x_k)^2$$

This approximation is valid provided $f \in C^3$ and $|x - x_k|$ is small.

**Step 1 — Substitute the candidate step:** Let the next iterate be $x_{k+1} = x_k + p$, where $p$ is the unknown step to be determined. Substituting $x = x_k + p$ into the surrogate model yields:

$$\hat{f}(x_k + p) = f(x_k) + f'(x_k)\,p + \frac{1}{2}f''(x_k)\,p^2$$

**Step 2 — Necessary condition for the minimum of the surrogate:** To minimize $\hat{f}$ with respect to $p$, take the derivative and set it to zero (first-order necessary condition for an unconstrained minimum):

$$\frac{d\hat{f}}{dp} = f'(x_k) + f''(x_k)\,p = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for the optimal step $p^*$:** Assuming $f''(x_k) \neq 0$ (non-degenerate curvature), we obtain:

$$p^* = -\frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

**Step 4 — Form the next iterate:** Substituting $p^*$ back into the iteration formula:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + p^* = x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

This is the **scalar Newton update rule**. The full multivariate form follows identically by replacing first derivatives with gradient components and second derivatives with Hessian entries, which we present next.

## 3.2 Multivariate Derivation

For $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, the second-order Taylor expansion in vector form is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{p}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{p} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p}$$

where $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ and $\mathbf{H}_k = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. Setting the gradient of this quadratic (w.r.t. $\mathbf{p}$) to zero:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{p}}\left[f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{p} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p}\right] = \mathbf{g}_k + \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving the resulting linear system (assuming $\mathbf{H}_k$ is non-singular):

$$\mathbf{p}^* = -\mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Scalar Function (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 6$ using Newton's method, starting from $x_0 = 1$, with $\varepsilon = 0.01$.

**Step A — Compute the derivatives analytically:**

$$f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2$$
$$f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x$$

**Step B — Iteration 0 (k = 0):**

Evaluate at $x_0 = 1$:

$$f'(1) = 4(1)^3 - 12(1)^2 = 4 - 12 = -8$$
$$f''(1) = 12(1)^2 - 24(1) = 12 - 24 = -12$$

Apply Newton update:

$$x_1 = 1 - \frac{-8}{-12} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Step C — Iteration 1 (k = 1):** Evaluate at $x_1 = \frac{1}{3}$:

$$f'\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) = 4\left(\tfrac{1}{27}\right) - 12\left(\tfrac{1}{9}\right) = \tfrac{4}{27} - \tfrac{12}{9} = \tfrac{4}{27} - \tfrac{36}{27} = -\tfrac{32}{27}$$
$$f''\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) = 12\left(\tfrac{1}{9}\right) - 24\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right) = \tfrac{12}{9} - \tfrac{24}{3} = \tfrac{4}{3} - 8 = -\tfrac{20}{3}$$

Apply update:

$$x_2 = \tfrac{1}{3} - \dfrac{-\tfrac{32}{27}}{-\tfrac{20}{3}} = \tfrac{1}{3} - \left(\tfrac{32}{27} \cdot \tfrac{3}{20}\right) = \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{96}{540} = \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{8}{45}$$

Convert to common denominator:

$$x_2 = \tfrac{15}{45} - \tfrac{8}{45} = \tfrac{7}{45} \approx 0.1556$$

**Step D — Iteration 2 (k = 2):** Evaluate at $x_2 = \frac{7}{45}$:

$$f'\left(\tfrac{7}{45}\right) = 4\left(\tfrac{343}{91125}\right) - 12\left(\tfrac{49}{2025}\right) = \tfrac{1372}{91125} - \tfrac{588}{2025}$$

Common denominator: convert second term to $91125$: $\frac{588}{2025} = \frac{588 \times 45}{91125} = \frac{26460}{91125}$. So:

$$f'(x_2) = \tfrac{1372 - 26460}{91125} = \tfrac{-25088}{91125} \approx -0.2753$$

$$f''\left(\tfrac{7}{45}\right) = 12\left(\tfrac{49}{2025}\right) - 24\left(\tfrac{7}{45}\right) = \tfrac{588}{2025} - \tfrac{168}{45} = \tfrac{588}{2025} - \tfrac{7560}{2025} = \tfrac{-6972}{2025} \approx -3.443$$

Apply update:

$$x_3 = \tfrac{7}{45} - \dfrac{-0.2753}{-3.443} = \tfrac{7}{45} - 0.07996 = 0.1556 - 0.07996 \approx 0.0756$$

**Step E — Termination Check:** The true minimum of $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 6$ is at $x^* = 0$ (since $f'(x) = 4x^2(x-3) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$ or $x = 3$, and $f''(0) = 0$ is a saddle, while $f'(0) = 0, f''(0) = 0$ — actually minimum is at $x = 3$ where $f''(3) = 36 > 0$).

Note: For this function, the minimum is at $x^* = 3$ with $f(3) = -21$. The iterations above are converging toward the **saddle point** $x = 0$ from above, illustrating that **Newton's method finds stationary points**, not always minima — the user must verify the second-order sufficiency condition $f''(x^*) > 0$ at the converged point.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Bivariate Vector Function

**Problem:** Minimize $f(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1 - 2)^4 + (x_1 - 2x_2)^2$ starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$.

**Step A — Compute gradient and Hessian:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 4(x_1 - 2)^3 + 2(x_1 - 2x_2) = \mathbf{g}_1$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -4(x_1 - 2x_2) = \mathbf{g}_2$$

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 12(x_1-2)^2 + 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step B — Iteration 0:** At $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0,0)^T$:

$$\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 4(-2)^3 + 2(0) \\ -4(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -32 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{H}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 12(4) + 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & -4 \\ -4 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step C — Compute $\mathbf{H}_0^{-1}$:** Determinant $= (50)(8) - (-4)(-4) = 400 - 16 = 384$. Inverse:

$$\mathbf{H}_0^{-1} = \frac{1}{384}\begin{bmatrix} 8 & 4 \\ 4 & 50 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.02083 & 0.01042 \\ 0.01042 & 0.13021 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step D — Compute Newton direction:**

$$\mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{H}_0^{-1}\mathbf{g}_0 = -\begin{bmatrix} 0.02083 & 0.01042 \\ 0.01042 & 0.13021 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -32 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} -0.6667 \\ -0.3333 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6667 \\ 0.3333 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step E — Update:**

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.6667 \\ 0.3333 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6667 \\ 0.3333 \end{bmatrix}$$

This single Newton step brings us from the origin to a point already very close to the true minimum $\mathbf{x}^* = (2, 1)^T$ — illustrating the **rapid convergence** of Newton's method in well-behaved regions.

## 3.5 Convergence Rate Derivation (Scalar Case)

Let $e_k = x_k - x^*$ denote the error at iteration $k$. Substituting $x_k = x^* + e_k$ into the Newton update and expanding $f'(x_k)$ and $f''(x_k)$ in Taylor series about $x^*$:

$$f'(x_k) = f''(x^*) e_k + \frac{1}{2}f'''(x^*) e_k^2 + O(e_k^3)$$
$$f''(x_k) = f''(x^*) + f'''(x^*) e_k + O(e_k^2)$$

Substituting these into $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$:

$$x_{k+1} = (x^* + e_k) - \frac{f''(x^*) e_k + \frac{1}{2}f'''(x^*) e_k^2 + \cdots}{f''(x^*) + f'''(x^*) e_k + \cdots}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $f''(x^*)$ and simplifying:

$$e_{k+1} = e_k - e_k \cdot \frac{1 + \frac{f'''(x^*)}{2f''(x^*)} e_k + \cdots}{1 + \frac{f'''(x^*)}{f''(x^*)} e_k + \cdots}$$

Applying the geometric series expansion $(1+u)^{-1} \approx 1 - u$ for small $u$:

$$e_{k+1} \approx \frac{f'''(x^*)}{2 f''(x^*)} e_k^2$$

Hence the **error recurrence** is:

$$\boxed{\vert e_{k+1} \vert \approx \mu \cdot \vert e_k \vert^2, \quad \text{where } \mu = \frac{\vert f'''(x^*) \vert}{2 \vert f''(x^*) \vert}}$$

This confirms the **quadratic convergence** of Newton's method in a small neighbourhood of the optimum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of Newton's Method (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START]) --> A1[Initialize: x0, tolerance eps, k=0]
    A1 --> A2[Evaluate gradient gk at xk]
    A2 --> A3[Evaluate Hessian Hk at xk]
    A3 --> A4{Is Hk positive definite?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Apply Hessian modification: Bk = Hk + delta*I]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Newton direction d = -Bk^-1 gk]
    A4 -- Yes --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Set step size alpha = 1]
    A7 --> A8[Update: x_new = xk + alpha*d]
    A8 --> A9{Convergence check: norm gk less than eps OR norm x_new - xk less than eps}
    A9 -- No --> A10[k = k+1]
    A10 --> A2
    A9 -- Yes --> A11[Report optimum x* = x_new]
    A11 --> A12([END])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Newton's Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Problem Definition f of x] --> P1[Input Module: x0 and tolerance]
    P1 --> P2[Derivative Engine: gradient and Hessian]
    P2 --> P3[Linear Algebra Solver: Newton linear system H d equals minus g]
    P3 --> P4[Step Generator: unit step size alpha = 1]
    P4 --> P5[Update Module: x_new equals x_old plus d]
    P5 --> P6[Convergence Analyzer]
    P6 --> P7{Converged?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Output Module: optimum x star and f of x star]
    P7 -- No --> P2
```

## 4.3 Comparative Convergence Behaviour — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0([Compare descent methods]) --> C1[Steepest Descent]
    C0 --> C2[Newton Method]
    C1 --> C3[Linear convergence: zig-zag path]
    C2 --> C4[Quadratic convergence: direct path to minimum]
    C3 --> C5[Cost per iteration: O of n]
    C4 --> C6[Cost per iteration: O of n cubed]
    C5 --> C7[Best for: large scale and ill conditioned]
    C6 --> C8[Best for: small to moderate n and smooth f]
```

## 4.4 Convergence Regime Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0([Newton convergence behaviour]) --> R1[Initial guess x0]
    R1 --> R2{Is x0 in basin of attraction?}
    R2 -- No --> R3[Divergence or oscillation]
    R2 -- Yes --> R4[Linear phase: gradual approach]
    R4 --> R5[Quadratic phase: error squared per step]
    R5 --> R6[Convergence to machine precision]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Newton-Raphson update formula for minimizing a scalar function $f(x)$. Why is the step size taken as unity in Newton's method? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Newton-Raphson update formula for minimizing a scalar, twice-differentiable function $f(x)$ is:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

The step size $\alpha_k = 1$ is chosen **by construction**, not by line search. The second-order Taylor surrogate $f(x_k) + f'(x_k)p + \frac{1}{2}f''(x_k)p^2$ is an exact quadratic, and its unique unconstrained minimizer is reached precisely at $p^* = -f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$. Hence, the Newton step already lands at the minimum of the local quadratic model, making a line search both unnecessary and counter-productive.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the order of convergence of Newton's method? Define the asymptotic error constant. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The order of convergence of Newton's method is **quadratic** ($p = 2$). This means the error at successive iterations satisfies:

$$\lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{k+1} \vert}{\vert e_k \vert^2} = \mu \neq 0$$

where $\mu$ is the **asymptotic error constant**, defined for the scalar case as:

$$\mu = \frac{\vert f'''(x^*) \vert}{2 \vert f''(x^*) \vert}$$

Practically, this means that the number of correct digits in the iterate **approximately doubles** with every iteration once the iterates enter the basin of quadratic convergence (i.e., once $\mathbf{x}_k$ is sufficiently close to $\mathbf{x}^*$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Newton update rule for minimizing a multivariable objective function $f(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, starting from the second-order Taylor series expansion. Clearly state the assumptions required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using Newton's method, find the minimum of the function $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ starting from $x_0 = 0$. Perform a minimum of **3 iterations** and verify whether the converged point is a minimum, maximum, or saddle. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. $f$ is at least twice continuously differentiable ($f \in C^2$).
2. The Hessian $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is non-singular (invertible) at each iterate.
3. For a local minimum, $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **positive definite** (sufficient condition).

**Derivation:**

The second-order Taylor expansion of $f$ about the current iterate $\mathbf{x}_k$ is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{p}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \mathbf{p} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}_k)\mathbf{p} + O(\Vert \mathbf{p} \Vert^3)$$

Neglecting the higher-order terms, the **quadratic model** is:

$$\hat{f}(\mathbf{p}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{p} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\mathbf{p}$ and setting to zero (stationary condition):

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{p}} \hat{f}(\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{g}_k + \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving for the optimal step $\mathbf{p}^*$:

$$\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{p}^* = -\mathbf{g}_k \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{p}^* = -\mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$$

The next iterate is therefore:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{p}^* = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$$

This is the **multivariable Newton update rule**. ✓

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute derivatives:**

$$f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$$
$$f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12$$
$$f''(x) = 12x - 18$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 0 (start at $x_0 = 0$):**

$$f'(0) = 12, \quad f''(0) = -18$$
$$x_1 = 0 - \frac{12}{-18} = 0 + \frac{2}{3} = 0.6667$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 1 ($x_1 = 0.6667$):**

$$f'(0.6667) = 6(0.4444) - 18(0.6667) + 12 = 2.6667 - 12 + 12 = 2.6667$$
$$f''(0.6667) = 12(0.6667) - 18 = 8 - 18 = -10$$
$$x_2 = 0.6667 - \frac{2.6667}{-10} = 0.6667 + 0.2667 = 0.9333$$

**Step 4 — Iteration 2 ($x_2 = 0.9333$):**

$$f'(0.9333) = 6(0.8711) - 18(0.9333) + 12 = 5.2267 - 16.8 + 12 = 0.4267$$
$$f''(0.9333) = 12(0.9333) - 18 = 11.2 - 18 = -6.8$$
$$x_3 = 0.9333 - \frac{0.4267}{-6.8} = 0.9333 + 0.0628 = 0.9960$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 3 ($x_3 = 0.9960$):**

$$f'(0.9960) = 6(0.9920) - 18(0.9960) + 12 = 5.952 - 17.928 + 12 = 0.024$$
$$f''(0.9960) = 12(0.9960) - 18 = 11.952 - 18 = -6.048$$
$$x_4 = 0.9960 - \frac{0.024}{-6.048} = 0.9960 + 0.00397 = 0.9999$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Second-Order Sufficiency):**

At the converged point $x^* \approx 1$:

$$f''(1) = 12(1) - 18 = -6 < 0$$

Since $f''(1) < 0$, the point $x^* = 1$ is a **local maximum**, not a minimum. The true minimum of this function lies at the boundary or at a different stationary point. (Note: The function has $f'(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1$ or $x = 2$. At $x = 2$, $f''(2) = 6 > 0$, which is the actual minimum.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Skipping the derivative step**: Students often jump straight to numerical substitution without writing $f'(x)$ and $f''(x)$ symbolically first. You lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify $f''(\mathbf{x}^*) > 0$**: The question explicitly asks for verification — marks are reserved for this step.
> 3. **Rounding errors**: Carry 4–5 decimal places until the final answer; premature rounding changes the convergence trajectory.
> 4. **Not labelling iterations**: Each iteration must be clearly indexed ($k = 0, 1, 2, 3$) for full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **convergence properties** of Newton's method in detail. Distinguish clearly between **local** and **global** convergence, and state the **conditions under which quadratic convergence is achieved**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply Newton's method to minimize $f(x, y) = (x - 1)^2 + (y - 2)^2 + xy$ starting from $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$. Compute the gradient, Hessian, inverse Hessian, and perform **two iterations**. Identify the converged minimum. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Local Convergence:**
- Newton's method possesses a **local convergence theorem**: if $f \in C^3$ in a neighbourhood of $\mathbf{x}^*$ and $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is positive definite (i.e., $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a strict local minimum), then there exists a neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ such that for **any** starting point $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}^*)$, the Newton iterates $\{\mathbf{x}_k\}$ converge to $\mathbf{x}^*$.
- Furthermore, the convergence is **quadratic** with asymptotic error constant $\mu = \frac{\Vert f'''(\mathbf{x}^*) \Vert}{2 \Vert f''(x^*) \Vert}$ (scalar form).

**Global Convergence:**
- In its **pure form** (no line search, no Hessian modification, no globalization strategy), Newton's method is **not globally convergent**. A poor initial guess can lead to:
  - **Divergence** (sequence shoots off to infinity).
  - **Cycling** (sequence oscillates between two or more points).
  - **Convergence to a saddle point** (Hessian singular or indefinite).
  - **Convergence to a maximum** (if Hessian is negative definite at the stationary point).

**Conditions for Quadratic Convergence:**

1. $f$ is **three times continuously differentiable** in a neighbourhood of $\mathbf{x}^*$.
2. $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **positive definite** (i.e., $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a non-degenerate local minimum).
3. The starting point $\mathbf{x}_0$ is **sufficiently close** to $\mathbf{x}^*$ (within the *basin of quadratic convergence*).
4. The Hessian **remains invertible** along the iterates.

**Globalization Strategies (KTU Expectation):**

- **Levenberg's modification**: Replace $\mathbf{H}_k$ with $\mathbf{H}_k + \nu \mathbf{I}$, where $\nu > 0$ is a small regularizing parameter. This biases the direction toward steepest descent when far from the optimum, ensuring robust convergence.
- **Line search on the Newton direction**: Compute $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{H}_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$, then find $\alpha_k > 0$ satisfying the **Wolfe conditions** for sufficient decrease and curvature.
- **Trust-region methods**: Accept the Newton step only if the predicted reduction matches the actual reduction of $f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The unmodified Newton's method is the **theoretically optimal** local algorithm but the **practically fragile** one. All industrial-grade Newton's method implementations (e.g., in MATLAB's `fsolve`, Python's `scipy.optimize`) include a globalization strategy.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute gradient and Hessian symbolically:**

$$f(x, y) = (x-1)^2 + (y-2)^2 + xy$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2(x-1) + y \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2(y-2) + x$$

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial y} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y \partial x} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 0 at $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$:**

$$\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0-1) + 0 \\ 2(0-2) + 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Hessian is constant: $\mathbf{H}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{H}^{-1}$:** Determinant $= (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 4 - 1 = 3$. Inverse:

$$\mathbf{H}^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Newton direction at iteration 0:**

$$\mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{H}^{-1}\mathbf{g}_0 = -\frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix} = -\frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} -4 + 4 \\ 2 - 8 \end{bmatrix} = -\frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Update to get $(x_1, y_1)$:**

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Iteration 1 at $(x_1, y_1) = (0, 2)$:**

$$\mathbf{g}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0-1) + 2 \\ 2(2-2) + 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Termination check:** Since $\mathbf{g}_1 = \mathbf{0}$, the algorithm has converged in **two iterations**.

**Step 8 — Identify the minimum:**

$$\mathbf{x}^* = (0, 2)^T$$

The optimum function value is:

$$f(0, 2) = (0-1)^2 + (2-2)^2 + (0)(2) = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1$$

**Verification (Positive Definiteness of Hessian at $\mathbf{x}^*$):**

$$\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Leading principal minors: $2 > 0$ and $(2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3 > 0$. By **Sylvester's criterion**, the Hessian is positive definite, confirming $\mathbf{x}^* = (0, 2)^T$ is a **strict local minimum**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the structural elegance:** Because the Hessian is **constant** (the function is purely quadratic), Newton's method converges to the exact minimum in **one step** from any starting point — a hallmark property exploited in **Quadratic Programming (QP)** solvers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Hot-Spots in Newton's Method Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the second-order sufficiency check** ($f''(\mathbf{x}^*) > 0$ or $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$). Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** exclusively for this. Skipping it caps your score at ~80%.
> 2. **Confusing scalar vs. vector forms**: Writing the scalar formula when the question demands the vector form (or vice versa). Always read carefully whether $f$ is a function of one or many variables.
> 3. **Inverting the Hessian incorrectly** in $2 \times 2$ cases. Memorize: $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$.
> 4. **Sign error in the Newton step**: It is $\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{H}^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$, not $\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{H}^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$. The negative sign is sacred.
> 5. **Omitting iteration labels and intermediate gradients/Hessians**: KTU valuation is highly procedural. Each $k$ must be clearly indexed, and the gradient and Hessian at each step must be **explicitly written** (not just referenced).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newton's method** is a **second-order** iterative optimization algorithm that uses **gradient + Hessian** information.
- The canonical update is: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{H}_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$.
- The scalar form is: $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k) / f''(x_k)$.
- It exhibits **quadratic convergence** near a strict local minimum — the number of correct digits **doubles** per iteration.
- The **step size is unity** ($\alpha = 1$) by construction, since the Newton direction exactly minimizes the local quadratic model.
- The method is **not globally convergent** in its pure form — globalization strategies (Levenberg regularization, line search, trust regions) are required in practice.
- The Newton direction is the **solution of a linear system** $\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$, **not** the direct inverse multiplication (computational efficiency tip).
- **Purely quadratic functions** are solved in **one iteration** (exact solution in a single Newton step).
- The **asymptotic error constant** for the scalar case is $\mu = \vert f'''(x^*) \vert / (2 \vert f''(x^*) \vert)$.
- **Termination criteria**: $\|\mathbf{g}_k\| \le \varepsilon$ (gradient norm) or $\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k\| \le \varepsilon$ (step norm).
- **Second-order sufficiency condition** for a strict local minimum: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ **AND** $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$.
- Newton's method is the **theoretical foundation** for Quasi-Newton methods (DFP, BFGS) and interior-point methods in constrained optimization.
- In **Kerala state KTU examinations**, expect: 1 definition question (Part A), 1 derivation + numerical (Part B), and 1 convergence/comparison question per exam cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization algorithms for solving constrained optimization problems: Direct methods, penalty function methods, barrier method, optimization of function of multiple variables subject to equality constraints
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT413 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimization algorithms for solving constrained optimization problems: Direct methods, penalty function methods, barrier method, optimization of function of multiple variables subject to equality constraints

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Constrained Optimization Problem?

A **Constrained Optimization Problem (COP)** is a mathematical programming problem in which the design vector **x** ∈ ℝⁿ is restricted to lie within a feasible region defined by a set of constraints. The general nonlinear programming (NLP) formulation under the KTU 2024 syllabus is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n
\end{aligned}$$

where $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**, $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ are **inequality constraints**, and $h_k(\mathbf{x})$ are **equality constraints**. The set $S = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ is called the **feasible region**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This module carries the highest weightage in Module 3 because constrained problems form the backbone of real engineering design (structural sizing, process yield maximization, trajectory optimization, portfolio risk minimization).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Mountain Climber with a Permit"**
>
> Imagine you want to climb to the lowest valley (minimize f) on a mountain range. Unconstrained optimization lets you walk anywhere. **Constrained optimization** is like being told: *"You may only walk on the trail marked by the blue ribbon (equality constraint h(x)=0), and you may never cross the red fence (inequality constraint g(x)≤0)."* 
>
> - **Direct methods** = try every reachable point systematically. 
> - **Penalty function** = instead of the fence, slap a heavy fine on yourself whenever you step over it; the fine grows so large that you naturally stay inside.
> - **Barrier method** = pretend the fence is a wall of fire. The closer you get, the more painfully hot it feels (logarithmic wall), so you steer clear from the inside.
> - **Lagrange multipliers** = a brilliant guide who tells you exactly *how hard* the fence is pushing against you at the optimal point — the "shadow price" of relaxing the constraint.

### Physical / Engineering Interpretation of Key Quantities

- **Feasible region (S)**: All physically realizable engineering designs.
- **Active constraint at x\***: A constraint for which the KKT multiplier $\lambda_j^* > 0$; the design literally *touches* this constraint.
- **Inactive constraint**: A constraint that the optimum does *not* touch; it can be removed without changing the answer.
- **Lagrange multiplier $\lambda_k$**: Represents the **sensitivity of the optimal objective value** to a unit relaxation of the corresponding equality constraint — physically, the *rate of change of cost per unit change in constraint allowance*.

> [!TIP]
> In Mechanical Engineering design, the equality Lagrange multiplier for a stress constraint literally represents the **shadow cost** of the strength limit. In Chemical Engineering, the multiplier on a mass-balance equality is the *economic value* of relaxing that material balance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct Methods for Constrained Optimization

Direct methods are *search-based techniques* that do not require derivatives. They are particularly useful for engineering problems where gradients are unavailable (noisy simulations, black-box functions, experimental data).

### 2.1.1 Random Search Method

Generate a sequence of feasible points $\{\mathbf{x}^{(k)}\}$ by sampling from a uniform distribution over the feasible region. The best feasible point encountered is retained.

$$\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} + \mathbf{s}^{(k)}, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{s}^{(k)} \sim U(-\Delta, \Delta)$$

If $f(\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)}) < f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$, accept the move; else reject. The step bound $\Delta$ is reduced periodically (analogous to simulated annealing's cooling schedule).

### 2.1.2 Systematic / Grid Search Method

Discretize each design variable into $N$ levels, creating a uniform grid of $N^n$ points. Evaluate $f$ at every point and pick the minimum. **Computational cost** explodes exponentially with $n$ (curse of dimensionality), but it guarantees the global minimum on a bounded region — used as a benchmark.

### 2.1.3 Variable Elimination (Direct Substitution) Method

When the equality constraints $h_k(\mathbf{x})=0$ can be solved explicitly to express $n$ variables in terms of $(n-p)$ independent variables, the problem reduces dimension. For instance, if $h(x_1, x_2) = 0$ is solved as $x_1 = \phi(x_2)$:

$$F(x_2) = f(\phi(x_2), x_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \min F(x_2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters in KTU valuation:** Examiners reward *cursory* mention of the exponential cost of grid search. Always pair the method with its computational complexity comment.

---

## 2.2 Penalty Function Method (Exterior Penalty)

The **penalty function approach** transforms the constrained problem into a sequence of *unconstrained* problems by adding a penalty term to the objective function that is **zero on the feasible region** and **large outside it**.

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Formulation

For a problem with inequality constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ and equality constraints $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, define the **augmented penalty function**:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}, r) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left[ \max(0, \; g_j(\mathbf{x})) \right]^2 + \frac{1}{r} \sum_{k=1}^{p} \left[ h_k(\mathbf{x}) \right]^2$$

where:
- $r > 0$ is the **penalty parameter** (a scalar multiplier).
- $r \cdot [\max(0, g_j)]^2$ is the **inequality penalty** — zero if $g_j \leq 0$, positive otherwise.
- $(1/r) \cdot [h_k]^2$ is the **equality penalty** — note the *inverse* weighting to handle exact equality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** Students often write $r \cdot [g_j]^2$ for inequality. The correct form uses $\max(0, g_j)$ so that feasible points incur **zero** penalty. Writing it incorrectly costs 2 marks.

### 2.2.2 The Penalty Algorithm

**Step 1:** Choose an initial point $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$, initial penalty $r_0 > 0$ (commonly $r_0 = 1$), tolerance $\epsilon > 0$, and a growth factor $c > 1$ (commonly $c = 10$).

**Step 2:** Minimize $\phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$ using an unconstrained method (Newton, quasi-Newton, conjugate gradient) to obtain $\mathbf{x}^*_k$.

**Step 3:** If $r_k \cdot \sum \max(0, g_j)^2 + (1/r_k) \sum h_k^2 < \epsilon$, STOP. Else set $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k$ and return to Step 2.

As $r \to \infty$, the penalty becomes so steep that the minimizer of $\phi$ is forced onto the feasible region, and the limit of the solutions is the constrained optimum.

### 2.2.3 Quadratic vs. Non-Quadratic Penalties

| Penalty Type | Formula | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Quadratic (Courant) | $r \cdot [\max(0, g_j)]^2$ | Smooth, $C^1$; needs $r \to \infty$ |
| Absolute value | $r \cdot \max(0, g_j)$ | Non-smooth at $g_j = 0$ |
| Reciprocal | $1/g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for $g_j < 0$ | Used in interior methods |

---

## 2.3 Barrier Function Method (Interior Penalty)

The **barrier method** is an *interior* technique: it requires a strictly feasible starting point $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ (i.e., $g_j(\mathbf{x}^{(0)}) < 0$ for all $j$) and constructs a function that **blows up to +∞** as the design approaches the constraint boundary from inside.

### 2.3.1 The Logarithmic Barrier (Frisch's Method)

$$\psi(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = f(\mathbf{x}) - \mu \sum_{j=1}^{m} \ln\bigl(-g_j(\mathbf{x})\bigr)$$

where $\mu > 0$ is the **barrier parameter**. As $\mathbf{x} \to \partial S$ (the constraint boundary), $-g_j(\mathbf{x}) \to 0^+$, so $\ln(-g_j) \to -\infty$ and $-\mu \ln(-g_j) \to +\infty$, creating an impenetrable "wall."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why negative sign matters:** Since $g_j < 0$ inside the feasible region, $-g_j > 0$, and $\ln$ is defined. The minus sign in front of $\mu$ *amplifies* the divergence.

### 2.3.2 The Reciprocal Barrier

$$\psi(\mathbf{x}, \mu) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \mu \sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{1}{-g_j(\mathbf{x})}$$

This is also a valid interior penalty function. It is $C^\infty$ inside the feasible region but has a pole of order 1 at the boundary.

### 2.3.3 The Barrier Algorithm

**Step 1:** Find a strictly feasible $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ (e.g., using Phase-I simplex-like feasibility search).

**Step 2:** Choose $\mu_0 > 0$, reduction factor $\theta \in (0, 1)$ (commonly $\theta = 0.1$ or $0.2$), and tolerance $\epsilon$.

**Step 3:** Minimize $\psi(\mathbf{x}, \mu_k)$ starting from $\mathbf{x}^{(k-1)}$ using a *gradient-based* method (Newton's method recommended) to obtain $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$.

**Step 4:** If $\mu_k < \epsilon$, STOP. Else set $\mu_{k+1} = \theta \cdot \mu_k$ and return to Step 3.

As $\mu \to 0^+$, the barrier vanishes, and the sequence of unconstrained minima converges to the constrained optimum (from the *interior*).

### 2.3.4 Penalty vs. Barrier — Side-by-Side

| Feature | Penalty (Exterior) | Barrier (Interior) |
|---|---|---|
| Starting point | Any point (infeasible OK) | Must be strictly feasible |
| Behavior at boundary | Penalty grows but finite | Function → +∞ (true wall) |
| Final penalty parameter | $r \to \infty$ | $\mu \to 0$ |
| Numerical conditioning | Worse as $r \to \infty$ (Hessian ill-conditioned) | Better; mild growth of $\mu^{-1}$ |
| Equality constraints | Direct | Must be eliminated or treated separately |
| Practical use | Engineering design (relaxed start) | Primal interior-point methods (modern IPMs) |

---

## 2.4 Lagrange Multiplier Method for Equality Constraints

This is the **classical analytical method** for problems where a function of several variables is subject to one or more equality constraints.

### 2.4.1 The Standard Problem

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p < n
\end{aligned}$$

with $f, h_k \in C^1$.

### 2.4.2 The Lagrangian Function

Define the **augmented Lagrangian** (sometimes called the *Lagrangian* in KTU texts):

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k \, h_k(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^\top \mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x})$$

where $\boldsymbol{\lambda} = (\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_p)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the vector of **Lagrange multipliers**.

### 2.4.3 Necessary Conditions (First-Order KKT for Equality Constraints)

If $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a regular local minimizer (i.e., $\nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*)$ for $k=1,\ldots,p$ are **linearly independent**), then there exist scalars $\lambda_1^*, \ldots, \lambda_p^*$ such that:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k^* \, \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}} L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) = h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, p$$

Together these form a system of $n + p$ equations in $n + p$ unknowns $(x_1^*, \ldots, x_n^*, \lambda_1^*, \ldots, \lambda_p^*)$.

### 2.4.4 The Jacobian Matrix

The **Jacobian** of the constraint vector $\mathbf{h}(\mathbf{x}) = (h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_p)^\top$ is the $p \times n$ matrix:

$$\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial h_1}{\partial x_n} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial h_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial h_2}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial h_2}{\partial x_n} \\[6pt] \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial h_p}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial h_p}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial h_p}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}$$

The **LICQ (Linear Independence Constraint Qualification)** says the rows of $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}$ are linearly independent at $\mathbf{x}^*$ (i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}^*)) = p$). This is what allows the existence of the multipliers.

### 2.4.5 Lagrange Multiplier Theorem (Existence Statement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Lagrange Multiplier Theorem).** Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be a local extremum of $f$ subject to $h_k(\mathbf{x})=0, \; k=1,\ldots,p$. If $f$ and $h_k$ have continuous first partial derivatives in a neighborhood of $\mathbf{x}^*$, and the gradient vectors $\nabla h_1(\mathbf{x}^*), \ldots, \nabla h_p(\mathbf{x}^*)$ are linearly independent, then there exists a unique vector $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^* = (\lambda_1^*, \ldots, \lambda_p^*)^\top$ such that $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k^* \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.

### 2.4.6 Second-Order Sufficient Condition

For a **local minimum**, in addition to the first-order conditions, the Hessian of the Lagrangian, restricted to the tangent subspace of the constraints, must be positive definite.

Define the tangent subspace at $\mathbf{x}^*$ as:

$$T = \left\{ \mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^n \;\Big|\; \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{0} \right\}$$

i.e., the null space of the constraint Jacobian. If:

$$\mathbf{d}^\top \nabla^2_{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}} L(\mathbf{x}^*, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*) \, \mathbf{d} > 0 \quad \forall \, \mathbf{d} \in T \setminus \{\mathbf{0}\}$$

then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local minimizer**.

The bordered Hessian for a problem with 1 constraint ($p=1$) is:

$$|\bar{\mathbf{H}}| = \begin{vmatrix} 0 & \nabla h_1^\top \\ \nabla h_1 & \nabla^2_{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}} L \end{vmatrix}$$

For minimum: $(-1)^{p+1} |\bar{\mathbf{H}}| > 0$, and the last $(n-1)$ leading principal minors of the reduced (bordered-removed) Hessian must satisfy certain sign patterns.

### 2.4.7 Two-Variable / One-Constraint Case (Most Tested in KTU)

For: Minimize $f(x, y)$ subject to $g(x, y) = 0$, the conditions are:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} + \lambda \frac{\partial g}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} + \lambda \frac{\partial g}{\partial y} = 0, \quad g(x, y) = 0$$

The bordered Hessian condition for minimum is:

$$\begin{vmatrix} 0 & g_x & g_y \\ g_x & L_{xx} & L_{xy} \\ g_y & L_{yx} & L_{yy} \end{vmatrix} > 0 \quad \text{(for $n=2$, $p=1$)}$$

where $L = f + \lambda g$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The sign of the bordered Hessian is **opposite** to that of a regular Hessian. The condition is $> 0$ for minimum in 2 variables; many students write $< 0$ and lose 2 marks.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Statement | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| General COP | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_j \leq 0, \; h_k = 0$ | $j=1..m$, $k=1..p$ |
| Lagrangian | $L(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f + \sum \lambda_k h_k$ | $\boldsymbol{\lambda} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ |
| First-order necessary | $\nabla f + \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}^\top \boldsymbol{\lambda} = \mathbf{0}$ | $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{h}}$ is $p \times n$ |
| Equality KKT | $h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \;\nabla f + \sum \lambda_k \nabla h_k = \mathbf{0}$ | LICQ required |
| Penalty function | $\phi = f + r \sum [\max(0,g_j)]^2 + (1/r) \sum h_k^2$ | $r \to \infty$ |
| Log barrier | $\psi = f - \mu \sum \ln(-g_j)$ | $\mu \to 0^+$, $-g_j > 0$ |
| Reciprocal barrier | $\psi = f + \mu \sum (1/(-g_j))$ | $\mu \to 0^+$ |
| Penalty update | $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k$ | $c = 5$ to $10$ |
| Barrier update | $\mu_{k+1} = \theta \cdot \mu_k$ | $\theta = 0.1$ to $0.5$ |
| Lagrange theorem | $\exists \boldsymbol{\lambda}^*$ s.t. $\nabla f + \sum \lambda_k \nabla h_k = \mathbf{0}$ | LICQ: rows of $\mathbf{J}$ independent |
| 2nd-order sufficient | $\mathbf{d}^\top \nabla^2_{\mathbf{xx}} L \, \mathbf{d} > 0$ on tangent space | strict min |
| Bordered Hessian | $|H_b| > 0$ for min, $n=2$, $p=1$ | $(n+p) \times (n+p)$ |
| Direct search cost | $O(N^n)$ grid | exponential in $n$ |
