<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]  
> Format: Direct conceptual or definition-type questions. Answer length: 3–5 lines.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**  
Define a Genetic Algorithm. List and briefly explain any **two** operators used in GA. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> A Genetic Algorithm is a population-based stochastic optimization technique that mimics the process of natural evolution. It works on a set (population) of candidate solutions called *chromosomes*, iteratively improving them by applying **selection**, **crossover**, and **mutation** operators.
>
> **(a) Selection:** The Roulette-Wheel method selects individuals with probability proportional to their fitness — fitter individuals get more copies in the mating pool.
>
> **(b) Crossover:** Combines two parent chromosomes at a randomly chosen cut-point, producing two offspring that inherit traits from both parents, enabling the exploration of new regions of the search space.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample Paper 1]**  
What is a **fitness function**? How is it related to the objective function in GA? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **fitness function** $F(\mathbf{x})$ is a non-negative scalar measure of how *good* a candidate solution $\mathbf{x}$ is. It is the GA's evaluation criterion used to rank individuals and drive selection.
>
> For a **maximization** problem, $F(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x})$ (the objective function directly).
>
> For a **minimization** problem, fitness inversion is used, e.g., $F(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{1 + f(\mathbf{x})}$ or $F(\mathbf{x}) = C_{\max} - f(\mathbf{x})$. The fitness function thus maps the objective to a **non-negative scale** suitable for probabilistic selection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> KTU 2024 ESE convention: each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The model answers below follow the official valuation key style.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q4(b)]**

**(a)** Explain the **different encoding schemes** used to represent chromosomes in a Genetic Algorithm. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Encoding is the mapping from the decision-variable space to the chromosome (genotype) space used by the GA. The three principal schemes are:

1. **Binary Encoding:** Each variable is represented by a fixed-length string of bits. A chromosome of $n$ variables each with $L$ bits has total length $nL$. Decoding uses  
   $$x = x_{\min} + \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^L - 1} \cdot \text{binary-to-integer}.$$  
   *Advantages:* Simple, well-studied theory (Schema Theorem). *Disadvantages:* Hamming-cliff problem (e.g., 0111 → 1000 flips 4 bits for a unit change in $x$).

2. **Real-Valued (Floating-Point) Encoding:** Each gene is a real number, suitable for continuous optimization in higher dimensions. Crossover becomes arithmetic blending, mutation becomes a small Gaussian perturbation. *Advantages:* No decoding error, larger domains.

3. **Permutation Encoding:** Each chromosome is a permutation of $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$, used for ordering problems (e.g., Traveling Salesman, scheduling). Special operators (PMX, OX crossover, swap mutation) preserve feasibility.

**Auxiliary schemes:** Gray-code encoding (avoids Hamming cliff), integer encoding, tree encoding (genetic programming).

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the working of **Roulette-Wheel Selection**. Compute the expected number of copies of each individual for the population: fitnesses $\{10, 20, 30, 40\}$, $N = 4$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

In **Roulette-Wheel Selection**, each individual is allocated a slot on a virtual roulette wheel whose angular width is proportional to its fitness. Spinning the wheel $N$ times yields the mating pool.

**Computation:**  
Total fitness:
$$\sum F_i = 10 + 20 + 30 + 40 = 100$$

Selection probabilities:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 10/100 = 0.10\\
P_2 &= 20/100 = 0.20\\
P_3 &= 30/100 = 0.30\\
P_4 &= 40/100 = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

Expected number of copies (with $N = 4$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= 4 \times 0.10 = 0.4\\
E_2 &= 4 \times 0.20 = 0.8\\
E_3 &= 4 \times 0.30 = 1.2\\
E_4 &= 4 \times 0.40 = 1.6
\end{aligned}
$$

The fittest individual (fitness 40) is expected to appear **1.6 times** in the mating pool, while the weakest (fitness 10) appears only **0.4 times** — illustrating the selection pressure toward better solutions. Limitation: fitness scaling becomes skewed when one individual dominates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2023, Q4(a)]**

**(a)** Write the **step-by-step algorithm** of a simple Genetic Algorithm. State any **two termination criteria**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm (Step-by-Step):**

1. **[Initialize]** Randomly generate a population $P(0)$ of $N$ chromosomes. Set generation counter $t \leftarrow 0$.
2. **[Evaluate]** Compute fitness $F_i$ of each individual $\mathbf{x}_i \in P(t)$.
3. **[Test]** If termination criterion is met, output the best individual and stop.
4. **[Select]** Form a mating pool by selecting parents (e.g., Roulette-Wheel or Tournament).
5. **[Crossover]** With probability $P_c$, apply crossover to selected parent pairs to produce offspring.
6. **[Mutate]** With probability $P_m$ (typically small), apply mutation to offspring genes.
7. **[Replace]** Form the next-generation population $P(t+1)$ (generational / steady-state / elitist).
8. **[Loop]** Set $t \leftarrow t + 1$ and go to Step 2.

**Two Termination Criteria:**

1. **Fixed number of generations** $T_{\max}$ is exhausted (most common).
2. **Convergence detection** — when the variance of population fitness falls below a threshold $\varepsilon$, i.e.,
   $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (F_i - \bar{F})^2 < \varepsilon$$
3. *(Optional third)* No improvement in best fitness for $K$ consecutive generations.

**(b)** Solve the following optimization problem using **one generation of a Genetic Algorithm** with binary encoding. Show all steps clearly. **[7 Marks]**

**Problem:** Maximize $f(x) = x^2$, $0 \leq x \leq 31$, integer. Use 5-bit binary encoding, population size 4, single-point crossover at the middle bit, no mutation, roulette-wheel selection.

**Given Initial Population:**

| Chromosome | Binary | $x$ | $f(x)$ |
|------------|--------|-----|--------|
| $C_1$ | 01101 | 13 | 169 |
| $C_2$ | 11000 | 24 | 576 |
| $C_3$ | 01000 | 8 | 64 |
| $C_4$ | 10011 | 19 | 361 |

**Step 1 — Total fitness:** $\sum F_i = 169 + 576 + 64 + 361 = 1170$.

**Step 2 — Selection Probabilities:**

| $i$ | $F_i$ | $P_i = F_i / 1170$ | $E_i = 4 P_i$ |
|-----|-------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | 169 | 0.1444 | 0.5778 |
| 2 | 576 | 0.4923 | 1.9692 |
| 3 | 64 | 0.0547 | 0.2188 |
| 4 | 361 | 0.3085 | 1.2342 |

**Step 3 — Mating Pool (sampling):** $C_2$ twice, $C_4$ twice, $C_1, C_3$ discarded. Mating pool = $\{11000, 11000, 10011, 10011\}$.

**Step 4 — Crossover at middle bit (between bit 2 and bit 3):**

| Parents | b4 b2 \| b1 b0 | Offspring 1 | Offspring 2 |
|---------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| 11000 × 11000 | 1 1 0 \| 0 0 | 11000 ($x=24, f=576$) | 11000 ($x=24, f=576$) |
| 10011 × 10011 | 1 0 0 \| 1 1 | 10011 ($x=19, f=361$) | 10011 ($x=19, f=361$) |

**Step 5 — New Population (Generation 1):**

| Chromosome | $x$ | $f(x)$ |
|------------|-----|--------|
| $C_1'$ | 24 | 576 |
| $C_2'$ | 24 | 576 |
| $C_3'$ | 19 | 361 |
| $C_4'$ | 19 | 361 |

**Step 6 — Comparison:**

| Metric | Gen 0 | Gen 1 |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Sum | 1170 | 1874 |
| Average | 292.5 | 468.5 |
| Minimum | 64 | 361 |
| Maximum | 576 | 576 |

**Result:** The minimum fitness rose from 64 to 361 (elimination of poor solution), and the average fitness improved by **60%**. The GA is converging toward better regions of the search space.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in GA Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by sum** in Roulette-Wheel probability — students often write $P_i = F_i$ instead of $P_i = F_i / \sum F_j$. This loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing fitness with objective function** for minimization problems. If $f(\mathbf{x})$ is to be minimized, fitness must be **inverted**; do not use raw $f$ in selection.
> 3. **Decimal precision in expected count** — KTU expects values rounded to **4 decimal places** (e.g., $0.1444$, not $0.14$).
> 4. **Not stating the encoding / parameter assumptions** — always declare: encoding scheme, chromosome length, $P_c$, $P_m$, selection method, crossover cut-point, number of generations. Missing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the comparison table** between generations — examiners want to *see* that average / min / max fitness improved. Always include a Gen-0 vs Gen-1 table.
> 6. **Forgetting elitism** — in real-world GAs, the best individual is carried forward unchanged. Mention this when asked about improvements.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Genetic Algorithms**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] GA is a **population-based, stochastic, derivative-free** meta-heuristic.
- [ ] Inspired by **Darwinian evolution** — survival of the fittest.
- [ ] Works on a **population** of **chromosomes** (candidate solutions).
- [ ] Each chromosome consists of **genes** (decision variable values).

### Algorithm Flow
- [ ] **Five stages:** Init → Evaluate → Select → Crossover → Mutate → Replace.
- [ ] Termination by **fixed generations**, **fitness convergence**, or **stagnation**.

### Encoding
- [ ] **Binary:** $L$-bit string decodes to real via $x = x_{\min} + \frac{(x_{\max} - x_{\min}) \cdot b}{2^L - 1}$.
- [ ] **Real-valued** for continuous, **Permutation** for ordering problems.

### Selection
- [ ] **Roulette-Wheel:** $P_i = F_i / \sum F_j$, expected count $E_i = N P_i$.
- [ ] **Tournament:** pick $k$, return best.
- [ ] **Rank-based:** probability proportional to rank, not fitness.

### Crossover
- [ ] **Single-point, two-point, uniform** for binary.
- [ ] **Arithmetic** for real-valued: $x_{child} = \alpha x_1 + (1-\alpha) x_2$.
- [ ] Typical $P_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$.

### Mutation
- [ ] **Bit-flip** for binary; **Gaussian** for real-valued.
- [ ] Typical $P_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ — small to preserve good solutions.

### Theory
- [ ] **Schema Theorem (Holland):** Above-average, short, low-order building blocks grow exponentially.
- [ ] **Building Block Hypothesis:** GA combines short useful schemas to form the optimum.

### Parameters
- [ ] Population size $N = 50$–$200$ typical.
- [ ] Use **elitism** to preserve best individual.

### Key Formulas (Quick Recall)
- [ ] $P_i = F_i / \sum F_j$
- [ ] $E_i = N P_i$
- [ ] $F(\mathbf{x}) = 1 / (1 + f(\mathbf{x}))$ for minimization
- [ ] $x = x_{\min} + \frac{(x_{\max} - x_{\min}) \cdot b}{2^L - 1}$

### KTU Exam Tips
- [ ] Always **show all generations** with comparison table.
- [ ] **State assumptions** (encoding, $P_c, P_m$, selection method).
- [ ] Mention **elitism** and **convergence** in long answers.
- [ ] Practice the **Goldberg $f(x) = x^2$** problem at least 3 times — it is the single most repeated KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulated annealing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT413 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simulated annealing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a **probabilistic metaheuristic** optimization algorithm inspired by the **metallurgical process of annealing**, in which a metal is heated to a high temperature and then slowly cooled to reduce internal defects and reach a low-energy crystalline state. The algorithm searches for the **global optimum** of an objective function by allowing **occasional uphill (worse) moves** with a probability that decreases as the "temperature" parameter is gradually reduced.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Simulated Annealing belongs to the family of **trajectory-based, single-solution metaheuristics** that emulate a physical process to escape **local optima** in a multi-modal search landscape. It was first proposed by **Kirkpatrick, Gelatt, and Vecchi (1983)** and independently by **Černý (1985)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Wandering Mountain Climber"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a blindfolded mountaineer trying to reach the lowest valley in a foggy mountain range.** A pure greedy climber (like *steepest descent*) would always step downhill. The moment he steps into a small dip, he is **trapped forever** — he never knows a deeper valley lies just over the next ridge.
>
> **Simulated Annealing is a "drunk-but-cooling" climber.** At high "temperature" (early on), he is willing to **walk uphill** — into foggier, higher regions — with a generous probability. As the temperature drops, he becomes **steadier and more rational**, finally only accepting downhill moves near the end. This controlled "shaking" allows him to **step out of local traps** and eventually find the deepest valley — the global minimum.

### 1.3 Physical-to-Optimization Mapping

| Physical Annealing Concept | Optimization Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Metal specimen | Problem instance (search space) |
| Energy of atoms $(E)$ | Objective function value $f(x)$ |
| Temperature $(T)$ | Control parameter $T$ (decreases over time) |
| Atomic configuration | Candidate solution $x$ |
| Ground state (lowest energy) | **Global optimum** $x^{*}$ |
| Cooling rate | Cooling schedule (e.g., $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$) |

### 1.4 Core Distinctive Feature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike *gradient-based* or *greedy* methods, SA can **accept a worse solution** with probability given by the **Boltzmann distribution**. This is the only mechanism by which it **escapes local minima**, and it is the key concept examiners will test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Philosophy

The fundamental problem with classical local search is the **local-optimum trap**. SA overcomes this by:

1. **Perturbing** the current solution to obtain a neighbor.
2. **Comparing** the neighbor's objective value to the current one.
3. **Always accepting** an improvement ($\Delta E < 0$).
4. **Sometimes accepting** a worse move ($\Delta E > 0$) with probability $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$.
5. **Cooling** the temperature so that uphill moves become progressively rarer.

This combination of *diversification* (early, high temperature) and *intensification* (late, low temperature) is the theoretical heart of SA.

### 2.2 Algorithmic Logic — Step by Step

1. **Initialize**: Choose initial solution $x_0$, initial temperature $T_0 > 0$, cooling factor $\alpha \in (0,1)$, and termination criterion.
2. **Set**: $x \leftarrow x_0$, $x^{*} \leftarrow x_0$, $T \leftarrow T_0$.
3. **Repeat** until stopping condition is met:
   - Generate a **neighbor** $x_{\text{new}}$ from the current $x$ using a perturbation rule.
   - Compute the **energy change**: $\Delta E = f(x_{\text{new}}) - f(x)$.
   - **Acceptance test**:
     - If $\Delta E \leq 0$ → accept: $x \leftarrow x_{\text{new}}$.
     - If $\Delta E > 0$ → generate $r \sim U(0,1)$. If $r < \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ → accept; else reject.
   - Update best: $x^{*} \leftarrow x_{\text{new}}$ if $f(x_{\text{new}}) < f(x^{*})$.
   - **Cool**: $T \leftarrow \alpha T$.
4. **Return** $x^{*}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four equations below are **examination-mandatory**. Memorize the symbols, units, and physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(\text{accept}) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta E}{T}\right)$ | **Boltzmann acceptance probability** for a worse move | $\Delta E > 0$, $T > 0$, $P \in (0,1)$ |
| 2 | $\Delta E = f(x_{\text{new}}) - f(x)$ | Energy (cost) change | Scalar; +ve = worse, −ve = better |
| 3 | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_{k}$ | **Geometric cooling schedule** | $\alpha \in (0.8, 0.99)$ typically; $T_k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ |
| 4 | $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{\,k}$ | Closed-form temperature at iteration $k$ | Used in convergence analysis |

**Why these matter for KTU 2024 Scheme**:
- Equation 1 is asked verbatim in **Part A** almost every exam cycle.
- Equation 3 appears in numerical problems in **Part B**.
- Equation 4 is used to compute the temperature at a specific iteration when asked.

### 2.4 Special Limits of the Boltzmann Probability

> [!NOTE]
> **As $T \to \infty$** (very high temperature): $\exp(-\Delta E / T) \to 1$. The algorithm **accepts every move**, behaving like a **random walk** — full exploration of the search space.
>
> **As $T \to 0$** (very low temperature): $\exp(-\Delta E / T) \to 0$. The algorithm **rejects all uphill moves**, collapsing to a **pure greedy local search** — pure exploitation near the current point.
>
> **The genius of SA** is the **smooth interpolation** between these two extremes controlled by the cooling schedule.

### 2.5 Cooling Schedules (Exam-Ready List)

| Schedule | Formula | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric (most common)** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ | Exponential decay; $\alpha \approx 0.9$–$0.99$ |
| **Linear** | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta$ | Slow, predictable drop |
| **Logarithmic (theoretical optimum)** | $T_k = T_0 / \log(k+1)$ | Guarantees convergence, but impractical |
| **Adaptive** | $T$ depends on $\Delta E$ statistics | Used in modern variants |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **VLSI circuit design** — optimal component placement on a chip.
- **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — large-route optimization.
- **Job-shop scheduling** in manufacturing.
- **Image processing** — image restoration, segmentation.
- **Structural design** — minimum-weight truss topology.
- **Neural network training** — to escape shallow local minima of the loss function.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Boltzmann Acceptance Criterion

The acceptance probability comes from **statistical mechanics**. In a thermal bath at temperature $T$, the probability that a system is in a state with energy $E$ is given by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$
P(E) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E}{k_B T}\right)
$$

For two states — current (energy $E_1$) and neighbor (energy $E_2 = E_1 + \Delta E$) — the **relative** probability of being in the higher-energy state is:

$$
\frac{P(E_2)}{P(E_1)} = \frac{\exp(-E_2 / k_B T)}{\exp(-E_1 / k_B T)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_2 - E_1}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)
$$

For optimization, the physical constant $k_B$ is **absorbed into a re-scaled temperature parameter** $T$ (a convention in computational SA), giving the canonical acceptance rule:

$$
P(\text{accept uphill move}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

This formula is the **single most-tested equation** in this module.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Exhaustive Iteration

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = (x - 4)^{2}$ using Simulated Annealing.
> **Given:** Initial solution $x_0 = 8$, initial temperature $T_0 = 100$, cooling factor $\alpha = 0.8$, perturbation $\pm 1$, termination after 4 iterations.

**Pre-iteration setup:**

$$
f(x_0) = f(8) = (8-4)^{2} = 16
$$

Set $x \leftarrow 8$, $T \leftarrow 100$, $x^{*} \leftarrow 8$.

---

**Iteration 1:** $T = 100$, current $x = 8$, $f(x) = 16$.

Generate neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = 8 - 1 = 7$.

$$
f(x_{\text{new}}) = f(7) = (7-4)^{2} = 9
$$

$$
\Delta E = 9 - 16 = -7
$$

Since $\Delta E \leq 0$, **accept unconditionally**. Update $x \leftarrow 7$, $x^{*} \leftarrow 7$.

Cool the temperature:

$$
T \leftarrow 0.8 \times 100 = 80
$$

---

**Iteration 2:** $T = 80$, current $x = 7$, $f(x) = 9$.

Generate neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = 7 + 1 = 8$.

$$
f(x_{\text{new}}) = (8-4)^{2} = 16
$$

$$
\Delta E = 16 - 9 = +7
$$

This is an **uphill move**. Compute acceptance probability:

$$
P = \exp\!\left(-\frac{7}{80}\right) = \exp(-0.0875) \approx 0.9162
$$

Draw $r = 0.45$ (assume). Since $r = 0.45 < P = 0.9162$, **accept the worse move**. Update $x \leftarrow 8$.

Cool:

$$
T \leftarrow 0.8 \times 80 = 64
$$

Best so far: $x^{*} = 7$, $f(x^{*}) = 9$.

---

**Iteration 3:** $T = 64$, current $x = 8$, $f(x) = 16$.

Generate neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = 8 - 1 = 7$.

$$
f(x_{\text{new}}) = 9, \quad \Delta E = 9 - 16 = -7
$$

$\Delta E \leq 0 \Rightarrow$ **accept**. $x \leftarrow 7$, $x^{*} \leftarrow 7$.

Cool:

$$
T \leftarrow 0.8 \times 64 = 51.2
$$

---

**Iteration 4:** $T = 51.2$, current $x = 7$, $f(x) = 9$.

Generate neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = 7 - 1 = 6$.

$$
f(6) = (6-4)^{2} = 4, \quad \Delta E = 4 - 9 = -5
$$

Accept. $x \leftarrow 6$, $x^{*} \leftarrow 6$, $f(x^{*}) = 4$.

Cool:

$$
T \leftarrow 0.8 \times 51.2 = 40.96
$$

---

**Final Result after 4 iterations:** $x^{*} = 6$, $f(x^{*}) = 4$.

> [!NOTE]
> The algorithm is **converging toward the true global minimum at $x = 4$**. The single uphill move in Iteration 2 (which worsened $f$ from 9 to 16) demonstrates **why SA works** — it deliberately "climbs out" of a local feature to keep searching.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Temperature on Acceptance

| Scenario | $\Delta E$ | $T$ | $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ | Practical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small uphill, high $T$ | 2 | 100 | $0.9802$ | Almost always accept → explore |
| Small uphill, low $T$ | 2 | 1 | $0.1353$ | Rarely accept → exploit |
| Large uphill, high $T$ | 50 | 100 | $0.6065$ | Often accept (escapes deep trap) |
| Large uphill, low $T$ | 50 | 1 | $1.93 \times 10^{-22}$ | Effectively reject → no escape |

This table is **exactly the type** the KTU board examiners use to test conceptual understanding in Part A.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Simulated Annealing Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize x0, T0, alpha] --> B[Set x = x0, x_best = x0, T = T0]
    B --> C{Stopping criterion met?}
    C -- Yes --> Z[Return x_best as final solution]
    C -- No --> D[Generate neighbor x_new from x]
    D --> E[Compute delta_E = f(x_new) - f(x)]
    E --> F{delta_E less than or equal to 0?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Accept: x becomes x_new]
    F -- No --> H[Generate r in U(0,1)]
    H --> I{r less than exp of minus delta_E over T?}
    I -- Yes --> G
    I -- No --> J[Reject: keep current x]
    G --> K[Update x_best if f(x_new) is better]
    J --> L[Cool: T becomes alpha times T]
    K --> L
    L --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Initialization
        I1[Initial Solution Generator]
        I2[Temperature Controller T0]
        I3[Cooling Schedule alpha]
    end
    subgraph CoreLoop[Inner Loop - Per Iteration]
        P1[Neighborhood Generator]
        P2[Objective Function Evaluator]
        P3[Acceptance Decision Module]
        P4[Best Solution Tracker]
        P5[Temperature Update Module]
    end
    subgraph Termination
        T1[Convergence Check]
        T2[Output Best Solution]
    end
    I1 --> CoreLoop
    I2 --> CoreLoop
    I3 --> P5
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> T1
    P5 --> P1
    T1 --> T2
    T1 -- not converged --> P1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Acceptance Behaviour vs. Temperature

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> HighT
    HighT --> MidT: T decreases geometrically
    MidT --> LowT: T decreases further
    HighT: High Temperature\nBehavior: Random walk\nAccept uphill with P approx 1
    MidT: Moderate Temperature\nBehavior: Selective exploration\nAccept uphill with moderate P
    LowT: Low Temperature\nBehavior: Greedy local search\nAccept uphill with P approx 0
    LowT --> [*]: Converge to optimum
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read these diagrams as a loop**, not a one-shot. SA's power lies in **repeatedly visiting** the three temperature regimes across thousands of iterations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Simulated Annealing. Mention the physical analogy it is based on.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Simulated Annealing (SA) is a **probabilistic metaheuristic algorithm** used to find the global optimum of an objective function. It is based on the **metallurgical process of annealing**, in which a metal is **heated to a high temperature** and then **gradually cooled** so that its atoms settle into a low-energy, defect-free crystalline state. The algorithm mimics this by:
- Treating the **objective function value** as energy $E$.
- Introducing a **temperature parameter $T$** that is gradually reduced.
- Allowing **uphill (worse) moves** with probability $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$ to escape local minima.

*(Examiner's note: The metal→energy and temperature→control parameter mapping should be explicit for full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the acceptance probability formula used in Simulated Annealing and explain the role of the temperature parameter $T$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The acceptance probability for a **worse move** ($\Delta E > 0$) is given by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$
P(\text{accept}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{T}\right)
$$

where $\Delta E = f(x_{\text{new}}) - f(x) > 0$ and $T > 0$ is the current temperature.

**Role of $T$:**
- When $T$ is **high** (start of search), $P \to 1$ — the algorithm behaves like a **random search**, freely exploring the space and escaping local minima.
- When $T$ is **low** (end of search), $P \to 0$ — the algorithm **rejects uphill moves**, behaving like a **greedy local search** that fine-tunes near the best-found solution.
- As $T$ is gradually cooled, the algorithm smoothly transitions from **exploration to exploitation**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Simulated Annealing algorithm** in detail. Discuss the role of the **cooling schedule** and state the **Boltzmann acceptance criterion**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using Simulated Annealing, **minimize** $f(x) = (x - 5)^{2}$ for **3 iterations** starting from $x_0 = 0$, with $T_0 = 50$, $\alpha = 0.9$, and a neighbor generation rule of $x_{\text{new}} = x + 1$. Use $r = 0.5$ whenever needed. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose an initial feasible solution $x_0$, initial temperature $T_0 > 0$ (large), a cooling factor $\alpha \in (0,1)$ (typically 0.8–0.99), and a stopping criterion (e.g., $T < T_{\min}$ or fixed iteration count).

**Step 2 — Main loop:**

(i) Generate a **neighbor** $x_{\text{new}}$ from the current $x$ by a small perturbation (e.g., adding random noise).

(ii) Compute $\Delta E = f(x_{\text{new}}) - f(x)$.

(iii) **Acceptance test:**
$$
x = \begin{cases} x_{\text{new}}, & \text{if } \Delta E \leq 0 \\[4pt] x_{\text{new}}, & \text{if } \Delta E > 0 \text{ and } r < \exp(-\Delta E / T) \\[4pt] x, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

(iv) Track the **best solution** $x^{*}$ seen so far.

(v) **Cool:** $T \leftarrow \alpha T$.

**Role of Cooling Schedule:** It controls the **exploration–exploitation trade-off**. A fast cooling (small $\alpha$) may trap the algorithm in a local minimum; a slow cooling (large $\alpha$) ensures near-global convergence but is computationally expensive. The **geometric schedule** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ is the most widely used in practice.

**Boltzmann Acceptance Criterion:** $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$. This probabilistic rule allows the algorithm to **accept worse solutions** early on, providing the **escape mechanism** from local optima.

---

**(b) Numerical Iteration (7 marks):**

**Setup:** $f(x) = (x-5)^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $T_0 = 50$, $\alpha = 0.9$, $x_{\text{new}} = x + 1$.

$$
f(x_0) = f(0) = (0-5)^2 = 25
$$

---

**Iteration 1:** $T = 50$, $x = 0$, $f(x) = 25$.

Neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = 0 + 1 = 1$.

$$
f(1) = (1-5)^2 = 16, \quad \Delta E = 16 - 25 = -9
$$

$\Delta E \leq 0 \Rightarrow$ **Accept**. $x \leftarrow 1$, $x^{*} \leftarrow 1$.

Cool: $T \leftarrow 0.9 \times 50 = 45$.

---

**Iteration 2:** $T = 45$, $x = 1$, $f(x) = 16$.

Neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = 1 + 1 = 2$.

$$
f(2) = (2-5)^2 = 9, \quad \Delta E = 9 - 16 = -7
$$

$\Delta E \leq 0 \Rightarrow$ **Accept**. $x \leftarrow 2$, $x^{*} \leftarrow 2$.

Cool: $T \leftarrow 0.9 \times 45 = 40.5$.

---

**Iteration 3:** $T = 40.5$, $x = 2$, $f(x) = 9$.

Neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = 2 + 1 = 3$.

$$
f(3) = (3-5)^2 = 4, \quad \Delta E = 4 - 9 = -5
$$

$\Delta E \leq 0 \Rightarrow$ **Accept**. $x \leftarrow 3$, $x^{*} \leftarrow 3$.

Cool: $T \leftarrow 0.9 \times 40.5 = 36.45$.

---

**Result after 3 iterations:** $x^{*} = 3$, $f(x^{*}) = 4$. *(True global minimum is at $x=5$, $f=0$; the algorithm is still approaching it.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget to update the best solution tracker $x^{*}$** when an accepted move is not better than $x^{*}$ but is better than the current $x$. Always check $f(x_{\text{new}}) < f(x^{*})$ separately. Also, students mistakenly **divide by $k_B$** in the acceptance formula — in computational SA, $k_B$ is absorbed into $T$; just use $T$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare the behaviour of Simulated Annealing at **$T \to \infty$**, **$T \to 0$**, and at **moderate $T$**. What type of search does the algorithm reduce to in each limit? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Minimize $f(x) = x^{2} - 6x + 10$ using SA for **4 iterations** starting from $x_0 = 0$, $T_0 = 20$, $\alpha = 0.8$, neighbor rule $x_{\text{new}} = x - 1$, and use $r = 0.6$ whenever an uphill move is encountered. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Behaviour at Three Temperature Regimes (7 marks):**

**Case 1: $T \to \infty$ (very high temperature).**
Then $\exp(-\Delta E / T) \to \exp(0) = 1$. The algorithm **accepts every move** with probability ≈ 1, irrespective of whether it improves or worsens the objective. It reduces to a **pure random walk** over the search space. The role of $f(x)$ is essentially ignored. This regime is useful for **global exploration** and ensures no region of the search space is permanently ignored.

**Case 2: $T \to 0$ (very low temperature).**
Then $\exp(-\Delta E / T) \to 0$ for any $\Delta E > 0$. The algorithm **rejects all uphill moves** and only accepts moves that strictly decrease $f(x)$. It reduces to a **pure greedy descent / local search**, equivalent to **steepest descent in the discrete neighborhood**. This regime is useful for **local exploitation** — fine-tuning a candidate solution.

**Case 3: Moderate $T$ (intermediate temperature).**
Here $P = \exp(-\Delta E / T)$ takes values strictly between 0 and 1. The algorithm **probabilistically accepts some uphill moves** while rejecting others. The probability of acceptance is higher for **small $\Delta E$** and **higher $T$**. This is the regime in which SA is uniquely useful — it balances **exploration** (uphill moves) and **exploitation** (downhill moves).

The cooling schedule **smoothly transitions** the algorithm from Case 1 to Case 3 to Case 2 across the run, which is the essence of the SA method.

---

**(b) Numerical Iteration (7 marks):**

**Setup:** $f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 10$, $x_0 = 0$, $T_0 = 20$, $\alpha = 0.8$, $x_{\text{new}} = x - 1$, $r = 0.6$.

$$
f(x_0) = f(0) = 0 - 0 + 10 = 10
$$

*(Note: This is a convex parabola with minimum at $x = 3$, $f(3) = 1$.)*

---

**Iteration 1:** $T = 20$, $x = 0$, $f(x) = 10$.

Neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = 0 - 1 = -1$.

$$
f(-1) = 1 + 6 + 10 = 17, \quad \Delta E = 17 - 10 = +7
$$

Uphill move. Compute:

$$
P = \exp(-7/20) = \exp(-0.35) \approx 0.7047
$$

Since $r = 0.6 < P = 0.7047$, **accept the worse move**. $x \leftarrow -1$.

Cool: $T \leftarrow 0.8 \times 20 = 16$.

*(Best so far: $x^{*} = 0$, $f = 10$.)*

---

**Iteration 2:** $T = 16$, $x = -1$, $f(x) = 17$.

Neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = -1 - 1 = -2$.

$$
f(-2) = 4 + 12 + 10 = 26, \quad \Delta E = 26 - 17 = +9
$$

Uphill. Compute:

$$
P = \exp(-9/16) = \exp(-0.5625) \approx 0.5698
$$

Since $r = 0.6 \not< P = 0.5698$, **reject**. $x$ remains $-1$.

Cool: $T \leftarrow 0.8 \times 16 = 12.8$.

---

**Iteration 3:** $T = 12.8$, $x = -1$, $f(x) = 17$.

Neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = -1 - 1 = -2$.

$$
f(-2) = 26, \quad \Delta E = 26 - 17 = +9
$$

Uphill. Compute:

$$
P = \exp(-9/12.8) = \exp(-0.7031) \approx 0.4950
$$

Since $r = 0.6 \not< 0.4950$, **reject**. $x$ remains $-1$.

Cool: $T \leftarrow 0.8 \times 12.8 = 10.24$.

---

**Iteration 4:** $T = 10.24$, $x = -1$, $f(x) = 17$.

Neighbor: $x_{\text{new}} = -1 - 1 = -2$.

$$
f(-2) = 26, \quad \Delta E = +9
$$

Uphill. Compute:

$$
P = \exp(-9/10.24) = \exp(-0.8789) \approx 0.4154
$$

Since $r = 0.6 \not< 0.4154$, **reject**. $x$ remains $-1$.

Cool: $T \leftarrow 0.8 \times 10.24 = 8.192$.

---

**Result after 4 iterations:** $x^{*} = 0$, $f(x^{*}) = 10$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall in Q(b):** Three common errors:
> 1. **Sign of $\Delta E$** — students sometimes compute $f(x) - f(x_{\text{new}})$ and then misapply the rule. Always $\Delta E = f(\text{new}) - f(\text{current})$.
> 2. **Confusion over $r < P$ vs. $r > P$** — the rule is $r < \exp(-\Delta E / T)$, not $r >$.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply cooling** after every iteration, even when a move is rejected.
>
> **Examiner's Pitfall in Q(a):** Students often just say "high T → explore, low T → exploit" without naming the **limiting algorithms** (random walk and greedy descent) or computing the limit of the exponential. Full marks require **explicit limits and the reduced search type**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for SA (Simulated Annealing)**

- **Inspiration:** Metallurgical annealing — heat then slow-cool to reach a low-energy crystalline state.
- **Energy analogue:** $E \leftrightarrow f(x)$ (objective function value).
- **Acceptance rule:** $\Delta E \leq 0$ → always accept; $\Delta E > 0$ → accept with probability $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$.
- **Cooling schedule (geometric):** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, with $\alpha \in (0.8, 0.99)$.
- **Closed-form temperature:** $T_k = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{k}$.
- **Three behavioural regimes:**
  - $T \to \infty$ → random walk.
  - Moderate $T$ → probabilistic balance of exploration and exploitation.
  - $T \to 0$ → greedy local search.
- **Why SA escapes local optima:** the **Boltzmann probability** permits uphill moves, which pure gradient methods forbid.
- **Algorithm inputs:** initial solution $x_0$, initial temperature $T_0$, cooling factor $\alpha$, stopping rule, neighborhood definition.
- **Algorithm output:** best-so-far solution $x^{*}$.
- **Convergence guarantee (theoretical):** with logarithmic cooling $T_k = T_0 / \log(k+1)$, SA converges in probability to the global optimum.
- **Common errors to avoid in exams:**
  - Forgetting the sign convention of $\Delta E$.
  - Mixing up $r < P$ vs. $r > P$.
  - Omitting the best-solution tracker $x^{*}$.
  - Treating $T$ as a physical temperature with units — it is a **dimensionless control parameter** in computational SA.
- **Comparison with other metaheuristics:** SA uses a **single solution** and **probabilistic acceptance**; Genetic Algorithms use a **population** and **probabilistic selection**; Tabu Search uses **memory structures**. All three can escape local optima but via different mechanisms.
- **One-line exam definition:** "Simulated Annealing is a single-solution probabilistic metaheuristic that mimics the physical annealing of metals, using the Boltzmann acceptance criterion to escape local optima while gradually reducing a temperature control parameter."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** is a population-based, stochastic, meta-heuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling. It was first proposed by **James Kennedy** (social psychologist) and **Russell Eberhart** (electrical engineer) in **1995**.

In PSO, a swarm of candidate solutions, called **particles**, moves through an $n$-dimensional search space. Each particle adjusts its trajectory dynamically based on:
1. Its own **inertial velocity** (the momentum it carries from previous iterations),
2. Its **cognitive component** (its own best-known position, denoted $p_{best}$), and
3. The **social component** (the best position found by the entire swarm or its neighbors, denoted $g_{best}$).

The algorithm is **derivative-free**, requires only a fitness function evaluation, and is classified under the broader umbrella of **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** algorithms under Computational Intelligence.

### Formal Definition
Let the objective be to find $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$ such that
$$f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in S} f(\mathbf{x})$$
where $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is the feasible search space. A PSO algorithm maintains a population of $N$ particles, each having a position vector $\mathbf{x}_i^{(t)}$ and a velocity vector $\mathbf{v}_i^{(t)}$, updating both based on local and global bests at each iteration $t$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bird Flock Analogy (Most Important Intuition for KTU Viva)**

Imagine a flock of birds searching for a hidden food source in a vast field. **No bird knows where the food is**, but each bird has a sense of how far it is from the food (this represents the **fitness function**).

- Every bird remembers the **closest it has personally ever come** to the food → this is the **personal best ($p_{best}$)** — *cognitive memory*.
- Every bird in the flock also knows where the **closest any bird has ever come** to the food → this is the **global best ($g_{best}$)** — *social knowledge*.
- Each bird then flies in a new direction that is a **weighted compromise** between:
  1. Continuing in its **current direction** (inertia),
  2. Moving back toward its **own best location** (cognitive pull),
  3. Moving toward the **swarm's best location** (social pull).

This simple, beautiful rule, when applied iteratively to a population, produces remarkably powerful global optimization behavior without any gradient information.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked to explain PSO in 3 marks, always begin with the *bird flocking analogy*, then state the two update equations, and finally mention $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Particle | $i$ | A single candidate solution in the swarm |
| Position | $\mathbf{x}_i$ | Current location of particle $i$ in the search space |
| Velocity | $\mathbf{v}_i$ | Current rate of change of position of particle $i$ |
| Personal Best | $p_{best,i}$ | Best position ever visited by particle $i$ |
| Global Best | $g_{best}$ | Best position ever visited by the entire swarm |
| Swarm Size | $N$ | Total number of particles (typically 20–50) |
| Inertia Weight | $w$ | Controls the impact of previous velocity |
| Cognitive Coefficient | $c_1$ | Pull toward $p_{best}$ |
| Social Coefficient | $c_2$ | Pull toward $g_{best}$ |
| Iteration | $t$ | Current generation / time step |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Recommended Values (Shi & Eberhart, 1998):**
> - $w$ (inertia weight) = **0.7** (linearly decreasing from 0.9 to 0.4 is also common)
> - $c_1$ (cognitive) = **1.5** to **2.0**
> - $c_2$ (social) = **1.5** to **2.0**
> - $N$ (swarm size) = **20 to 50**

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Philosophy: The "Why" Behind PSO

PSO is built on three core principles drawn from **social psychology** and **artificial life**:

1. **Evaluative (Proximity):** The population is able to evaluate the quality of solutions via a fitness function.
2. **Quality (Quality Response):** The population responds to quality variations in the environment.
3. **Principle of Stability & Adaptability (Kennedy & Eberhart):** The population must be stable enough to retain beneficial behavior patterns, yet adaptive enough to shift strategies when warranted.

Mathematically, every particle $i$ at iteration $t$ is characterized by:
- A **position vector**: $\mathbf{x}_i^{(t)} = (x_{i1}^{(t)}, x_{i2}^{(t)}, \ldots, x_{in}^{(t)}) \in \mathbb{R}^n$
- A **velocity vector**: $\mathbf{v}_i^{(t)} = (v_{i1}^{(t)}, v_{i2}^{(t)}, \ldots, v_{in}^{(t)}) \in \mathbb{R}^n$

At each iteration, three bests are tracked:
$$\begin{aligned}
p_{best,i}^{(t)} &= \arg\min_{\tau \leq t} f(\mathbf{x}_i^{(\tau)}) \\
g_{best}^{(t)} &= \arg\min_{j \in \{1,\ldots,N\}} f(p_{best,j}^{(t)})
\end{aligned}$$

## 2.2 The Two Governing Update Equations (Heart of PSO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two equations are **the single most frequently asked topic** in any KTU PSO question. Memorize both forms.

### (A) Velocity Update Equation

For the $d$-th dimension of particle $i$ at iteration $t+1$:

$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = w \cdot v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 \left( p_{best,id}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)} \right) + c_2 r_2 \left( g_{best,d}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)} \right)$$

### (B) Position Update Equation

$$x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + v_{id}^{(t+1)}$$

where:
- $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ (particle index)
- $d = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ (dimension index)
- $w$ = **inertia weight** (controls exploration vs. exploitation trade-off)
- $c_1$ = **cognitive acceleration coefficient**
- $c_2$ = **social acceleration coefficient**
- $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0, 1)$ = **uniformly distributed random numbers** in $[0, 1]$
- $p_{best,id}^{(t)}$ = personal best of particle $i$ in dimension $d$
- $g_{best,d}^{(t)}$ = global best of the swarm in dimension $d$

## 2.3 Detailed Component Breakdown

### (1) The Inertia Term: $w \cdot v_{id}^{(t)}$
- **$w$ = inertia weight** — Shi & Eberhart (1998) introduced this.
- If $w$ is large → particle flies across the space (**exploration**, global search).
- If $w$ is small → particle sticks to local region (**exploitation**, local search).
- **Common strategy:** Linearly decreasing $w$ from $0.9$ to $0.4$ over iterations.
  $$w^{(t)} = w_{max} - \frac{(w_{max} - w_{min}) \cdot t}{T_{max}}$$

### (2) The Cognitive Term: $c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x)$
- Pulls the particle back toward **its own best-known location**.
- Encodes the "**nostalgia**" — particle remembers and learns from its own past.
- Coefficient $c_1$ controls how strongly a particle trusts its own memory.

### (3) The Social Term: $c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x)$
- Pulls the particle toward the **best-known location of the entire swarm**.
- Encodes the "**conformity**" — particle imitates the swarm's best.
- Coefficient $c_2$ controls how strongly a particle trusts its neighbors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are $r_1$ and $r_2$ included?**
> The random numbers $r_1, r_2 \in [0, 1]$ introduce **stochasticity** into the search, ensuring diversity and preventing premature convergence to local optima. Without them, the algorithm becomes deterministic and may get stuck.

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Velocity Update** | $v_{id}^{(t+1)} = w v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{best,id} - x_{id}^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best,d} - x_{id}^{(t)})$ | Determines next-step velocity of particle $i$ in dim $d$ |
| 2 | **Position Update** | $x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + v_{id}^{(t+1)}$ | Moves the particle using updated velocity |
| 3 | **Inertia Weight Decay** | $w^{(t)} = w_{max} - (w_{max} - w_{min}) t / T_{max}$ | Linearly decreases $w$ for global-to-local refinement |
| 4 | **Velocity Clipping** | $v_{id} \in [-v_{max}, \; v_{max}]$ | Prevents explosion of particle velocity (often $v_{max} = k \cdot x_{max}$, $k \in [0.1, 0.2]$) |
| 5 | **Standard Parameter Set** | $w = 0.7, \; c_1 = 1.5, \; c_2 = 1.5, \; N = 20\text{–}50$ | Default recommended values |
| 6 | **Convergence Condition** | $\vert v_{id}^{(t+1)} \vert \to 0$ or $\Delta f < \epsilon$ | Stops when particles stop moving or fitness plateaus |
| 7 | **Trust Parameter Form** (Clerc, 1999) | $v_{id}^{(t+1)} = \chi [v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x)]$ | Constriction factor $\chi$ ensures convergence: $\chi = 2k / \vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert$, $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$ |

## 2.5 Why PSO is Important in Engineering

PSO is one of the most widely used nature-inspired optimizers in engineering because:

1. **Simplicity:** Only **two equations** per particle per iteration. Implementation is trivial.
2. **Derivative-free:** No need to compute $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$, ideal for noisy, discontinuous, or black-box functions.
3. **Parallelizability:** All particle updates are independent → trivially parallel on GPU/multicore.
4. **Few tuning parameters:** Only $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$, and $N$ — far less than GA (which has crossover rate, mutation rate, selection pressure, etc.).
5. **Wide applications:** Neural network training, antenna design, power system dispatch, PID controller tuning, feature selection, job-shop scheduling, image segmentation, and **economic load dispatch** (a KTU-favorite application!).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application to Remember for KTU:**
> **Economic Load Dispatch in Power Systems** — PSO is used to minimize fuel cost $\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i(P_i)$ subject to equality and inequality constraints on power generation. GA struggles here due to constraint handling; PSO handles it elegantly with penalty functions.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Update Equation (Conceptual Derivation)

The standard PSO velocity update can be **derived from first principles** by combining three intuitive forces acting on each particle:

### Step 1: The Inertial Force
Without any external influence, a particle should continue in its current direction of motion at the same speed. This is simply **Newton's First Law of Motion**:
$$v_{inertia} = w \cdot v_{id}^{(t)}$$
where $w$ is a scaling factor (inertia weight) introduced by Shi & Eberhart (1998) to allow control of the magnitude.

### Step 2: The Cognitive Force (Personal Experience)
The particle should be **attracted to the best location it has personally visited**, $p_{best,id}$. The "pull" toward this location is proportional to the **distance** between the current position and $p_{best,id}$:
$$v_{cognitive} = c_1 r_1 \left( p_{best,id}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)} \right)$$
- $c_1$ is the cognitive acceleration constant.
- $r_1 \sim U(0,1)$ adds stochasticity (the particle doesn't always move with full cognitive force — it might hesitate).

### Step 3: The Social Force (Swarm Knowledge)
The particle should also be **attracted to the best location the entire swarm has discovered**, $g_{best,d}$:
$$v_{social} = c_2 r_2 \left( g_{best,d}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)} \right)$$

### Step 4: Superposition of Forces
Adding all three components vectorially (superposition principle):
$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = \underbrace{w \cdot v_{id}^{(t)}}_{\text{inertia}} + \underbrace{c_1 r_1 (p_{best,id}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)})}_{\text{cognitive}} + \underbrace{c_2 r_2 (g_{best,d}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)})}_{\text{social}}$$

### Step 5: Position Update (Integration)
Since velocity is the rate of change of position (discrete time):
$$x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + v_{id}^{(t+1)}$$

This is the discrete-time equivalent of $dx/dt = v$.

## 3.2 Convergence Analysis of PSO (Stability Condition)

We can derive a **convergence condition** for the simplified deterministic PSO (set $r_1 = r_2 = 0.5$, $p_{best} = p$, $g_{best} = g$).

### Step 1: Combine Position and Velocity
Substituting $v_{id}^{(t+1)}$ into $x_{id}^{(t+1)}$:
$$x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + w v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p - x_{id}^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g - x_{id}^{(t)})$$

### Step 2: Define an Effective Constant
Let $\phi_1 = c_1 r_1$ and $\phi_2 = c_2 r_2$. Then:
$$x_{id}^{(t+1)} = (1 - \phi_1 - \phi_2) x_{id}^{(t)} + w v_{id}^{(t)} + \phi_1 p + \phi_2 g$$

### Step 3: Form the Recurrence
Define the deviation $\delta_{id}^{(t)} = x_{id}^{(t)} - x^*$ where $x^*$ is the equilibrium point. Substituting and simplifying yields a **2nd-order linear homogeneous recurrence**:
$$\delta_{id}^{(t+1)} = (1 + w - \phi_1 - \phi_2) \delta_{id}^{(t)} - w \delta_{id}^{(t-1)}$$

### Step 4: Characteristic Equation
Assume solution of the form $\delta_{id}^{(t)} = \lambda^t$:
$$\lambda^2 - (1 + w - \phi_1 - \phi_2) \lambda + w = 0$$

### Step 5: Apply Jury's Stability Criterion
For asymptotic convergence, **both roots must lie inside the unit circle**. Jury's test yields:
$$-1 < w < 1, \qquad 0 < \phi_1 + \phi_2 < 2(1 + w)$$

### Step 6: Special Case — Clerc's Constriction Factor
If $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ and $\phi = c_1 + c_2 = 4.1$, the constriction factor is:
$$\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert} = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - 4.1 - \sqrt{16.81 - 16.4} \vert} = \frac{2}{\vert -2.1 - 0.641 \vert} = \frac{2}{2.741} \approx 0.729$$
This guarantees convergence without velocity clamping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Convergence requires $w < 1$ and bounded $\phi_1 + \phi_2$. Setting $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.4962$ is a known "magic" combination that ensures guaranteed convergence (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002).

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ using PSO with 3 particles. Initial positions: $P_1 = (3, 2)$, $P_2 = (-1, 4)$, $P_3 = (2, -1)$. Initial velocities: $V_1 = (0.5, -0.5)$, $V_2 = (0.2, 0.3)$, $V_3 = (-0.4, 0.1)$. Parameters: $w = 0.5$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.0$. Compute one full iteration. Use $r_1 = r_2 = 0.5$ for all particles.

### Step 1: Compute Fitness
$$\begin{aligned}
f(P_1) &= 3^2 + 2^2 = 9 + 4 = 13 \\
f(P_2) &= (-1)^2 + 4^2 = 1 + 16 = 17 \\
f(P_3) &= 2^2 + (-1)^2 = 4 + 1 = 5
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Initialize $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$
Since this is the first iteration, $p_{best,i} = \mathbf{x}_i^{(0)}$.
$$\begin{aligned}
p_{best,1} &= (3, 2), \quad p_{best,2} = (-1, 4), \quad p_{best,3} = (2, -1) \\
g_{best} &= \arg\min\{13, 17, 5\} = P_3 = (2, -1)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Update Velocity for $P_1$
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{1,1}^{(1)} &= w v_{1,1}^{(0)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{best,1,1} - x_{1,1}^{(0)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best,1} - x_{1,1}^{(0)}) \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 0.5 + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (3 - 3) + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (2 - 3) \\
&= 0.25 + 0 - 0.5 = -0.25
\end{aligned}$$
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{1,2}^{(1)} &= 0.5 \cdot (-0.5) + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (2 - 2) + 1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (-1 - 2) \\
&= -0.25 + 0 - 1.5 = -1.75
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Update Position for $P_1$
$$\begin{aligned}
x_{1,1}^{(1)} &= 3 + (-0.25) = 2.75 \\
x_{1,2}^{(1)} &= 2 + (-1.75) = 0.25
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Update Velocity for $P_2$
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{2,1}^{(1)} &= 0.5 \cdot 0.2 + 0.5 \cdot (-1 - (-1)) + 0.5 \cdot (2 - (-1)) \\
&= 0.1 + 0 + 1.5 = 1.6 \\
v_{2,2}^{(1)} &= 0.5 \cdot 0.3 + 0.5 \cdot (4 - 4) + 0.5 \cdot (-1 - 4) \\
&= 0.15 + 0 - 2.5 = -2.35
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Update Position for $P_2$
$$\begin{aligned}
x_{2,1}^{(1)} &= -1 + 1.6 = 0.6 \\
x_{2,2}^{(1)} &= 4 - 2.35 = 1.65
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: Update Velocity for $P_3$
$$\begin{aligned}
v_{3,1}^{(1)} &= 0.5 \cdot (-0.4) + 0.5 \cdot (2 - 2) + 0.5 \cdot (2 - 2) \\
&= -0.2 + 0 + 0 = -0.2 \\
v_{3,2}^{(1)} &= 0.5 \cdot 0.1 + 0.5 \cdot (-1 - (-1)) + 0.5 \cdot (-1 - (-1)) \\
&= 0.05 + 0 + 0 = 0.05
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8: Update Position for $P_3$
$$\begin{aligned}
x_{3,1}^{(1)} &= 2 + (-0.2) = 1.8 \\
x_{3,2}^{(1)} &= -1 + 0.05 = -0.95
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 9: New Fitness Values
$$\begin{aligned}
f(P_1^{(1)}) &= 2.75^2 + 0.25^2 = 7.5625 + 0.0625 = 7.625 \\
f(P_2^{(1)}) &= 0.6^2 + 1.65^2 = 0.36 + 2.7225 = 3.0825 \\
f(P_3^{(1)}) &= 1.8^2 + (-0.95)^2 = 3.24 + 0.9025 = 4.1425
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 10: Update $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$
- $f(P_1^{(1)}) = 7.625 < 13$, so $p_{best,1} \leftarrow (2.75, 0.25)$.
- $f(P_2^{(1)}) = 3.0825 < 17$, so $p_{best,2} \leftarrow (0.6, 1.65)$.
- $f(P_3^{(1)}) = 4.1425 > 5$, so $p_{best,3}$ remains $(2, -1)$.
- New $g_{best} = (0.6, 1.65)$ with $f = 3.0825$.

> [!TIP]
> **Notice:** All particles have moved **closer to the origin** (which is the true global minimum at $(0,0)$ for this sphere function). This empirically demonstrates the convergence behavior of PSO.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture: How PSO Works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Init["Initialize swarm of N particles with random positions xi and velocities vi"]
    Init --> FitnessEval["Evaluate fitness f xi for every particle"]
    FitnessEval --> InitBest["Set pbesti = xi and gbest = argmin f pbestj"]
    InitBest --> LoopCheck{"Termination criterion met? t greater than Tmax or convergence?"}
    LoopCheck -- No --> VelUpdate["For each particle i and dimension d: vid t plus 1 equals w times vid t plus c1 r1 pbestid minus xid plus c2 r2 gbestd minus xid"]
    VelUpdate --> ClipVel{"Clamp vid within vmax to minus vmax"}
    ClipVel -- Yes --> PosUpdate["Update position: xid t plus 1 equals xid t plus vid t plus 1"]
    ClipVel -- No --> PosUpdate
    PosUpdate --> NewFitness["Recompute fitness at new positions"]
    NewFitness --> PbestUpdate["Update pbesti if f xi t plus 1 less than f pbesti"]
    PbestUpdate --> GbestUpdate["Update gbest if any pbesti better than current gbest"]
    GbestUpdate --> IncT["Increment iteration t equals t plus 1"]
    IncT --> LoopCheck
    LoopCheck -- Yes --> Output(["Return gbest as optimal solution"])
    Output([End])
```

## 4.2 Force Diagram on a Particle (Intuitive Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Particle xid at iteration t] -->|v id t inertia component| V[New Velocity vid t plus 1]
    Pbest[pbestid cognitive memory] -->|c1 r1 attraction| V
    Gbest[gbestd social knowledge] -->|c2 r2 attraction| V
    V -->|position update| Pnext[New Position xid t plus 1]
    P -->|position update| Pnext
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of PSO Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Initialization
        A1[Random Position Generator] --> A2[Particle Position Matrix X]
        A3[Random Velocity Generator] --> A4[Particle Velocity Matrix V]
    end
    subgraph Evaluation
        A2 --> B1[Fitness Function Module]
        B1 --> B2[Fitness Vector f X]
    end
    subgraph Memory
        B2 --> C1[pbest Tracker]
        B2 --> C2[gbest Tracker]
    end
    subgraph Update Engine
        C1 --> D1[Velocity Calculator]
        C2 --> D1
        A4 --> D1
        A2 --> D1
        D1 --> D2[Velocity Clamper]
        D2 --> D3[Position Updater]
        A2 --> D3
        D3 --> E1[New Position Matrix]
    end
    E1 --> B1
```

## 4.4 Exploration vs. Exploitation Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High w greater than 1.2] -->|Strong inertia| B[Exploration: Particles fly far, search new regions]
    C[Low w less than 0.4] -->|Weak inertia| D[Exploitation: Particles converge to best region]
    E[Balanced w equals 0.7 to 0.9] -->|Optimal balance| F[Global Convergence to Optimum]
    B --> G[May miss optimum]
    D --> H[May converge to local optimum]
    F --> I[Robust Optimization Success]
```

## 4.5 Variant Family Tree of PSO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSO[Standard PSO Kennedy and Eberhart 1995] --> IWPSO[Inertia Weight PSO Shi 1998]
    PSO --> CPSO[Constriction Factor PSO Clerc 2002]
    IWPSO --> LDW[Linearly Decreasing w PSO]
    IWPSO --> AIW[Adaptive Inertia Weight PSO]
    PSO --> TRIB[Triplet Topology PSO]
    PSO --> BBPSO[Bare Bones PSO Kennedy 2003]
    CPSO --> TVAC[Time Varying Acceleration Coefficients PSO]
    PSO --> MPSO[Multi-Swarm PSO]
    PSO --> CPSOS[Comprehensive Learning PSO]
    PSO --> QPSO[Quantum PSO Sun 2004]
```
