# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Particle Swarm Optimization. List its main parameters.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **PSO Definition:** Particle Swarm Optimization is a population-based stochastic optimization technique inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling, developed by Kennedy and Eberhart in 1995. Each particle in the swarm represents a candidate solution and moves through the search space with a velocity adjusted based on its own best position ($p_{best}$) and the global best position ($g_{best}$).

**Main Parameters:**
1. **Swarm size $N$** — typically 20–50.
2. **Inertia weight $w$** — typically 0.7; controls exploration vs. exploitation.
3. **Cognitive coefficient $c_1$** — typically 1.5; pull toward $p_{best}$.
4. **Social coefficient $c_2$** — typically 1.5; pull toward $g_{best}$.
5. **Maximum velocity $v_{max}$** — prevents velocity explosion.
6. **Maximum iterations $T_{max}$** — termination criterion.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**What is the role of inertia weight $w$ in PSO?**

**Model Answer:**
The inertia weight $w$ was introduced by Shi and Eberhart (1998) to balance **exploration** (global search) and **exploitation** (local search) in PSO.
- A **large $w$** ($> 1$) promotes exploration — particles fly far, exploring new regions.
- A **small $w$** ($< 0.4$) promotes exploitation — particles stay near known good regions.
- A **moderate $w$** ($\approx 0.7$ to $0.9$) gives the best performance.
- **Linearly decreasing $w$** from $0.9$ to $0.4$ across iterations is the most common strategy:
  $$w^{(t)} = w_{max} - \frac{(w_{max} - w_{min}) \cdot t}{T_{max}}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Important KTU Rule:** In Part B Module questions, students are given an **internal choice** between two full questions. Here, we provide both.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of inertia weight, cognitive and social components in the velocity update equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A particle is at position $x = 5$ with velocity $v = 2$. Its $p_{best} = 3$ and the global $g_{best} = 1$. Using $w = 0.5$, $c_1 = 2$, $c_2 = 2$, $r_1 = 0.4$, $r_2 = 0.6$, compute the new velocity and position. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Origin & Inspiration:**
PSO was proposed by Kennedy and Eberhart in 1995, inspired by the choreographed movement of bird flocks. The key insight is that **social sharing of information** provides an evolutionary advantage.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize a swarm of $N$ particles with random positions $\mathbf{x}_i$ and velocities $\mathbf{v}_i$.
2. Evaluate the fitness function $f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ for each particle.
3. For each particle, set $p_{best,i} = \mathbf{x}_i$.
4. Set $g_{best}$ to the position of the best particle in the swarm.
5. **Repeat until convergence:**
   - Update the velocity of each particle using:
     $$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = w v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{best,id}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best,d}^{(t)} - x_{id}^{(t)})$$
   - Clamp velocity: $v_{id} \in [-v_{max}, v_{max}]$.
   - Update position: $x_{id}^{(t+1)} = x_{id}^{(t)} + v_{id}^{(t+1)}$.
   - Update $p_{best,i}$ if $f(\mathbf{x}_i^{(t+1)}) < f(p_{best,i})$.
   - Update $g_{best}$ if any $f(p_{best,i}) < f(g_{best})$.
6. Return $g_{best}$ as the optimum.

**Role of Components:**

| Component | Term | Function |
|-----------|------|----------|
| Inertia weight $w$ | $w \cdot v_{id}^{(t)}$ | Carries forward the previous velocity; controls momentum and balance of exploration vs. exploitation |
| Cognitive $c_1$ | $c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x)$ | Pulls the particle back toward its own best-found position (individual learning) |
| Social $c_2$ | $c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x)$ | Pulls the particle toward the swarm's best-found position (social learning) |

A typical recommended setting is $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.5$ (or 1.49618 with the constriction factor approach for guaranteed convergence).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $x = 5$, $v = 2$, $p_{best} = 3$, $g_{best} = 1$, $w = 0.5$, $c_1 = 2$, $c_2 = 2$, $r_1 = 0.4$, $r_2 = 0.6$.

**Step 1: Compute new velocity $v^{(t+1)}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
v^{(t+1)} &= w \cdot v^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x^{(t)}) \\
&= (0.5)(2) + (2)(0.4)(3 - 5) + (2)(0.6)(1 - 5) \\
&= 1.0 + (0.8)(-2) + (1.2)(-4) \\
&= 1.0 - 1.6 - 4.8 \\
&= -5.4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute new position $x^{(t+1)}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
x^{(t+1)} &= x^{(t)} + v^{(t+1)} \\
&= 5 + (-5.4) \\
&= -0.4
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The new velocity is $v = -5.4$ and the new position is $x = -0.4$. The particle has overshot the global best ($g_{best} = 1$) and the personal best ($p_{best} = 3$), moving rapidly into the negative region. This is normal early-iteration behavior as PSO explores aggressively.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Compare Genetic Algorithm (GA) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) in terms of their origin, operators, parameters, and convergence behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Constriction Factor approach in PSO. How does it ensure convergence, and what are its typical values? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Feature | Genetic Algorithm (GA) | Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) |
|---------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Origin** | Holland (1975); inspired by Darwinian evolution and natural selection | Kennedy & Eberhart (1995); inspired by bird flocking |
| **Population Representation** | Chromosomes (binary, real, etc.) | Particles with position and velocity |
| **Selection Operator** | Roulette wheel, tournament, rank-based | No explicit selection — implicit via $g_{best}$ |
| **Variation Operators** | Crossover (recombination) and mutation | Velocity and position update equations |
| **Number of Parameters** | Many: population size, crossover rate, mutation rate, selection pressure | Few: $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$, $N$ |
| **Memory** | None (history is lost) | Yes — $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$ retain best solutions |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slower (requires generations of crossover) | Faster (direct gradient-like information sharing) |
| **Premature Convergence** | Susceptible | Less susceptible (randomness in $r_1, r_2$) |
| **Constraint Handling** | Penalty functions, special operators | Penalty functions, bounds on $v_{max}$ |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Moderate to high | Very low (two equations) |

**Summary:** PSO is simpler, has fewer parameters, retains memory of best solutions, and generally converges faster than GA. However, GA offers more flexibility for combinatorial problems, whereas PSO excels in continuous optimization.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The Constriction Factor Approach (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002):**

The original PSO suffered from **velocity explosion** — particles could fly out of the search space because the velocity update could produce increasingly large values. Clerc and Kennedy introduced a **constriction factor** $\chi$ that mathematically guarantees convergence.

**Modified Velocity Update Equation:**

$$v_{id}^{(t+1)} = \chi \left[ v_{id}^{(t)} + c_1 r_1 (p_{best,id} - x_{id}^{(t)}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best,d} - x_{id}^{(t)}) \right]$$

**Derivation of $\chi$:**

The constriction factor is given by:
$$\chi = \frac{2}{\left\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \right\vert}$$
where $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$.

**How it ensures convergence:** The factor $\chi$ (always $< 1$) acts as a **velocity dampener**. For the canonical choice $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ (so $\phi = 4.1$):
$$\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - 4.1 - \sqrt{16.81 - 16.4} \vert} = \frac{2}{2.741} \approx 0.729$$

**Typical Values:**
- $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05 \Rightarrow \chi \approx 0.7298$ (most widely used "magic" setting).
- Equivalently, the inertia weight form uses $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.4962$.

**Advantage:** Eliminates the need for explicit $v_{max}$ clamping; convergence is mathematically guaranteed for any function $f$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting random numbers $r_1$ and $r_2$** in the velocity equation — these are stochastic components and must be present.
> 2. **Confusing $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$** — $p_{best}$ is per-particle; $g_{best}$ is global.
> 3. **Mixing up the dimensions** — $p_{best,id}$ and $g_{best,d}$ refer to the $d$-th component of vectors.
> 4. **Not showing position update** — both velocity AND position must be updated. Half-mark loss otherwise.
> 5. **Forgetting to clamp velocity** — $v_{id}$ must lie in $[-v_{max}, +v_{max}]$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **PSO = Kennedy & Eberhart, 1995**, inspired by **bird flocking / fish schooling**.
- ✅ **Two equations only:** velocity update + position update. **MEMORIZE BOTH.**
- ✅ **Three components of velocity:** Inertia ($w v$), Cognitive ($c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x)$), Social ($c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x)$).
- ✅ **Inertia weight $w$** controls exploration vs. exploitation; common range $0.4$ to $0.9$.
- ✅ **Standard parameters:** $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.5$, $N = 20\text{–}50$.
- ✅ **Linearly decreasing $w$** from $0.9$ to $0.4$ is a widely used variant.
- ✅ **Constriction factor** $\chi \approx 0.7298$ with $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ guarantees convergence (Clerc & Kennedy).
- ✅ **$p_{best}$** = particle's own best-known position (cognitive memory).
- ✅ **$g_{best}$** = swarm's best-known position (social knowledge).
- ✅ **Random numbers** $r_1, r_2 \in [0, 1]$ introduce stochasticity and prevent premature convergence.
- ✅ **PSO is derivative-free** — needs only fitness function evaluation.
- ✅ **Velocity clamping** is essential to prevent explosion of the search.
- ✅ **Convergence conditions** (Jury's test): $0 < w < 1$ and $0 < \phi_1 + \phi_2 < 2(1 + w)$.
- ✅ **Applications:** neural network training, economic load dispatch, antenna design, image segmentation, PID tuning.
- ✅ **GA vs. PSO:** PSO has fewer parameters, retains memory, and converges faster on continuous problems.
- ✅ **PSO variants to know:** Inertia Weight PSO, Constriction Factor PSO, Linearly Decreasing Weight PSO, Bare Bones PSO, Quantum PSO, Comprehensive Learning PSO.
- ✅ **Termination criteria:** maximum iterations $T_{max}$ reached OR $\vert g_{best}^{(t+1)} - g_{best}^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ OR swarm velocity $\to 0$.
- ✅ **Time-varying acceleration coefficients (TVAC):** $c_1$ decreases from 2.5 to 0.5 while $c_2$ increases from 0.5 to 2.5 over iterations (encourages early exploration, late exploitation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ant colony optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT413 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ant colony optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a population-based, nature-inspired metaheuristic algorithm belonging to the family of **Swarm Intelligence** techniques, introduced by **Marco Dorigo** in his doctoral thesis (early 1990s) and formally published in 1996/1997. It is designed to solve discrete combinatorial optimization problems (COPs) by simulating the foraging behaviour of real ant colonies.

In the KTU 2024 PERAT413 framework, ACO is classified under **stochastic, constructive, graph-based metaheuristics** that iteratively build candidate solutions using a **stochastic local search** guided by two competing forces: **artificial pheromone trails** (representing learned global knowledge of good solutions) and **heuristic information** (representing problem-specific greedy desirability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ACO is a **constructive** metaheuristic (solutions are built component-by-component) as opposed to local search metaheuristics (SA, TS) that improve complete solutions. It uses **positive feedback** (pheromone reinforcement) and **negative feedback** (pheromone evaporation) — a critical exam distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a colony of ants searching for the shortest path from their nest to a food source. Initially, each ant wanders randomly, leaving a chemical substance called a **pheromone** on the ground. When an ant reaches a fork in the path, it chooses one branch with a probability proportional to the **concentration of pheromone** already deposited on that branch.

The key biological phenomenon is called **stigmergy** — indirect communication through environmental modification. Ants that happen to take the shorter path complete the round-trip faster, deposit pheromone sooner, and the trail becomes reinforced. Over time, the shorter path accumulates more pheromone, attracting even more ants, while longer paths lose pheromone due to **evaporation**. Eventually, almost all ants converge on the optimal (shortest) path.

**Engineering Translation:** Replace "ants" with "computational agents," "path" with a "candidate solution in a search graph," and "pheromone" with a "numerical weight stored on edges of the graph."

> [!NOTE]
> Real ants are essentially **blind** — they cannot see the global layout. They solve the shortest-path problem using only local information (pheromone intensity on adjacent edges). This remarkable decentralised intelligence is what makes ACO a powerful optimisation paradigm.

## 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

- **Stigmergy:** Indirect coordination between agents via environmental traces.
- **Pheromone (τ_ij):** Numeric value stored on edge $(i, j)$ representing learned desirability of traversing that edge.
- **Heuristic information (η_ij):** Problem-specific a priori information, typically $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ for shortest path problems.
- **Evaporation (ρ):** The pheromone decay coefficient where $0 < \rho \le 1$ (ρ = 0.5 is standard).
- **Pheromone influence exponent (α):** Controls the relative weight of accumulated pheromone vs. heuristic information.
- **Heuristic influence exponent (β):** Controls the relative weight of greedy information vs. accumulated pheromone.

> [!WARNING]
> Students frequently confuse **α and β**. Remember: **α amplifies the past experience (pheromone)**, while **β amplifies the greedy shortcut (heuristic)**. Setting $\beta = 0$ reduces ACO to a pure stochastic search biased only by history; setting $\alpha = 0$ reduces it to a pure greedy heuristic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Architecture — The Big Picture

The ACO algorithm executes a tight feedback loop comprising four logical phases per iteration:

1. **Solution Construction Phase:** Each of $m$ artificial ants incrementally builds a solution by moving through a fully connected **construction graph** $G = (C, L)$, where $C$ is the set of components (nodes) and $L$ is the set of possible connections (edges).
2. **Optional Local Search Phase:** A local optimiser may refine the constructed solutions (e.g., 2-opt for TSP). This is an *enhancement* to vanilla ACO and not strictly required.
3. **Pheromone Update Phase:** After all ants have constructed solutions, the pheromone trails are **evaporated** globally and then **reinforced** on edges that belonged to the best-so-far solutions.
4. **Termination Check:** If the iteration count reaches $NC_{max}$ or a stagnation condition is met, output the best solution; otherwise, return to Phase 1.

> [!NOTE]
> The canonical text by **Dorigo & Stützle (2004), "Ant Colony Optimization," MIT Press** is the foundational reference. KTU examiners expect familiarity with the **Ant System (AS)**, **Ant Colony System (ACS)**, and **Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)** variants.

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works

### Why Solution Construction is Probabilistic
- **Why:** Pure greedy construction (always pick shortest edge) gets stuck in local optima. Pure random construction wastes computational effort.
- **How:** A **roulette-wheel (proportional random) selection** balances exploitation (current best) and exploration (less-tried edges).

### Why Pheromone Evaporation Exists
- **Why:** Without evaporation, early suboptimal trails (found by chance) would persist forever, biasing all future searches. Evaporation is the algorithm's **forgetting mechanism**.
- **How:** Every pheromone value is multiplied by $(1 - \rho)$ at the end of each iteration, ensuring outdated information decays.

### Why Pheromone Deposit is Selective
- **Why:** Depositing on *all* traversed edges dilutes the signal. Reinforcing only the best tours focuses search on promising regions of the solution space.
- **How:** Either the **iteration-best** or the **global-best** (or both) ants deposit pheromone, while all others do not.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Bounds |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $P^k_{ij}(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}^k_i} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ | **Stochastic transition probability** that ant $k$ at node $i$ moves to node $j$ at iteration $t$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| 2 | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | **Heuristic desirability** (inverse distance) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}$ | **Global pheromone update rule** | $\rho \in (0, 1]$ |
| 4 | $\Delta\tau_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau^{k}_{ij}$ | **Total pheromone added to edge $(i, j)$** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta\tau^{k}_{ij} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ uses edge } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | **Ant System (AS) deposit** (Q is constant, $L_k$ is tour length) | $Q$ typically = tour length of nearest neighbour |
| 6 | $\Delta\tau^{bs}_{ij} = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \rho \cdot \dfrac{1}{L_{bs}}$ | **Best-so-far update** for MMAS | $L_{bs}$ = best-so-far tour |
| 7 | $\tau_{ij} \in [\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ | **Pheromone bounds in MMAS** | Prevents stagnation |
| 8 | $j = \arg\max_{l \in \mathcal{N}^k_i} \{[\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}\}$ with prob. $q_0$; else eq. (1) | **Pseudo-random proportional rule of ACS** | $q_0 \in [0,1]$ |
| 9 | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \xi)\tau_{ij} + \xi \cdot \tau_0$ | **ACS local pheromone update** (online deferred) | $\xi \in (0,1]$ |
| 10 | $m \approx n$ | Recommended number of ants ≈ number of nodes $n$ | Empirical guideline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Tip:** Formula (1) is the *signature equation* of ACO. Memorise its numerator (product of powers) and denominator (sum over all allowed neighbours $\mathcal{N}^k_i$). Formula (3) is the **canonical update rule** — every KTU question on ACO reduction will feature it.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ACO is widely deployed for solving NP-hard discrete optimisation problems that classical exact methods (branch-and-bound, dynamic programming) cannot handle at scale:

- **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP):** The benchmark problem. ACO finds near-optimal tours for thousands of cities.
- **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP):** Logistics and supply-chain optimisation — FedEx, UPS, Amazon routing.
- **Job-Shop / Flow-Shop Scheduling:** Manufacturing process optimisation.
- **Telecommunications Network Routing:** Adaptive routing in packet-switched networks (AntNet).
- **Robotics & Swarm Robotics:** Multi-robot path planning in cluttered environments.
- **Structural Design Optimisation:** Truss topology, beam cross-section sizing.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein folding, DNA sequencing assembly.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Transition Probability (Equation 1)

The transition probability in ACO is derived from a **biased stochastic sampling** perspective. We begin with the unnormalised attractiveness $A_{ij}$ of moving from $i$ to $j$:

$$
A_{ij}(t) = [\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}
$$

**Step 1 — Interpret the role of each factor:**

$$
[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha}: \text{Weight of historical desirability (exploitation).}
$$

$$
[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}: \text{Weight of problem-specific greedy desirability (exploration).}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that probabilities must sum to unity over the feasible neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}^k_i$ of ant $k$ at node $i$:**

$$
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}^k_i} P^k_{ij}(t) = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Normalise $A_{ij}$ by dividing by the sum of all attractivenesses in the neighbourhood:**

$$
P^k_{ij}(t) = \frac{A_{ij}(t)}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}^k_i} A_{il}(t)} = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}^k_i}[\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the **roulette-wheel mechanism** borrowed from evolutionary algorithms. It guarantees that **high-pheromone, short-distance edges** have a high probability of selection while still allowing less-attractive edges to be sampled occasionally — preventing premature convergence.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pheromone Update Rule (Equation 3)

We start from the first principles of **pheromone dynamics** in real ant colonies. Real pheromone obeys a **first-order decay model** (exponential evaporation):

$$
\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{dt} = -\rho \cdot \tau_{ij}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve the continuous decay over a single time-step $\Delta t = 1$:**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) \cdot e^{-\rho} \approx \tau_{ij}(t)(1 - \rho) \quad \text{(for small } \rho\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Add the deposit term. Ants traversing edge $(i, j)$ contribute $\Delta\tau^{k}_{ij}$:**

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau^{k}_{ij}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the individual ant's contribution. In the standard Ant System:**

$$
\Delta\tau^{k}_{ij} = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ traverses } (i,j) \text{ in its tour} \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $Q/L_k$ ensures that **shorter tours deposit more pheromone** (inverse relationship with length). The factor $Q$ is a constant controlling total deposit scale.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Transition Probability

Consider an ant at node $i$ with three allowed neighbours: $j_1, j_2, j_3$. Given:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{i,j_1} = 2.0, \quad \eta_{i,j_1} &= 1/4 = 0.250 \\
\tau_{i,j_2} = 3.0, \quad \eta_{i,j_2} &= 1/2 = 0.500 \\
\tau_{i,j_3} = 1.0, \quad \eta_{i,j_3} &= 1/5 = 0.200 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\alpha = 1, \quad \beta = 2
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the unnormalised attractiveness $A_{i,j_x}$:**

$$
A_{i,j_1} = (2.0)^1 \cdot (0.250)^2 = 2.0 \cdot 0.0625 = 0.125
$$

$$
A_{i,j_2} = (3.0)^1 \cdot (0.500)^2 = 3.0 \cdot 0.25 = 0.75
$$

$$
A_{i,j_3} = (1.0)^1 \cdot (0.200)^2 = 1.0 \cdot 0.04 = 0.04
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the denominators:**

$$
\sum = 0.125 + 0.75 + 0.04 = 0.915
$$

**Step 3 — Normalise to obtain the transition probabilities:**

$$
P_{i,j_1} = \frac{0.125}{0.915} \approx 0.1366
$$

$$
P_{i,j_2} = \frac{0.75}{0.915} \approx 0.8197
$$

$$
P_{i,j_3} = \frac{0.04}{0.915} \approx 0.0437
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the sum:**

$$
P_{i,j_1} + P_{i,j_2} + P_{i,j_3} = 0.1366 + 0.8197 + 0.0437 = 1.000 \checkmark
$$

**Conversion logic:** Even though $j_1$ has more pheromone than $j_3$, $j_2$ is chosen with overwhelming probability because its heuristic value ($0.5$) is double that of $j_1$ and amplified by $\beta = 2$. The squared distance penalty strongly favours the geometrically closer node.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithmic Parameters and Their Effects

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect of Increase |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------------------|
| Number of ants | $m$ | $n$ to $10n$ | Better coverage, more compute per iteration |
| Pheromone exponent | $\alpha$ | $0$ to $5$ | More exploitation, faster but riskier convergence |
| Heuristic exponent | $\beta$ | $0$ to $10$ | Greedier behaviour, may lock onto local optima |
| Evaporation rate | $\rho$ | $0.01$ to $0.5$ | Faster forgetting, more exploration |
| Pheromone initial value | $\tau_0$ | $m/L_{nn}$ | Higher initial diversity |
| Pseudo-random parameter | $q_0$ (ACS) | $0$ to $1$ | Higher = more exploitation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ACO Master Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize pheromone tau_ij = tau_0] --> B[Place m ants on random start nodes]
    B --> C{Iteration t = 1 to NC_max}
    C --> D[For each ant k: Build solution by transition rule]
    D --> E[Optional: Local search refinement]
    E --> F[Evaluate all m solutions: compute L_k]
    F --> G[Identify best tour L_best]
    G --> H[Apply pheromone evaporation: tau_ij = 1-rho times tau_ij]
    H --> I[Apply pheromone deposit on best-tour edges]
    I --> J{Termination criterion met?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Output global best solution]
    K --> L[End]
```

## 4.2 Ant Decision-Making at a Node

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Ant k at node i] --> N2[Identify feasible neighbours N_k_i]
    N2 --> N3[Compute A_il for each neighbour l]
    N3 --> N4[For l in N_k_i: A_il = tau_il^alpha times eta_il^beta]
    N4 --> N5[Sum of all A_il values]
    N5 --> N6[Compute P_il = A_il divided by Sum]
    N6 --> N7[Roulette wheel selection of next node j]
    N7 --> N8[Move ant k to node j]
    N8 --> N9[Update tabu list visited nodes]
```

## 4.3 ACO Variants Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACO[Ant Colony Optimization Family] --> AS[Ant System AS - 1991]
    ACO --> EAS[Elitist AS]
    ACO --> ACS[Ant Colony System ACS - 1997]
    ACO --> MMAS[Max-Min AS MMAS - 2000]
    AS --> A1[Use all ants for deposit]
    EAS --> A2[Extra deposit on global best edges]
    ACS --> A3[Pseudo random proportional rule]
    ACS --> A4[Local pheromone update during construction]
    MMAS --> A5[Pheromone bounded between tau_min and tau_max]
    MMAS --> A6[Only best ant iteration or best so far deposits]
```

## 4.4 Pheromone Update Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Edge i-j has tau_ij at time t] --> P2[Evaporation: tau_ij new = 1-rho times tau_ij old]
    P2 --> P3[Best ants deposit Q over L_k on their traversed edges]
    P3 --> P4[Updated tau_ij at time t+1]
    P4 --> P5[Used in transition probability for next iteration]
    P5 --> P6[Influences ant movement decisions]
    P6 --> P7[New solutions are constructed]
    P7 --> P8[Best of new solutions update pheromone]
    P8 --> P1
```

## 4.5 Application Mapping: ACO for TSP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Input: List of n cities and distance matrix d_ij] --> T2[Initialize tau_ij = tau_0 for all i-j]
    T2 --> T3[Set m ants on n random cities]
    T3 --> T4[Each ant builds tour visiting each city once]
    T4 --> T5[Compute tour length L_k for each ant]
    T5 --> T6[Update pheromone: evaporate then deposit]
    T6 --> T7{Iter < NC_max?}
    T7 -- Yes --> T4
    T7 -- No --> T8[Return shortest tour found]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Ant Colony Optimization. Explain the biological inspiration behind it in terms of stigmergy and pheromone-based communication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) is a population-based metaheuristic proposed by Marco Dorigo (1996) that solves combinatorial optimization problems by simulating the foraging behaviour of real ant colonies. **Biological inspiration:** Real ants, although individually blind, collectively find shortest paths between nest and food source using **stigmergy** — an indirect form of communication mediated by depositing a chemical substance called **pheromone** on the traversed path. Ants probabilistically prefer paths with higher pheromone concentration; shorter paths are reinforced faster (more pheromone per unit time), creating a positive feedback loop. In the artificial model, pheromone is replaced by a numerical weight $\tau_{ij}$ stored on graph edges, and ants become computational agents that construct candidate solutions. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for stigmergy, 1 for pheromone-feedback logic)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the role of pheromone evaporation coefficient $\rho$ and explain what happens if $\rho$ is set to 0 or 1.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pheromone evaporation coefficient $\rho \in (0, 1]$ controls the rate at which old pheromone information is **forgotten** between iterations. If $\rho = 0$, **no evaporation occurs**; consequently, pheromone from the very first iteration persists forever, causing the algorithm to lose exploratory capability and prematurely converge on the first (possibly suboptimal) solution. If $\rho = 1$, **complete evaporation** occurs each iteration, meaning pheromone carries no information across iterations — the algorithm reduces to an independent random search at every step, with no learning. The optimal balance is empirically around $\rho = 0.1$ to $0.5$, which preserves useful history while allowing newer, better solutions to dominate. **(3 marks: 1 for role, 1 each for the two extreme cases)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a clearly labelled block diagram, describe the **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) algorithm** in detail. Discuss each of the four major phases: solution construction, optional local search, pheromone update, and termination.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) algorithm is a metaheuristic for solving combinatorial optimization problems. It uses a colony of artificial ants that build solutions by traversing a construction graph, guided by pheromone trails $\tau_{ij}$ and heuristic information $\eta_{ij}$.

**Phase 1: Solution Construction (2 marks):**
Each ant $k$ starts at a randomly chosen node. At each step, it probabilistically selects the next node $j$ from the feasible neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}^k_i$ using the transition rule:

$$
P^k_{ij}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}^k_i} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}
$$

The ant maintains a memory (tabu list) of visited nodes to prevent revisits. The parameter $\alpha$ controls the influence of accumulated pheromone (exploitation), while $\beta$ controls the influence of greedy heuristic (exploration).

**Phase 2: Optional Local Search (1 mark):**
After construction, each ant's solution may be improved using a local search operator such as 2-opt (for TSP) or 3-opt. This is optional and not part of the original Ant System but is included in advanced variants (e.g., ACS with local search).

**Phase 3: Pheromone Update (2 marks):**
Pheromone is updated in two steps. First, **evaporation** is applied to all edges:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)
$$

Then, **deposit** is added on edges that belong to the best tour (iteration-best or global-best):

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k \in \text{best ants}} \frac{Q}{L_k}
$$

The evaporation prevents stagnation; the deposit reinforces good solutions.

**Phase 4: Termination (1 mark):**
The algorithm terminates when either a maximum number of iterations $NC_{max}$ is reached, a pre-defined solution quality is achieved, or stagnation is detected (no improvement in the best solution for a number of iterations).

**Block Diagram Reference:** [Refer to Section 4.1]

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a TSP with **4 cities** A, B, C, D with the following distance matrix:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & A & B & C & D \\
\hline
A & 0 & 2 & 7 & 5 \\
B & 2 & 0 & 4 & 8 \\
C & 7 & 4 & 0 & 3 \\
D & 5 & 8 & 3 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

Three ants start from cities A, B, C respectively with $\tau_{ij}(0) = 1$ for all edges, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$. Use $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$. Compute the transition probabilities from city **A** for ant 1 and determine the next city visited.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute heuristic values for edges from A:

$$
\eta_{AB} = 1/2 = 0.500, \quad \eta_{AC} = 1/7 \approx 0.143, \quad \eta_{AD} = 1/5 = 0.200
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute attractiveness $A_{ij} = \tau_{ij}^{\alpha} \cdot \eta_{ij}^{\beta}$ for each neighbour of A. With $\tau = 1$ and $\beta = 2$:

$$
A_{AB} = (1)^1 \cdot (0.500)^2 = 0.250
$$

$$
A_{AC} = (1)^1 \cdot (0.143)^2 = 0.0204
$$

$$
A_{AD} = (1)^1 \cdot (0.200)^2 = 0.040
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the sum:

$$
\sum A = 0.250 + 0.0204 + 0.040 = 0.3104
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute transition probabilities:

$$
P_{AB} = \frac{0.250}{0.3104} = 0.8054
$$

$$
P_{AC} = \frac{0.0204}{0.3104} = 0.0657
$$

$$
P_{AD} = \frac{0.040}{0.3104} = 0.1289
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Verification: $0.8054 + 0.0657 + 0.1289 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion: Ant 1 at city A will visit **city B next** with probability 0.8054, which is the highest. This is expected since AB is the shortest edge from A (distance 2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **pheromone update equation** of the Ant System from first principles. Clearly distinguish between evaporation and deposit. Show how the choice of $\Delta\tau^k_{ij}$ influences the algorithm's behaviour.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — First-Order Decay Model (2 marks):**
Real pheromone decay obeys the differential equation:

$$
\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{dt} = -\rho \cdot \tau_{ij}
$$

Discretising over a single iteration ($\Delta t = 1$) and using the small-$\rho$ approximation $e^{-\rho} \approx 1 - \rho$:

$$
\tau_{ij}^{\text{after evap}}(t) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Adding the Deposit (2 marks):**
After evaporation, ants that have traversed edge $(i, j)$ in their tours deposit additional pheromone. The total deposit is the sum across all $m$ ants:

$$
\Delta\tau_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau^{k}_{ij}
$$

The complete update rule is therefore:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau^{k}_{ij}
$$

**Step 3 — Defining $\Delta\tau^{k}_{ij}$ (2 marks):**
In the canonical Ant System:

$$
\Delta\tau^{k}_{ij} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ traverses } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $L_k$ is the total tour length of ant $k$ and $Q$ is a constant. **Behavioural influence:** Shorter tours deposit more pheromone per edge (since $1/L_k$ is larger), which reinforces high-quality solutions. Using $Q$ in the numerator (rather than setting $Q = 1$) gives a scale-invariant deposit.

**Step 4 — Variants (1 mark):**
In **Ant Colony System (ACS)**, the deposit is restricted to the **global best** tour only, accelerating convergence. In **Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)**, deposit is by either the iteration-best or best-so-far tour, with pheromone bounded by $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ to prevent stagnation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **Ant System (AS)**, **Ant Colony System (ACS)**, and **Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)** in a tabular form with respect to at least six distinguishing features. Briefly state the advantages of MMAS over AS.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | Ant System (AS) | Ant Colony System (ACS) | Max-Min Ant System (MMAS) |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| Year proposed | 1991 | 1997 | 2000 |
| Transition rule | Proportional random (eq. 1) | Pseudo-random proportional (eq. 8) | Proportional random (eq. 1) |
| Pheromone deposit | All $m$ ants | Only global best (or iteration best) | Iteration-best or best-so-far only |
| Local update | None | Yes, during construction (eq. 9) | None |
| Pheromone bounds | None | None | $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ enforced |
| Initial pheromone | $Q / (n \cdot L_{nn})$ | $\tau_0$ uniform | $\tau_{max}$ |
| Re-initialisation | No | No | Yes, on stagnation |
| Convergence speed | Slow | Fast | Moderate-fast |
| Stagnation resistance | Weak | Moderate | Strong |

**Advantages of MMAS over AS (2 marks):**
1. **Pheromone bounds $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$** prevent any single edge from being either completely ignored ($\tau \to 0$) or overwhelmingly dominant ($\tau \to \infty$), ensuring continued exploration throughout the run.
2. **Restricted deposit (only best-so-far or iteration-best)** sharpens the search focus on high-quality regions, leading to faster convergence to better solutions.
3. **Re-initialisation on stagnation** allows the algorithm to escape prolonged local optima traps — a feature absent in vanilla AS.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in ACO Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ exponents in the transition rule** — students often write $[\tau_{ij}][\eta_{ij}]$ instead of $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$. This costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Not normalising the denominator** — the transition probability is a *ratio*, not just the numerator. If the denominator is missing, **0 marks** for that step.
> 3. **Confusing pheromone *evaporation* and *deposit*** — evaporation *reduces* $\tau_{ij}$, deposit *increases* $\tau_{ij}$. The two are applied in sequence: first evaporate, then deposit. Writing the wrong order loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing $1 - \rho$ as the *total* pheromone update** — students forget the additive deposit term $\sum \Delta\tau^k_{ij}$. Always show both terms.
> 5. **In numerical problems, not showing the tabu list update** — when an ant visits a city, it must be removed from the feasible neighbourhood for the next step. Omitting this costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Setting $\alpha = \beta = 1$ without justification** — these are parameters that must be specified, not defaults.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ACO is a constructive, population-based, stochastic metaheuristic** inspired by ant foraging behaviour and the phenomenon of **stigmergy**.
- ✅ The **transition probability** $P^k_{ij}$ is the central decision-making rule, combining $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}$ (history) and $[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$ (heuristic), normalised over the feasible neighbourhood.
- ✅ The **pheromone update rule** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum \Delta\tau^k_{ij}$ performs **evaporation (forgetting)** and **deposit (learning)** in sequence.
- ✅ **$\alpha$** controls exploitation of historical pheromone; **$\beta$** controls exploitation of greedy heuristic; **$\rho$** is the evaporation rate.
- ✅ Three main variants: **AS** (basic, all ants deposit), **ACS** (pseudo-random rule + local update + global-best deposit), **MMAS** (pheromone bounds + best-only deposit + re-initialisation).
- ✅ ACO is best suited for **discrete combinatorial problems** — TSP, VRP, scheduling, network routing.
- ✅ Compared to GA, ACO uses **problem-specific constructive moves** rather than recombination; compared to PSO, ACO works on **discrete graphs** rather than continuous space.
- ✅ **Limitations:** slow convergence on large instances, many parameters to tune, possible stagnation if not bounded.
- ✅ **Initial pheromone** in AS is typically $\tau_0 = Q / (n \cdot L_{nn})$ where $L_{nn}$ is the nearest-neighbour tour length.
- ✅ **Number of ants** is empirically chosen as $m \approx n$ for TSP-class problems.
- ✅ **Termination:** fixed iteration count, time budget, target solution quality, or stagnation detection.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineering applications of unconstrained and constrained optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT413 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Engineering applications of unconstrained and constrained optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Engineering Application of Optimization"?

In the context of **PERAT413 – Engineering Optimization (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**, *engineering applications of optimization* refers to the practical use of the calculus-based and numerical techniques studied in the previous modules to solve **real design and operating problems** faced by engineers in mechanical, civil, electrical, and industrial domains.

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> An *engineering application of optimization* is the formulation of a physical design or operating problem into a mathematical program — i.e., a single **objective function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ to be minimized (cost, weight, loss) or maximized (efficiency, profit), subject to a set of **design variables** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, and optionally a set of **equality constraints** $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and **inequality constraints** $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$.

Two broad families exist:

| Type | Mathematical Form | Typical Engineering Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Unconstrained** | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ over $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Shape of a tensioned cable, optimal thickness of a column |
| **Constrained** | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_k(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Pressure vessel, two-bar truss, spring, welded beam |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 4, students are expected to be able to **formulate**, **solve** (using Lagrange multipliers or Kuhn–Tucker conditions), and **interpret** classical engineering problems. Numerical search algorithms (search methods, penalty functions) are covered in earlier modules; Module 4 focuses on the **closed-form application**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Carpenter vs. The Architect"

Imagine you are building a wooden chair:
- An **unconstrained** optimization problem is like a carpenter choosing the *thickness* of a leg to minimize wood used. No rules — just pick the lightest shape that doesn't fail.
- A **constrained** optimization problem is like an architect designing the same chair but **must** support a 120 kg load, **must** fit through a 60 cm door, and **must** cost less than ₹800. Now the carpenter has boundaries (constraints) defining what is acceptable.

In engineering, almost every real problem is **constrained** because of safety codes, manufacturing limits, cost ceilings, and physical laws. Unconstrained problems, however, are useful as *idealized starting points* and are simpler to solve with calculus ($\nabla f = 0$).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in B.Tech

Most KTU exam questions from this module test a student's ability to:
1. Read an engineering word problem.
2. Identify the **objective** (minimize/maximize), **variables**, and **constraints**.
3. Apply **Lagrange multipliers** (for equality-constrained problems) or **Kuhn–Tucker conditions** (for inequality-constrained problems).
4. Solve the resulting KKT system and present the **optimum point** and **optimum value**.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to know at least **two standard unconstrained** and **two standard constrained** engineering problems thoroughly. The pressure vessel and two-bar truss are by far the most frequently asked.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Mathematical Setup

A general constrained non-linear programming problem is written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f$, $g_i$, and $h_j$ are differentiable real-valued functions.

## 2.2 KKT (Karush–Kuhn–Tucker) Conditions

At a local minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$, there must exist Lagrange multipliers $\lambda_i \ge 0$ (for inequalities) and $\mu_j$ (free sign, for equalities) such that:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) &= 0 \\
\lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) &= 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, m \quad \text{(complementary slackness)} \\
g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) &\le 0, \quad h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0 \\
\lambda_i &\ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For **unconstrained** problems, the condition reduces to $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ (and $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ positive definite for a minimum).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the KKT conditions?** They are the *necessary* first-order optimality conditions for a smooth constrained problem. Without them, students cannot prove a candidate is optimal — they can only guess.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 4)

| # | Topic | Formula / Condition | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unconstrained necessary condition | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | All unconstrained problems |
| 2 | Unconstrained sufficient condition | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$ (positive definite Hessian) | Confirming minimum |
| 3 | Lagrangian function | $\mathcal{L} = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \mu_j h_j$ | Constrained problems |
| 4 | Stationarity (KKT) | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0$ | All constrained problems |
| 5 | Complementary slackness | $\lambda_i g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Inequality constraints |
| 6 | Feasibility | $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0,\ h_j = 0$ | All constrained problems |
| 7 | Dual feasibility | $\lambda_i \ge 0$ (for $\le$ constraints) | Inequality constraints |
| 8 | Pressure vessel — volume | $V = \pi R^2 L + \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | Civil/Mechanical design |
| 9 | Pressure vessel — mass | $W = \rho \, V$ | Pressure vessel |
| 10 | Truss — weight | $W = 2 \rho A L$ (two-bar truss) | Structural design |
| 11 | Truss — length | $L = \dfrac{x_2}{\sin\theta}$, $\sin\theta = \dfrac{x_2}{\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}}$ | Two-bar truss |
| 12 | Truss — stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}}{x_2 \, A}$ | Two-bar truss |
| 13 | Spring — shear stress | $\tau = \dfrac{8 P D K_w}{\pi d^3}$ | Spring design |
| 14 | Spring — deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{8 P D^3 N}{G d^4}$ | Spring design |

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Optimal shape and weight of pressure vessels, springs, gears, and beams.
- **Civil/Structural Engineering:** Minimum-weight trusses, frames, and columns carrying specified loads.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Optimal filter design, transformer core sizing.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Optimal production planning with resource constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> All engineering optimization problems in KTU Module 4 reduce to **solving a small system of algebraic equations**. Mastery of (i) formulating the problem and (ii) solving the KKT system is the difference between a full mark and a partial-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **complete step-by-step solution** of the two most important KTU problems from Module 4:
- **Problem A (Unconstrained):** Minimum-weight design of a **stepped circular column** carrying a compressive load.
- **Problem B (Constrained):** Minimum-volume design of a **tensioned thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel**.

---

## 3.1 Unconstrained Application — Minimum-Weight Stepped Column

### Problem Statement (KTU Standard)
A stepped circular column made of a material of density $\rho$ is to carry a compressive axial load $P$. The column is made of **two uniform sections** of equal length $L$, with diameters $d_1$ (top) and $d_2$ (bottom). Determine $d_1$ and $d_2$ to **minimize the total volume** of material used, given that the stresses in both sections must equal the **allowable stress $\sigma_a$** and the column must be safe against **Euler buckling** in each section.

Assume the column is fixed at the base and free at the top (effective length factor $K = 2$). Modulus of elasticity is $E$.

### Step 1 — Define the Design Variables
$$
\mathbf{x} = (d_1, d_2)
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Objective Function (Volume)
Volume of a circular shaft of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is $\tfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 L$. Hence:

$$
V = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 L + \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 L = \frac{\pi L}{4}\,(d_1^2 + d_2^2)
$$

Minimize $V$ is equivalent to minimizing $f(d_1, d_2) = d_1^2 + d_2^2$ since $\tfrac{\pi L}{4}$ is a positive constant.

### Step 3 — Express the Stress Constraint
Axial stress in each section must equal $\sigma_a$:
$$
\sigma = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2} = \sigma_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad d^2 = \frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_a}
$$

This means **the diameter is fixed by the strength requirement alone** — there is no freedom in $d$ from strength. However, **buckling** provides a second equation.

### Step 4 — Euler Buckling Constraint
For a column fixed at the base and free at the top, the critical buckling load is:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(KL)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{4 L^2}
$$

where $I = \tfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$. Setting $P_{cr} = P$:

$$
P = \frac{\pi^2 E}{4 L^2} \cdot \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi^3 E d^4}{256 L^2}
$$

Solving for $d^4$:

$$
d^4 = \frac{256 P L^2}{\pi^3 E}
$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the Trade-off
- **Strength** requires $d^2 = \tfrac{4P}{\pi \sigma_a}$ (smaller $d$ allowed).
- **Buckling** requires $d^4 = \tfrac{256 P L^2}{\pi^3 E}$ (larger $d$ needed).

The **governing** (larger) of the two is the minimum safe diameter:

$$
d^2 = \max\!\left(\frac{4P}{\pi \sigma_a},\; \sqrt{\frac{256 P L^2}{\pi^3 E}}\right)
$$

For the column to be simultaneously safe, each section is sized by whichever is larger.

### Step 6 — Reduce to an Unconstrained Problem
Let $d^2$ be denoted $u$. Then the volume is:

$$
V = \frac{\pi L}{2} \, u \quad \text{(sum of two equal sections)}
$$

and the binding buckling constraint defines $u$ exactly. Therefore the problem is **fully solved by the buckling equation**:

$$
d_1 = d_2 = \left(\frac{256 P L^2}{\pi^3 E}\right)^{1/4}
$$

### Step 7 — Verification via Calculus
If we treat the buckling as the *only* constraint, $V = \tfrac{\pi L}{2} d^2$ with $d^4 = \tfrac{256 P L^2}{\pi^3 E}$.

By substitution: $d^2 = \sqrt{d^4} = \sqrt{\tfrac{256 P L^2}{\pi^3 E}}$. So

$$
V_{\min} = \frac{\pi L}{2} \sqrt{\frac{256 P L^2}{\pi^3 E}} = \frac{\pi L}{2} \cdot \frac{16 \sqrt{P L^2}}{\pi^{3/2} \sqrt{E}} = \frac{8 L \sqrt{P L^2}}{\pi^{1/2} \sqrt{E}}
$$

$$
V_{\min} = \frac{8 L^{2}}{\sqrt{\pi}} \sqrt{\frac{P}{E}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The optimal column has equal-diameter sections when both carry the same load and have the same length. If the loads were different, the diameters would differ in proportion to $P^{1/4}$ (Euler regime) or $P^{1/2}$ (strength regime).

---

## 3.2 Constrained Application — Tensioned Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

### Problem Statement (KTU Standard)
A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel with hemispherical end caps is closed at both ends. It is to be designed for a working internal pressure $p$, with inner radius $R$, wall thickness $t$, and length of the cylindrical section $L$ (excluding the hemispherical caps). The vessel must satisfy a **minimum volume** criterion, subject to a constraint on the allowable tensile stress $\sigma$ in the cylindrical wall.

### Step 1 — Define Design Variables
Choose $\mathbf{x} = (t, L)$ (assuming $R$ is a *given* design parameter — the inner radius is fixed by storage volume requirements).

### Step 2 — Write the Objective Function (Volume of Material)
For a thin-walled shell, the wall has volume = surface area $\times$ thickness.

- **Cylindrical shell** (lateral area $2\pi R L$): volume $= 2\pi R L t$
- **Two hemispherical end caps** (= one full sphere of radius $R$): surface area $4\pi R^2$, volume $= 4\pi R^2 t$

Total material volume:

$$
V(t, L) = 2\pi R L t + 4\pi R^2 t = 2\pi R t (L + 2R)
$$

We minimize this volume.

### Step 3 — Identify the Stress Constraint
For a thin-walled cylinder under internal pressure $p$, the **hoop (circumferential) stress** is the largest:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{p R}{t}
$$

The allowable-stress constraint is:

$$
g(t) = \frac{p R}{t} - \sigma_a \le 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
t \ge \frac{p R}{\sigma_a}
$$

To **minimize** volume, the optimal choice is to use the **smallest feasible thickness**:

$$
t^* = \frac{p R}{\sigma_a}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back into the Objective
Plug $t^*$ into $V$:

$$
V^* = 2\pi R \cdot \frac{p R}{\sigma_a} \cdot (L + 2R) = \frac{2 \pi p R^2}{\sigma_a}(L + 2R)
$$

But $L$ is still free! We need a **second constraint** to pin it down — usually a **required storage volume** of fluid.

### Step 5 — Add the Storage-Volume Constraint
The required internal volume of the vessel (excluding wall thickness) is:

$$
V_{\text{storage}} = \pi R^2 L + \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \ge V_0
$$

where $V_0$ is the minimum required storage volume. This is an *equality* at optimum (we do not want extra length).

### Step 6 — Formulate as a Constrained NLP

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & V(t, L) = 2\pi R t (L + 2R) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & h_1(t) = \frac{p R}{t} - \sigma_a = 0 \\
& h_2(L) = \pi R^2 L + \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 - V_0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Apply the Method of Lagrange Multipliers
Construct the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(t, L, \mu_1, \mu_2) = 2\pi R t (L + 2R) + \mu_1\!\left(\frac{p R}{t} - \sigma_a\right) + \mu_2\!\left(\pi R^2 L + \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 - V_0\right)
$$

### Step 8 — Take Partial Derivatives = 0
**With respect to $t$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial t} = 2\pi R (L + 2R) - \frac{\mu_1 p R}{t^2} = 0
$$

**With respect to $L$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial L} = 2\pi R t + \mu_2 \pi R^2 = 0
$$

### Step 9 — Solve the System
From the $t$-equation:

$$
\mu_1 = \frac{2\pi R (L + 2R)\, t^2}{p R} = \frac{2\pi t^2 (L + 2R)}{p}
$$

From the $L$-equation:

$$
\mu_2 = -\frac{2\pi R t}{\pi R^2} = -\frac{2t}{R}
$$

The sign of $\mu_2$ is **negative**, which is acceptable for equality constraints (multipliers are unrestricted in sign).

### Step 10 — Apply the Equality Constraints
From $h_1 = 0$:

$$
t^* = \frac{p R}{\sigma_a}
$$

From $h_2 = 0$:

$$
L^* = \frac{V_0 - \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{\pi R^2} = \frac{V_0}{\pi R^2} - \frac{4R}{3}
$$

### Step 11 — Final Optimum Values

$$
t^* = \frac{p R}{\sigma_a}, \qquad L^* = \frac{V_0}{\pi R^2} - \frac{4R}{3}
$$

The minimum material volume is:

$$
V_{\min} = \frac{2\pi p R^2}{\sigma_a} \left( \frac{V_0}{\pi R^2} - \frac{4R}{3} + 2R \right) = \frac{2\pi p R^2}{\sigma_a} \left( \frac{V_0}{\pi R^2} + \frac{2R}{3} \right)
$$

$$
V_{\min} = \frac{2 p V_0}{\sigma_a} + \frac{4\pi p R^3}{3 \sigma_a}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - The **first term** $\tfrac{2 p V_0}{\sigma_a}$ is the *minimum volume* dictated purely by the storage requirement — independent of $R$!
> - The **second term** $\tfrac{4\pi p R^3}{3\sigma_a}$ is the *penalty* for choosing a vessel that is *wide and short*. A small $R$ is favored.

### Step 12 — Optimize Further Over $R$ (Optional Extension)
Setting $\partial V_{\min}/\partial R = 0$ gives a transcendental equation for the optimal $R$. In practice, codes (ASME, IS 2825) fix $R$ for other reasons, and the optimization is performed on $t$ and $L$ alone, as above.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How to Identify Each Component

| Component | Question to Ask | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | "What is being minimized/maximized?" | $f(\mathbf{x}) = $ cost / weight / volume / loss |
| **Variables** | "What can the designer change?" | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots)$ — continuous, positive |
| **Equality constraints** | "What is fixed exactly?" | $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ (e.g., storage volume, load) |
| **Inequality constraints** | "What must not be violated?" | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ (e.g., stress $\le \sigma_a$, deflection $\le \delta_a$) |
| **Active constraints** | "Which constraints bind at the optimum?" | $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ |
| **Inactive constraints** | "Which are slack?" | $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_i = 0$ |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Optimization Workflow (Module 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Word Problem] --> B{Identify Type}
    B -- No constraints --> C[Unconstrained]
    B -- With constraints --> D[Constrained]
    C --> E[Formulate Objective f x]
    D --> F[Formulate f x, g x, h x]
    F --> G[Build Lagrangian L = f + sum lambda g + sum mu h]
    G --> H[Apply KKT Conditions]
    H --> I[Solve Algebraic System]
    E --> J[Set nabla f = 0]
    J --> I
    I --> K[Check Second-Order Conditions]
    K --> L[Optimum x* and f x*]
    L --> M[Engineering Interpretation]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Pressure Vessel Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inner Radius R] --> B[Wall Thickness t]
    B --> C[Hoop Stress sigma_h = pR / t]
    C --> D{sigma_h less than sigma_a?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Feasible]
    D -- No --> F[Increase t]
    F --> B
    E --> G[Cylindrical Section L]
    G --> H[Hemispherical Caps 4 pi R squared / 2 each]
    H --> I[Total Material Volume V = 2 pi R t L + 4 pi R squared t]
    I --> J[Minimize V subject to V_storage = V0]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Classifying the Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> Q1{Is there a stress/load/budget ceiling?}
    Q1 -- No --> U[Unconstrained: Use nabla f = 0]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is constraint equality or inequality?}
    Q2 -- Equality --> L1[Use Lagrange Multipliers]
    Q2 -- Inequality --> L2[Use KKT Conditions]
    L1 --> OUT[Solve algebraic system]
    L2 --> L3[Check complementary slackness]
    L3 --> OUT
    U --> OUT
    OUT --> END[Report x* and f x*]
```

## 4.4 Flowchart — Two-Bar Truss Optimization (KTU Hot Topic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Two-Bar Truss Carries Load P] --> V[Variables: x1 height, x2 half-span]
    V --> W[Objective: Minimize Weight W = 2 rho A L]
    W --> L[Length: L = sqrt x1 squared + x2 squared]
    L --> SIG[Stress: sigma = P L / x2 A]
    SIG --> DEF[Deflection: delta = PL / AE ... ]
    DEF --> SOLVE[Formulate Lagrangian and Solve KKT]
    SOLVE --> RES[Optimal x1, x2 for given load P]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between unconstrained and constrained optimization problems. Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Unconstrained:** A problem in which the design variables can take any real value without violating any restriction. The optimum is found by setting $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.
  *Engineering example:* Minimum cross-sectional area of a tensile bar carrying a fixed axial load — the only requirement is to satisfy the stress limit, which is handled as an equality rather than a constraint.
- **Constrained:** A problem in which the variables must satisfy at least one equality $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ or inequality $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ condition. The KKT conditions must be applied.
  *Engineering example:* Minimum-weight design of a two-bar truss carrying a load $P$, with constraints on stress and deflection.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) necessary conditions for the problem:**
$$
\min f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \ i = 1, \dots, m.
$$

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
At a local minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$ there exist multipliers $\lambda_i \ge 0$ such that:
1. **Stationarity:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$
2. **Primal feasibility:** $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0$ for all $i$
3. **Dual feasibility:** $\lambda_i \ge 0$ for all $i$
4. **Complementary slackness:** $\lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ for all $i$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of inner radius $R = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and length $L = 2 \text{ m}$ has hemispherical end caps. It is to be designed for an internal pressure $p = 2 \text{ MPa}$ with allowable tensile stress $\sigma_a = 100 \text{ MPa}$.**
**(a)** Formulate the problem of **minimizing the volume of material** used as a constrained optimization problem. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Using the method of **Lagrange multipliers**, find the **optimum wall thickness $t^*$** and the **minimum material volume**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Formulation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Design variable:** $t$ (wall thickness). $R$ and $L$ are given.

**Step 2 — Objective (material volume):**
$$
V(t) = \underbrace{2\pi R L t}_{\text{cylindrical shell}} + \underbrace{4\pi R^2 t}_{\text{two hemispherical caps}} = 2\pi t (RL + 2R^2)
$$

Substituting $R = 0.5$, $L = 2$:
$$
V(t) = 2\pi t (0.5 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 0.25) = 2\pi t (1 + 0.5) = 3\pi t \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Stress constraint:** Hoop stress in cylindrical shell:
$$
\sigma_h = \frac{pR}{t} \le \sigma_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(t) = \frac{pR}{t} - \sigma_a \le 0
$$

Substituting values:
$$
g(t) = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5}{t \cdot 10^6} - 100 \cdot 10^6 \;\; \text{(in consistent units, MPa)}
$$

**Final formulation:**
$$
\min \; V(t) = 3\pi t \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \frac{pR}{t} - \sigma_a \le 0, \; t \ge 0
$$

**(b) Solution by Lagrange (7 marks):**

Since the constraint is an *inequality*, we convert it to a Lagrangian with multiplier $\lambda \ge 0$:
$$
\mathcal{L}(t, \lambda) = 3\pi t + \lambda\!\left(\frac{pR}{t} - \sigma_a\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Stationarity w.r.t. $t$:**
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial t} = 3\pi - \frac{\lambda p R}{t^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{3\pi t^2}{pR}
$$

**Step 2 — Complementary slackness:** Since $V$ is increasing in $t$, the optimum will *activate* the constraint (otherwise $t \to 0$):
$$
\frac{pR}{t} - \sigma_a = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t^* = \frac{pR}{\sigma_a} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5}{100} = 0.01 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiplier check:** $\lambda = \tfrac{3\pi (0.01)^2}{2 \cdot 0.5} = \tfrac{3\pi \cdot 10^{-4}}{1} \approx 9.42 \times 10^{-4} \ge 0$ ✓ (dual feasibility satisfied).

**Step 4 — Minimum volume:**
$$
V_{\min} = 3\pi t^* = 3\pi \cdot 0.01 = 0.03\pi \approx 0.0942 \text{ m}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation:**
> - Many students **forget the volume of the hemispherical end caps** and write $V = 2\pi R L t$ only — this loses 2 marks.
> - Some write the constraint as $\ge$ instead of $\le$, giving wrong signs in the Lagrangian.
> - Do not forget to check $\lambda \ge 0$ — this is a 1-mark deduction in the KTU key.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**A two-bar truss consists of two steel bars of density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, pinned at the top and bottom, and carries a vertical load $P = 50 \text{ kN}$ at the joint. Let $x_1$ be the height of the truss and $x_2$ be the half-span. The allowable tensile stress is $\sigma_a = 250 \text{ MPa}$ and allowable joint deflection is $\delta_a = 2 \text{ mm}$.**
**(a)** Formulate the problem of minimizing the **total weight** of the two bars as a constrained optimization problem. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the **optimal dimensions** using KKT conditions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Formulation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Variables:** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)$ with $x_1 > 0$, $x_2 > 0$.

**Step 2 — Length of each bar:**
$$
\ell = \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional area $A$** — to be determined.

**Step 4 — Objective (total weight):**
$$
W(A, x_1, x_2) = 2 \rho A \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Stress constraint:** The force in each bar is $F = \tfrac{P}{2 \sin\theta}$ where $\sin\theta = \tfrac{x_1}{\sqrt{x_1^2+x_2^2}}$. Hence:
$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{P \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}}{2 A x_1} \le \sigma_a
$$

This is the **inequality constraint** $g_1 \le 0$.

**Step 6 — Final formulation:**
$$
\min \; W = 2\rho A \ell, \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_1 = \frac{P \ell}{2 A x_1} - \sigma_a \le 0
$$

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Lagrangian:**
$$
\mathcal{L} = 2\rho A \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2} + \lambda\!\left(\frac{P \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}}{2 A x_1} - \sigma_a\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Active constraint at optimum:** Since the weight is increasing in $A$ and $A$ does not appear in any other constraint, the stress constraint will be active:
$$
A^* = \frac{P \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}}{2 \sigma_a x_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**
$$
W^* = 2 \rho \cdot \frac{P \sqrt{x_1^2+x_2^2}}{2\sigma_a x_1} \cdot \sqrt{x_1^2+x_2^2} = \frac{\rho P (x_1^2 + x_2^2)}{\sigma_a x_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimize over $x_1, x_2$:** Set $\partial W^* / \partial x_2 = 0$ trivially gives $x_2 \to 0$, which is impractical. Hence add a *practical* constraint $x_2 = $ constant (say, $x_2 = 1$ m) or fix one variable and optimize the other.

**Step 5 — With $x_2 = 1$ m fixed:** Minimize
$$
W^*(x_1) = \frac{\rho P (x_1^2 + 1)}{\sigma_a x_1}
$$

Differentiating:
$$
\frac{dW^*}{dx_1} = \frac{\rho P}{\sigma_a} \left(1 - \frac{1}{x_1^2}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1^* = 1 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $A^*$:**
$$
A^* = \frac{50\,000 \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2 \cdot 250 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 1} = \frac{50\,000 \cdot 1.414}{5 \cdot 10^8} = 1.414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \approx 141.4 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 7 — Optimum weight:**
$$
W^* = 2 \cdot 7850 \cdot 1.414 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 3.13 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - The most common error is forgetting the **factor of 2** in the force — students write $F = P$ instead of $F = P/(2\sin\theta)$. Cost: 2 marks.
> - Do not forget to check **dimensional consistency** — $P$ in N, $\sigma$ in Pa, lengths in m. Mixing mm and m is a top reason for losing marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- **Formulation** is worth 50% of the marks: identify the **objective**, **variables**, and **constraints** first; the math is secondary.
- For **unconstrained** problems, the **necessary condition is $\nabla f = 0$**, and the **sufficient condition is $\mathbf{H} \succ 0$** (Hessian positive definite).
- For **constrained** problems, always **build the Lagrangian first**, then take partial derivatives — this avoids missing terms.
- **Complementary slackness** ($\lambda_i g_i = 0$) is the KKT condition that is **most often forgotten** by KTU students.
- The **sign of $\lambda_i$** is $\ge 0$ for $\le$ constraints and $\le 0$ for $\ge$ constraints — a sign error is a 1-mark deduction.
- In the **pressure vessel problem**, the hoop stress $\sigma = pR/t$ is **twice** the longitudinal stress $\sigma = pR/(2t)$ — the hoop stress governs.
- In the **two-bar truss**, the force in each bar is $F = P/(2\sin\theta)$ — the **factor of 2** is essential.
- Always **check the second-order conditions** (Hessian of the Lagrangian bordered matrix) only when the question explicitly asks; for KTU Module 4, **first-order KKT** is usually sufficient.
- **Units consistency**: use SI throughout — lengths in m, stresses in Pa, forces in N.
- Practice the **four classical problems**: (i) pressure vessel, (ii) two-bar truss, (iii) spring design, (iv) stepped column.
- A final sanity check: the optimum should make **engineering sense** — a negative thickness or zero span indicates a formulation error.
- **Past KTU papers (2019–2024)** consistently ask the pressure vessel or the truss problem for 14 marks — prioritize these two.
